text:
A plaintiff brings a claim against a corporation for $4.2 million in compensatory damages arising from an alleged breach of a commercial lease. The defendant argues that the Superior Court of Justice does not have jurisdiction to hear the claim because the amount exceeds some monetary cap.

Which statement correctly describes the monetary jurisdiction of the Ontario Superior Court of Justice?
opts: The Superior Court of Justice has jurisdiction only over claims between $35,000 and $200,000.
The Superior Court of Justice has unlimited monetary jurisdiction and may hear any civil claim regardless of the amount in dispute, pursuant to s.11 of the Courts of Justice Act.
The Superior Court of Justice has a monetary cap of $500,000 for breach of contract claims; larger claims must proceed in the Court of Appeal.
The Superior Court of Justice shares jurisdiction equally with the Divisional Court for claims over $35,000.
expl: Under s.11 of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, the Superior Court of Justice is vested with inherent jurisdiction as a superior court of record. Unlike the Small Claims Court, the Superior Court has no upper monetary limit on the claims it may adjudicate. The defendant's argument has no basis in law; a $4.2 million commercial claim is squarely within the Superior Court's jurisdiction. The Superior Court's inherent jurisdiction also includes the power to grant equitable remedies and orders not expressly granted by statute.
caseRef: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.11
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Court Jurisdiction and Court Hierarchy | Superior Court of Justice — Inherent Jurisdiction and Monetary Threshold

text:
Maria has a claim against her former contractor for $28,000 in deficient renovation work. She is considering whether to bring the claim in Small Claims Court or in the Superior Court of Justice. Her friend tells her she cannot use Small Claims Court because her claim is 'too large.'

Is her friend's advice correct, and what is the monetary limit of the Small Claims Court in Ontario?
opts: Her friend is correct — the Small Claims Court monetary limit is $25,000, so a $28,000 claim must be brought in the Superior Court of Justice.
Her friend is incorrect — the Small Claims Court has no monetary limit and Maria can bring any claim there for efficiency.
Her friend is incorrect — the Small Claims Court monetary limit is $35,000 under s.22 of the Courts of Justice Act, so a $28,000 claim is within the court's jurisdiction.
Her friend is incorrect — the Small Claims Court monetary limit is $50,000, and a $28,000 claim is well within that limit.
expl: Under s.22 of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43 (as amended by O. Reg. 626/00), the monetary jurisdiction of the Small Claims Court is set at $35,000. A claim for $28,000 falls within this limit, so Maria can bring her claim in Small Claims Court. Her friend's advice is incorrect. The Small Claims Court offers a simplified and less expensive procedure designed for self-represented parties and smaller disputes. If Maria's damages exceeded $35,000, she would need to either reduce her claim to $35,000 or proceed in the Superior Court of Justice.
caseRef: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.22; O. Reg. 626/00
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Court Jurisdiction and Court Hierarchy | Small Claims Court — Monetary Limit and Appeals

text:
A party loses a motion in the Superior Court of Justice for an amount of $42,000. The losing party wants to appeal the order. Counsel advises that the appeal must go to the Divisional Court, not the Court of Appeal.

Which statement correctly describes the Divisional Court's jurisdiction over appeals from the Superior Court of Justice?
opts: The Divisional Court has jurisdiction to hear all appeals from the Superior Court of Justice regardless of the amount in dispute.

The Divisional Court has jurisdiction under s.19 of the Courts of Justice Act to hear appeals from the Superior Court where the amount in dispute is under $50,000 (for final orders), as well as judicial review applications from administrative tribunals.
The Divisional Court is a separate court from the Superior Court and therefore can only hear appeals by leave of the Court of Appeal.
The Divisional Court hears only judicial reviews of administrative tribunal decisions; all appeals from the Superior Court go to the Court of Appeal.
expl: Under s.19 of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, the Divisional Court is established as a branch of the Superior Court of Justice. The Divisional Court has jurisdiction to hear appeals from final orders of the Superior Court in proceedings where the amount in dispute does not exceed $50,000, as well as appeals from interlocutory orders with leave. The Divisional Court also has jurisdiction to hear judicial review applications from decisions of administrative tribunals under s.2 of the Judicial Review Procedure Act. The Court of Appeal hears appeals from final orders of the Superior Court where the amount exceeds $50,000.
caseRef: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.19
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Court Jurisdiction and Court Hierarchy | Divisional Court — Jurisdiction and Appeals

text:
A plaintiff issued a statement of claim on February 10, 2026. The defendant delivered a statement of defence on March 12, 2026. The plaintiff has not yet replied to the defence and pleadings are not yet closed. On April 15, 2026, the plaintiff wishes to amend the statement of claim to add an additional defendant and a new cause of action.

Under r.26.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, may the plaintiff amend the statement of claim without court leave at this stage?
opts: No — a plaintiff may only amend a statement of claim with leave of the court once a statement of defence has been delivered.
Yes — under r.26.01, a party may amend a pleading once without leave at any time before the close of pleadings.
No — a plaintiff may never amend a statement of claim to add a new party; a separate action must be commenced.
Yes — but only if all parties consent to the amendment in writing.
expl: Under r.26.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a party may amend a pleading once without leave before the close of pleadings. Pleadings are not yet closed here — the plaintiff has not yet replied to the statement of defence, so the pleadings remain open. The plaintiff may therefore amend the statement of claim once as of right, without obtaining court leave or the consent of other parties. After the close of pleadings, or after the right to amend as of right has been exercised, leave of the court is required under r.26.01.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.26.01
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Pleadings | Amendment of Pleadings — As of Right vs With Leave

text:
The trial in a construction dispute is scheduled for six weeks' time. The plaintiff brings a motion for leave to amend the statement of claim to add a new head of damages that was not originally pleaded, relying on newly obtained expert evidence. The defendant argues that leave should be refused because the trial is imminent and the amendment would cause prejudice.

Under r.26.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what is the governing principle when a court considers whether to grant leave to amend a pleading, and how does prejudice factor into the analysis?
opts: The court must refuse leave to amend once the trial has been scheduled; no amendments are permitted within 90 days of trial.
The court has an absolute discretion to grant or refuse leave to amend and applies no particular principle; each case is decided on its own unique facts.
Under r.26.01, leave to amend shall be granted on such terms as are just unless the amendment would result in prejudice to the other party that could not be compensated by costs.
The court will grant leave to amend only if the moving party demonstrates the proposed amendment raises a new cause of action that could not have been pleaded earlier.
expl: Under r.26.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), on motion at any stage of an action, the court shall grant leave to amend a pleading on such terms as are just unless the amendment would result in prejudice to the other party that could not be compensated by costs. The governing principle favours granting leave to amend; the threshold is whether the opposing party would suffer non-compensable prejudice. Delay and the imminence of trial are factors that may bear on the prejudice analysis, but they do not automatically defeat an amendment motion. If the defendant's prejudice can be addressed by an adjournment and an award of costs thrown away, the amendment will generally be permitted.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.26.01
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Pleadings | Amendment of Pleadings — Leave to Amend After Close of Pleadings

text:
A plaintiff's lawyer issues a statement of claim on March 1, 2026. Due to difficulties locating the defendant, the plaintiff does not serve the claim until August 20, 2026 — which is 172 days after issuance. The defendant moves to have the claim dismissed as having been served out of time.

Is the statement of claim served out of time, and what is the applicable rule?
opts: Yes — r.14.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure requires a statement of claim to be served within 30 days of issuance; 172 days is far too late.
No — there is no deadline for serving a statement of claim after it is issued; the plaintiff can serve it at any time.

Yes — r.14.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure requires a statement of claim to be served within 6 months of the date it was issued; service at 172 days is within this period so the motion should be dismissed.
Yes — the Rules require service within 90 days of issuance; 172 days exceeds this deadline.
expl: Under r.14.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a statement of claim shall be served within 6 months after the date it is issued. Six months from March 1, 2026 is September 1, 2026. Service on August 20, 2026 — which is 172 days after issuance — falls within the 6-month period and is therefore timely. The defendant's motion should be dismissed. If service had not been effected within 6 months, the plaintiff would need to obtain an order renewing the claim under r.14.08(2).
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.14.08
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Pleadings | Statement of Claim — Time to Serve After Issue

text:
The defendant in a fraud action receives the plaintiff's statement of claim but finds the allegations of fraud are stated in general terms without identifying specific representations, dates, or amounts alleged to have been fraudulently misrepresented. The defendant wants more detail before being required to plead.

What procedural step should the defendant take under the Rules of Civil Procedure to obtain more detailed information about the allegations?
opts: The defendant should bring a motion to strike the statement of claim under r.25.11 because the pleading is deficient.
The defendant should demand particulars from the plaintiff under r.25.10; if the plaintiff fails to supply them, the defendant may move for a court order compelling particulars.
The defendant should simply file a statement of defence denying all allegations and seek particulars at the examination for discovery stage.
The defendant should bring a motion for summary judgment immediately on the grounds that the plaintiff's claim has no merit.
expl: Under r.25.10 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a party may demand particulars of another party's pleading. A party who requires clarification of the details of another party's pleading may demand particulars in writing. If the other party fails to supply adequate particulars, the demanding party may bring a motion for a court order compelling production of particulars. Particulars are especially important in fraud allegations, which under r.25.06(8) must be pleaded with full particulars of the circumstances said to constitute fraud.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.25.10; r.25.06(8)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Pleadings | Particulars — Demand and Court Order

text:
A defendant brings a motion under r.21.01(1)(b) of the Rules of Civil Procedure to strike the plaintiff's statement of claim on the grounds that it discloses no reasonable cause of action. The plaintiff's claim is novel and has not been recognised by Ontario courts, but is not clearly without foundation. No evidence is filed on the motion.

What is the test on a r.21.01(1)(b) motion to strike for no reasonable cause of action, and what is the outcome if the claim is novel?
opts: The court will strike the claim unless the plaintiff proves on a balance of probabilities that the cause of action is well-established in Ontario law.

The court applies the test from Hunt v. Carey Canada Inc.: the claim will be struck only if it is plain and obvious, assuming all facts pleaded to be true, that the pleading discloses no reasonable cause of action; a novel but arguable claim should not be struck.
A motion under r.21.01(1)(b) must be supported by evidence; if no evidence is filed, the motion must be dismissed.
The court will strike any claim that has not been previously recognised by Ontario courts because such a claim has no reasonable prospect of success.
expl: Under r.21.01(1)(b) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, the applicable test is from Hunt v. Carey Canada Inc., [1990] 2 SCR 959: the claim will be struck only if it is plain and obvious, assuming the facts as pleaded are true, that the pleading discloses no reasonable cause of action. The court assumes all facts pleaded to be true and gives the claim the most generous reading possible. No evidence is admissible on a r.21.01(1)(b) motion. A novel claim that raises an arguable point of law that has not been authoritatively settled should not be struck, as such claims deserve to be adjudicated at trial.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.21.01(1)(b); Hunt v. Carey Canada Inc., [1990] 2 SCR 959
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Pleadings | Striking Out Pleadings — No Reasonable Cause of Action

text:
A process server is unable to personally serve an individual defendant at any location. The plaintiff's lawyer considers using an alternative to personal service permitted by the Rules of Civil Procedure, without obtaining a court order.

Which of the following is a method of service that may be used as an alternative to personal service on an individual under r.16.03, without a court order?
opts: Leaving a copy of the document taped to the front door of the defendant's residence.
Sending the document by email to the defendant's known email address.
Leaving a copy of the document at the defendant's place of residence with an adult person who is apparently resident there, and mailing another copy to the same address on the same or following day.
Sending the document by fax to the defendant's place of employment.
expl: Under r.16.03(5) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), as an alternative to personal service, a document may be served on an individual by leaving a copy at the individual's place of residence with an adult person who is apparently a resident there, and by sending a copy of the document to the defendant's place of residence by ordinary mail or prepaid courier on the same day or the following day. This two-step process — leaving with an adult resident plus mailing — constitutes valid service without a court order.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.16.03(5)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Service of Process | Alternative to Personal Service — Methods Under r.16.03

text:
A process server is attempting to personally serve a statement of claim on Marcus, an individual defendant. Marcus is not at his home but the process server finds Marcus at his place of work. Marcus refuses to accept the documents but the process server leaves them on Marcus's desk in his presence.

Under r.16.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, has personal service on Marcus been validly effected?
opts: No — personal service on an individual is only valid if the defendant personally accepts the documents from the process server's hands.
Yes — leaving the documents with Marcus in his presence at his place of work constitutes valid personal service under r.16.02, even though Marcus refused to accept them.
No — personal service must be effected at the defendant's residence; service at a place of work is not valid personal service.
No — the process server must first obtain a court order permitting service at the defendant's place of work.
expl: Under r.16.02(1)(a) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), personal service on an individual shall be made by leaving a copy of the document with the individual. Physical acceptance is not required — leaving the document with the person, whether or not they choose to accept it, constitutes valid personal service. A defendant cannot avoid service by refusing to take documents that are placed in their presence. Personal service may be effected at any location where the individual is found, including a place of work.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.16.02(1)(a)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Service of Process | Personal Service on Individuals — Methods Under r.16.02

text:
A plaintiff's process server attempts to personally serve a statement of claim on Takahashi Holdings Inc., an Ontario corporation. The process server attends the corporation's registered office and is met by the office receptionist, who says she is authorized to accept service on behalf of the company. The receptionist accepts the documents and signs an acknowledgment.

Under r.16.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, which of the following methods constitutes valid personal service on a corporation?
opts: Service can only be effected on a corporation by leaving the documents with its registered agent with the court; leaving them with a receptionist is never valid.

Service on a corporation is effected by leaving a copy of the document with an officer, director, or agent of the corporation, or with a person at any place of business of the corporation who appears to be in control or management of the place of business.
Service on a corporation must be effected by registered mail to the corporation's head office; personal service is not available against corporations.
Service on a corporation is only valid if made directly on the CEO or President of the corporation.
expl: Under r.16.02(1)(c) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), personal service on a corporation may be made by leaving a copy of the document with an officer, director, or agent of the corporation, or with a person at any place of business of the corporation who appears to be in control or management of that place of business. A receptionist who accepts documents on behalf of the company and who appears to be in a position of control at that location qualifies. The process server does not need to find the CEO or a specific named officer.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.16.02(1)(c)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Service of Process | Personal Service on Corporations — Methods Under r.16.02

text:
After personally serving a statement of claim on an individual defendant, a process server is asked to prepare the necessary documentation to prove that service occurred. The plaintiff's lawyer explains that this document will be filed with the court.

Under r.16.09 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, how is proof of service of a document established, and what must the proof contain?
opts: Proof of service is established by a written acknowledgment signed by the defendant confirming receipt of the documents.
Proof of service is established by filing an affidavit of service, sworn by the person who effected service, stating the time, date, place, and manner of service and identifying the document served.
Proof of service is established by a certificate signed by the plaintiff's counsel confirming that service was effected.
Proof of service is established by filing the document itself with the court; no separate proof document is required.
expl: Under r.16.09 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), proof of service of a document may be made by an affidavit of the person who effected service. The affidavit must set out: the name of the person served; the date, time, and place of service; and the manner in which service was effected. This sworn document provides the court with the evidentiary basis to confirm that service was properly made. An affidavit of service is filed with the court when default proceedings are pursued or when service is otherwise in issue.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.16.09
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Service of Process | Proof of Service — Affidavit of Service Requirements

text:
A plaintiff has made numerous attempts to personally serve a defendant over a period of two months, including multiple visits to the defendant's last known address, inquiries with the defendant's former employer, and a search of online directories. The defendant cannot be located. The plaintiff wants to serve the defendant by publishing notice in a local newspaper.

What must the plaintiff do under r.16.04 of the Rules of Civil Procedure before using a substituted method of service such as newspaper publication?
opts: The plaintiff may proceed with newspaper publication immediately after two failed personal service attempts; no court order is required.

The plaintiff must obtain a court order under r.16.04 authorizing substituted service; the court will grant the order if it is satisfied that personal service is impractical and the substituted method is likely to come to the defendant's attention.
Newspaper publication is never a valid form of substituted service in Ontario civil proceedings.
The plaintiff must serve the defendant's last known family member and then advertise in a newspaper simultaneously, without requiring a court order.
expl: Under r.16.04 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), where it appears to the court that it is impractical to effect prompt personal service of an originating process, the court may make an order for substituted service. The court may authorize service by any method — including newspaper publication — and will do so where: (1) personal service is impractical, and (2) the proposed substituted method is likely to come to the attention of the person to be served. A court order is mandatory; the plaintiff cannot unilaterally proceed with substituted service.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.16.04
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Service of Process | Substituted Service — When Permitted and Court Order Required

text:
A plaintiff is attempting to serve a defendant who has left Ontario and whose whereabouts are completely unknown. The plaintiff cannot identify any method of substituted service that is likely to come to the defendant's attention. The plaintiff brings a motion asking the court to dispense with service entirely.

Can a court dispense with service entirely under the Rules of Civil Procedure, and if so, what must the plaintiff demonstrate?
opts: No — a court can never dispense with service; it can only order a form of substituted service.
Yes — under r.16.04, a court may dispense with service entirely where it is satisfied that service is not reasonably possible and that dispensing with service is appropriate in the circumstances.
Yes — but only if the plaintiff agrees to indemnify the defendant for any costs arising from the default judgment obtained without service.
Yes — a court may dispense with service automatically after 6 months of failed service attempts without any further motion.
expl: Under r.16.04 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the court has authority not only to order substituted service but also to dispense with service entirely in appropriate circumstances. Where the court is satisfied that service of the originating process is not reasonably possible because the defendant's whereabouts are unknown and no method of substituted service would likely reach the defendant, the court has the discretion to make an order dispensing with service. This is an exceptional order reserved for cases where all reasonable efforts to notify the defendant have failed.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.16.04
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Service of Process | Substituted Service — Dispensing with Service

text:
A plaintiff served a statement of claim on the defendant on April 1, 2026. The defendant, served in Ontario, failed to deliver a statement of defence within the prescribed time. The plaintiff wishes to obtain a default judgment.

What procedural steps must the plaintiff take under the Rules of Civil Procedure before a default judgment can be signed?
opts: The plaintiff may immediately obtain a default judgment from the court without any prior steps once 20 days have passed from service.
Under r.19.01, the plaintiff must first have the registrar note the defendant in default; once noted in default, the plaintiff may then seek to obtain a default judgment under r.19.02 or r.19.04.
The plaintiff must bring a motion before a judge to obtain an order that the defendant is in default before any default judgment can be issued.
The plaintiff must serve a notice of default on the defendant and wait a further 10 days before any default judgment can be obtained.
expl: Under r.19.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), where a defendant fails to deliver a statement of defence within the prescribed time, the plaintiff may have the registrar note the defendant in default. This notation is the necessary prerequisite to obtaining a default judgment. Once the defendant has been noted in default, the plaintiff may seek default judgment — either by filing a requisition with the registrar (for liquidated claims under r.19.02) or by bringing a motion to the court (for unliquidated claims under r.19.04).
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.19.01; r.19.02; r.19.04
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Default Proceedings and Dismissal for Delay | Default Proceedings — Noting in Default and Default Judgment

text:
A defendant was noted in default after failing to deliver a statement of defence. Before a default judgment was signed, the defendant brings a motion under r.19.05 to set aside the noting in default. The defendant explains that the delay was caused by a serious illness that rendered the defendant unable to communicate with counsel for six weeks. The defendant's proposed statement of defence raises a credible limitation period defence.

Under r.19.05, what factors will the court consider on a motion to set aside a noting in default?
opts: The court will only set aside a noting in default if the defendant can prove the service of the statement of claim was invalid.
The court will automatically set aside any noting in default if it is brought before a default judgment has been signed.

The court may set aside a noting in default on such terms as are just, considering whether: there is a reasonable explanation for the default, the defendant has an arguable defence on the merits, and whether the plaintiff would suffer prejudice.
The court will not set aside a noting in default once the prescribed time to deliver a defence has expired by more than 30 days.
expl: Under r.19.05 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the court may set aside a noting of default on such terms as are just. Courts have consistently applied a three-part analysis: (1) whether there is a reasonable explanation for the default (here, a serious illness is a compelling excuse); (2) whether the defendant has an arguable defence on the merits (here, the limitation period defence is credible); and (3) whether granting the order would cause prejudice to the plaintiff that cannot be compensated by costs. Courts are generally inclined to set aside a noting in default before judgment is signed, as the objective of the Rules is to decide cases on their merits.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.19.05
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Default Proceedings and Dismissal for Delay | Setting Aside Noting in Default — r.19.05

text:
A defendant is frustrated by the plaintiff's failure to advance a personal injury action for the past three years. No discoveries have been completed, no trial date has been scheduled, and the plaintiff has taken no steps despite repeated requests. The defendant wants to have the action dismissed for delay.

Apart from the registrar's automatic dismissal process under r.48.14, what avenue does the defendant have to seek dismissal of the action for delay?
opts: The defendant may bring a motion under r.24.01 to dismiss the action for delay where the plaintiff has not taken steps in the action as required and the delay is unreasonable.
The defendant must wait for the registrar to issue a status notice before any motion to dismiss for delay can be brought.
The defendant cannot bring a motion to dismiss for delay; only the registrar can dismiss an action under the Rules.
The defendant may bring a motion for summary judgment on the grounds that the delay is evidence that the plaintiff has abandoned the claim.
expl: Under r.24.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a defendant may bring a motion to have an action dismissed for delay where the plaintiff has failed to take the steps required to bring the action to trial as required by the Rules. This motion is available independently of the registrar's automatic process under r.48.14. The court will consider the length and reasons for the delay, whether the defendant has been prejudiced, and the merits of the action. The power of a party to move to dismiss for delay under r.24.01 is in addition to — not a replacement for — the registrar's automatic process.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.24.01
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Default Proceedings and Dismissal for Delay | Dismissal for Delay — Motion to Dismiss by Defendant

text:
An action has been dormant for 18 months. No status notice has been issued by the registrar. The defendant's counsel is considering whether to wait for the registrar to issue a status notice or to bring a r.24.01 motion to dismiss for delay immediately.

Which of the following correctly distinguishes between the registrar's power to dismiss under r.48.14 and a party's right to move for dismissal under r.24.01?
opts: Under r.48.14, the registrar may dismiss an action after two years of inaction following a status notice; under r.24.01, any party may bring a motion to dismiss for delay at any time, regardless of how long the delay has been.
Under r.48.14, only the plaintiff can trigger the registrar's process; under r.24.01, only the defendant can bring a motion.
Under r.48.14, the registrar dismisses an action automatically without notice; under r.24.01, the motion requires the filing of expert evidence on the issue of delay.
Under r.48.14 and r.24.01, the standard for dismissal is identical; the only difference is who initiates the process.
expl: Under r.48.14 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, a registrar-initiated process applies after the action has been dormant for two years and the parties have failed to respond to a status notice; the registrar may then dismiss the action. Rule 24.01, by contrast, provides a party-initiated right to bring a motion to dismiss for delay at any time without waiting for the two-year mark or a status notice. The standards applied may differ: r.24.01 requires the court to consider all circumstances of the delay and prejudice, while r.48.14 is a more administrative process triggered by passage of time and non-response to the status notice. The defendant in the scenario can bring a r.24.01 motion without waiting 18 additional months for the registrar's process to mature.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.24.01; r.48.14
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Default Proceedings and Dismissal for Delay | Registrar's Dismissal vs Motion to Dismiss — Comparison

text:
The registrar dismissed an action for delay under r.48.14 after the plaintiff failed to respond to a status notice. The plaintiff's new counsel discovers the dismissal order two months later and brings a motion under r.37.14 to set aside the dismissal order and restore the action.

On a motion to set aside the registrar's dismissal order under r.37.14, what factors will the court consider?
opts: The court will automatically restore the action because a registrar's order can always be set aside by a judge on motion without showing any grounds.
The court will refuse to set aside the order because a registrar's dismissal for delay under r.48.14 is final and cannot be appealed or set aside.

The court will consider whether there is a reasonable explanation for the failure that led to dismissal, whether the action has merit, and whether restoring the action would cause prejudice to the defendant that cannot be compensated by costs.
The court will only set aside the dismissal if the plaintiff can prove it was caused by the defendant's misconduct.
expl: Under r.37.14 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a party may move to set aside or vary an order made by the registrar, or by the court on a motion without notice. On a motion to set aside a registrar's dismissal for delay, courts have consistently applied an analysis that considers: (1) whether there is a reasonable explanation for the default that led to dismissal; (2) whether the action has merit; and (3) whether restoring the action would cause prejudice to the defendant that cannot be compensated by costs. The court exercises discretion; the failure to respond to the status notice must be satisfactorily explained.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.37.14; r.48.14
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Default Proceedings and Dismissal for Delay | Setting Aside Dismissal Order — r.37.14

text:
Sebastiano brings an urgent motion without notice (ex parte) to preserve certain electronic evidence he believes the defendant is about to destroy. On the motion, Sebastiano's counsel fails to disclose to the judge that the defendant had already voluntarily agreed in writing three days earlier to preserve all relevant electronic records pending the litigation. The court grants the order. The defendant subsequently moves to set aside the preservation order.

What is the most likely outcome on the defendant's motion to set aside the order?
opts: The order will be maintained because the defendant had an obligation to bring the agreement to the court's attention before the ex parte motion was heard.
The order will be maintained unless the defendant can show it was prejudiced by the non-disclosure.

The court will set aside the order because Sebastiano's counsel breached the duty of full and frank disclosure by failing to disclose the defendant's prior agreement to preserve the evidence, which was material to whether the ex parte order was necessary.
The court will set aside the order only if the defendant can prove that Sebastiano's counsel acted in bad faith.
expl: On a motion without notice under r.37.07(7) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, the moving party owes the court a duty of full and frank disclosure of all material facts, including facts that are adverse to the moving party's position. The defendant's prior written agreement to preserve electronic records was a material fact directly relevant to the necessity of the ex parte preservation order — had the court known of the agreement, it may well have declined to grant the order or required notice to be given. Failure to disclose a material fact on an ex parte motion is a recognised ground for setting aside the resulting order, regardless of whether the non-disclosure was intentional. The court has discretion to set aside or vary the order.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.37.07(7)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Motions | Motion Without Notice — Permitted Circumstances and Disclosure Duty

text:
Fernanda brings a motion to compel production of certain documents. The court grants the motion and awards costs to Fernanda. The judge does not give any special reasons for the costs award and simply says 'costs to the successful party.' The defendant asks whether this is an unusual outcome.

Which statement best describes the general principle governing costs on motions under the Rules of Civil Procedure?
opts: Costs on motions are always in the cause — they are never awarded immediately after a motion and always await the outcome of the trial.
Under r.57.01, the general principle is that costs follow the event, meaning the successful party on a motion is presumptively entitled to the costs of the motion.
Costs on motions are awarded at the court's discretion but the starting presumption is that no costs are payable on interlocutory motions.
Costs on a motion can only be awarded at the end of trial; interim cost awards are not permitted.
expl: Under r.57.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, the court has a broad discretion in awarding costs, but the general principle is that costs follow the event — the successful party is presumptively entitled to its costs. On a motion, this means the party who succeeds on the motion is ordinarily awarded its costs of the motion. The court retains discretion to depart from this principle in appropriate circumstances (e.g., where the motion was necessary due to the conduct of both parties), but the default starting point is that the successful party receives its costs.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.01
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Motions | Costs on Motions — General Principles

text:
Counsel for the moving party prepares a motion record for an upcoming motion. He includes: (1) a table of contents, (2) the notice of motion, (3) all affidavits and other evidence to be used on the motion, (4) a draft order, and (5) transcripts of any cross-examinations on affidavits. The opposing party argues that the motion record is missing a required document.

Which of the following must also be included in a motion record under r.37.10 of the Rules of Civil Procedure?
opts: A copy of every pleading filed in the action to date.
The factum of the moving party setting out a concise statement of facts and law.
The trial transcript from any prior proceedings between the parties.
A certificate signed by all parties confirming they have exchanged all relevant documents.
expl: Rule 37.10(2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure specifies the required contents of a motion record. In addition to the table of contents, notice of motion, supporting affidavits and evidence, transcripts of cross-examinations, and draft order, the motion record must also include a copy of every pleading or other document on which the motion is based that has been filed in the proceeding. The opposing party is correct that the pleadings filed in the action must be included in the motion record.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.37.10(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Motions | Motion Record — Required Contents and Filing

text:
Novak Pharmaceuticals Inc. is a defendant in a product liability action brought by several plaintiffs. During documentary discovery, Novak produces internal safety test results to the plaintiffs under compulsion of the Rules of Civil Procedure. One of the plaintiffs, without obtaining a court order or Novak's consent, provides copies of the safety test results to a journalist, who publishes them in an investigative article. Novak brings a motion alleging a breach of the implied undertaking rule.

Which of the following best describes the implied undertaking rule and its application to these facts?
opts: The implied undertaking rule only applies to information obtained on oral examination for discovery, not to documents produced in the affidavit of documents process.

The implied undertaking rule under r.30.1.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure prohibits a party who receives documents through compelled disclosure from using those documents for any purpose other than the litigation in which they were produced; providing them to a journalist for publication breaches this rule.
The implied undertaking rule applies only to privileged documents; since Novak produced the safety results as non-privileged documents, there is no implied undertaking over them.

The implied undertaking rule prohibits use of documents outside the litigation unless the opposing party consents, but publication by a journalist is a protected exercise of freedom of expression that overrides the undertaking.
expl: Rule 30.1.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure codifies the implied undertaking rule in Ontario. It provides that all documents and information obtained through the discovery process — including documents produced in the affidavit of documents under compulsion of the Rules — are subject to an implied undertaking that they will be used only for the purposes of the litigation in which they were produced and will not be used for any other collateral purpose. The rule applies to all compelled disclosure, not only privileged documents or examination for discovery transcripts. Providing the safety test results to a journalist for publication in an article is plainly a collateral use outside the litigation and constitutes a breach of the implied undertaking under r.30.1.01. The undertaking can be released by court order or with the consent of the producing party, neither of which was obtained here.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.30.1.01
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Documentary Discovery | Implied Undertaking Rule — Collateral Use Prohibited

text:
Beaumont Construction Ltd. is defending a claim for defective workmanship. After the statement of claim was issued, Beaumont's project manager retained a structural engineering firm to inspect the building and prepare a report. The engineer prepared the report at the request of Beaumont's litigation counsel. The plaintiff now demands production of the engineering report on discovery, arguing it is not privileged because it was prepared by a third party, not by counsel.

Is the engineering report protected by litigation privilege?
opts: No — litigation privilege applies only to documents created by or exchanged directly between the lawyer and client; third-party reports are never protected.
Yes — any document created after litigation commences is automatically protected by litigation privilege.

Yes — provided that the dominant purpose for which the report was created was for use in the existing litigation; litigation privilege extends to communications with third parties when this dominant purpose test is met.
No — only solicitor-client privilege can protect third-party reports, and it would not apply here because the engineer is not Beaumont's lawyer.
expl: The Supreme Court of Canada in Blank v. Canada (Minister of Justice), 2006 SCC 39 confirmed that litigation privilege protects documents and communications — including those with third parties — whose dominant purpose is preparation for the conduct of reasonably anticipated or existing litigation. The privilege is not limited to lawyer-client communications; it extends to communications between a party (or their counsel) and third parties, such as expert witnesses, investigators, or consultants, so long as the dominant purpose of the communication is litigation preparation. Here, the structural engineering report was prepared at counsel's request specifically for the purpose of defending the existing litigation; the dominant purpose test is satisfied.
caseRef: Blank v. Canada (Minister of Justice), 2006 SCC 39; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.30.03(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Documentary Discovery | Litigation Privilege — Dominant Purpose Test

text:
Vandenberg Shipping Inc. settled a cargo damage claim brought by Okafor Importers Ltd. in 2021. The settlement was reached before trial and the action was dismissed. A separate action is now brought by a third party, Leblanc Freight Ltd., alleging it was damaged by the same cargo handling incident. Leblanc seeks production of investigation reports and witness statements that Vandenberg's counsel prepared in the 2021 Okafor action, arguing they are relevant to the current proceeding.

Are Vandenberg's investigation reports and witness statements from the concluded Okafor action protected by litigation privilege in the current Leblanc proceeding?
opts: Yes — litigation privilege is permanent, like solicitor-client privilege, and the documents remain protected in all future proceedings.
No — litigation privilege expired when the Okafor action was settled and dismissed; the documents are no longer protected and must be produced in the Leblanc action.
Yes — litigation privilege continues to apply to the documents in the new proceeding because the new claim arises from the same incident.
No — investigation reports prepared by counsel are never protected by litigation privilege; only direct communications between lawyer and client are covered.
expl: As confirmed by the Supreme Court of Canada in Blank v. Canada (Minister of Justice), 2006 SCC 39, litigation privilege is temporary and expires when the litigation for which the documents were prepared has concluded — whether by settlement, dismissal, judgment, or otherwise. Unlike solicitor-client privilege, which is permanent, litigation privilege exists solely to protect the adversarial process in the specific litigation for which the documents were created. Once the Okafor action was settled and dismissed, litigation privilege over the investigation reports and witness statements expired. Those documents are no longer protected in the subsequent Leblanc action (though solicitor-client privilege may independently protect any portions that constitute legal advice communications).
caseRef: Blank v. Canada (Minister of Justice), 2006 SCC 39; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.30.03(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Documentary Discovery | Litigation Privilege — Duration

text:
The defendant in a breach of contract action wants to make a formal offer to settle the action. The defendant's counsel is preparing the offer and asks what formal requirements must be satisfied under the Rules of Civil Procedure for the offer to attract the cost consequences under r.49.10.

Under r.49.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what are the basic requirements for a valid offer to settle?
opts: An offer to settle must be filed with the court and approved by a judge before it is effective.
An offer to settle may be made orally or in writing; if made in writing, it is effective when delivered to the opposing party's counsel.
An offer to settle must be in writing, signed by the party or their counsel, and served on the opposing party; it is effective when served.
An offer to settle must be made before the statement of defence is delivered; offers made after defence cannot attract cost consequences.
expl: Under r.49.02(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a party may make an offer to settle at any time. Rule 49.02(2) requires that an offer to settle be made in writing, and r.49.02(3) provides that an offer to settle is effective when it is served on the other party. The offer must be in writing to attract the cost consequences under r.49.10. An oral offer does not constitute an offer to settle for the purposes of r.49. The offer need not be filed with or approved by the court.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.02
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Offers to Settle | Making an Offer to Settle — Requirements Under r.49.02

text:
The plaintiff serves a written offer to settle on the defendant on April 10, 2026. The offer does not state a specific expiry date. On April 19, 2026 — nine days after service — the defendant serves a written acceptance of the offer. The plaintiff argues the offer had already lapsed because more than a week has passed.

Under r.49.04 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, when does an offer to settle that does not specify an expiry date expire?
opts: An offer expires automatically 7 days after service if no expiry date is stated.
An offer does not expire if no expiry date is stated; it remains open for acceptance indefinitely until withdrawn.
An offer expires automatically 10 days after service if no expiry date is stated.
An offer without an expiry date expires at the commencement of the trial.
expl: Under r.49.04 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), an offer to settle that does not specify an expiry date may be accepted at any time before the commencement of the hearing (trial). The offer does not automatically expire after 7 days or 10 days merely because no expiry date is stated. In this scenario, the defendant's acceptance on April 19 — nine days after service and before the commencement of trial — is a valid acceptance of the plaintiff's offer. The plaintiff is incorrect that the offer lapsed.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.04
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Offers to Settle | Withdrawal and Expiry of Offers to Settle — r.49.04

text:
The defendant serves a written offer to settle for $80,000 on the plaintiff. The plaintiff rejects the offer and proceeds to trial. At trial, the plaintiff is awarded judgment for $70,000 — less than the defendant's offer. The defendant seeks enhanced cost consequences.

Under r.49.10 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what are the cost consequences where the plaintiff fails to obtain a judgment more favourable than the defendant's offer to settle?
opts: The plaintiff is entitled to full solicitor-client costs for the entire action because the plaintiff succeeded at trial.
There are no cost consequences because the plaintiff obtained a judgment; the plaintiff's entitlement to partial indemnity costs for the entire action is unaffected.
Under r.49.10(1), the plaintiff is entitled to partial indemnity costs to the date of the offer, but the defendant is entitled to partial indemnity costs from the date the offer was made.
Under r.49.10(1), the plaintiff is entitled to partial indemnity costs to the date the offer was made, but the defendant is entitled to substantial indemnity costs from that date.
expl: Under r.49.10(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), where the defendant makes an offer to settle that is not accepted, and the plaintiff obtains a judgment that is no more favourable than the terms of the offer, the plaintiff is entitled to partial indemnity costs to the date the offer was made. However, the defendant is entitled to partial indemnity costs from the date the offer was made. This split costs award is designed to incentivise the acceptance of reasonable offers: the plaintiff recovers normal costs up to the offer date but must pay the defendant's partial indemnity costs after that date. Note: under the current Rules, the defendant is entitled to partial indemnity costs (not substantial indemnity costs) unless the court orders otherwise.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.10(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Offers to Settle | Cost Consequences — Defendant's Offer Better Than Plaintiff's Recovery

text:
The plaintiff served an offer to settle for $150,000. The defendant rejected the offer. At trial, the plaintiff was awarded $175,000 — more than the plaintiff's offer. The plaintiff seeks enhanced costs from the date of the offer.

Under r.49.10(2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what are the cost consequences where the plaintiff obtains a judgment more favourable than their own offer to settle?
opts: The plaintiff is entitled to partial indemnity costs for the entire action from the start to the end of trial.
The plaintiff is entitled to partial indemnity costs to the date the offer was made, and substantial indemnity costs from the date of the offer to the end of trial.
The plaintiff is entitled to solicitor-client costs for the entire action because the defendant unreasonably refused the offer.
There are no additional cost consequences beyond the ordinary partial indemnity costs award; the plaintiff's offer to settle has no relevance to costs.
expl: Under r.49.10(2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), where the plaintiff makes an offer to settle that is not accepted, and the plaintiff obtains a judgment as favourable as or more favourable than the terms of the offer, the plaintiff is entitled to partial indemnity costs to the date the offer was made, and substantial indemnity costs from the date of the offer to the end of trial. This enhanced cost consequence — the shift from partial to substantial indemnity — is the financial incentive for defendants to accept reasonable plaintiff offers. Substantial indemnity costs are approximately 1.5 times partial indemnity costs.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.10(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Offers to Settle | Cost Consequences — Plaintiff's Offer Beaten by Plaintiff's Recovery

text:
During the course of a personal injury action, the plaintiff makes three separate offers to settle: one for $200,000 (declined), one for $175,000 (declined), and one for $150,000 (declined). The defendant also makes one offer for $100,000 (declined). At trial, the plaintiff is awarded $160,000. Both parties seek cost consequences based on their respective offers.

Under r.49.13 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, how does the court determine which offer governs the cost consequences where multiple offers have been made?
opts: The court applies the cost consequences based on the plaintiff's first offer only; subsequent offers supersede prior offers and are treated as withdrawals.
The court applies cost consequences based on the most recent offer made before the close of trial.
Under r.49.13, the court has discretion to take into account any or all of the offers made in determining the appropriate cost consequences; the court is not restricted to a single offer.
The plaintiff's most recent offer ($150,000) governs exclusively because it is the only offer that was beaten by the trial award.
expl: Under r.49.13 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), despite r.49.10, the court, in exercising its discretion to award costs, may take into account any offer to settle made in writing, the date the offer was made, the terms of the offer, and the reasonableness of the parties' conduct in relation to the offer. Rule 49.13 gives the court broad discretion to consider the totality of the settlement history and all offers made by any party when determining the appropriate cost award. The court is not strictly bound to apply the automatic consequences of r.49.10 where multiple offers complicate the analysis; it can craft a just costs order considering all the circumstances.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.13
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Offers to Settle | Multiple Offers to Settle — r.49.13

text:
A defendant successfully defends a breach of contract action. The trial judge awards costs to the defendant on a partial indemnity basis but fixes the amount at $45,000 rather than the $90,000 sought by the defendant. The defendant's counsel believes the judge made an error in fixing the amount and wishes to appeal the costs order alone — not the dismissal of the action.

What procedural requirement must the defendant satisfy to appeal the costs order alone?
opts: The defendant may appeal the costs order as of right to the Court of Appeal without seeking leave, as any final order may be appealed.
Leave to appeal is required to appeal a costs order alone; an appeal that challenges only costs — not the merits of the decision — requires leave of the court because costs orders are discretionary.
The defendant may not appeal a costs order alone; the defendant must appeal both the costs order and another aspect of the trial decision simultaneously.
The defendant must first bring a motion at the trial court level to vary the costs order before any appellate court will consider an appeal.
expl: Under s.133(b) of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, no appeal lies from an order as to costs only, except by leave of the court to which the appeal is taken. Costs orders are discretionary in nature, and appellate courts grant leave to appeal costs only sparingly. Leave will generally be granted only where there is a strong case that the trial judge erred in principle in exercising the costs discretion, not merely because the appellant disagrees with the quantum fixed. The defendant must therefore seek and obtain leave before the appeal of the costs order alone can proceed.
caseRef: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.133(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 — Costs | Appeal of Costs Order — Leave Required

text:
Following a successful trial, the plaintiff has been awarded costs to be assessed. The plaintiff's lawyer files a bill of costs with the court. An assessment officer reviews the bill. The defendant disputes several items in the bill, arguing they were unreasonable or unnecessary.

Under Part 58 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what document does an assessment officer issue at the conclusion of a successful assessment, and what is its significance?
opts: The assessment officer issues a final costs order that is equivalent to a trial judgment and is immediately enforceable against the defendant.
The assessment officer issues a certificate of assessment, which sets out the amount allowed on assessment; the certificate may be filed with the court and has the effect of a court order.
The assessment officer issues a report to the trial judge recommending the quantum of costs; the trial judge must then issue a separate order confirming the amount.
The assessment officer's decision is not binding; it is merely an advisory opinion that either party may choose to follow or disregard.
expl: Under Part 58 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), when an assessment officer completes a review of a bill of costs, the officer issues a certificate of assessment setting out the amount allowed. Under r.58.10, the certificate of assessment may be filed with the registrar and, upon filing, has the same force and effect as an order of the court for the payment of money. This makes it enforceable by any of the normal enforcement mechanisms available for money judgments, including writs of execution and garnishment.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.58; r.58.10
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 — Costs | Assessment of Costs — Assessment Officer and Certificate

text:
At the conclusion of a commercial trial, the judge must fix the costs award payable by the defendant to the successful plaintiff. Counsel for both parties have filed their respective costs outlines. The defendant argues that the plaintiff's claimed costs are excessive and asks the court to reduce them significantly.

Under r.57.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, which of the following factors may the court consider when fixing costs?
opts: The court may only consider the hourly rate charged by counsel; all other factors are irrelevant to the quantum of costs.

The court may consider the amount claimed and the amount recovered in the proceeding, the complexity of the issues, the importance of the matter to the parties, the conduct of any party that tended to shorten or lengthen the proceedings, and whether any step in the proceeding was improper or unnecessary.
The court may only consider the Tariff A amounts prescribed for each step in the proceeding; it has no discretion to depart from those amounts.
The court may consider only whether the successful party was represented by senior or junior counsel.
expl: Under r.57.01(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the court shall consider a non-exhaustive list of factors when exercising its discretion to award costs. These include: the amount claimed and the amount recovered, the complexity of the issues, the importance of the matter to the parties, the conduct of any party that tended to shorten or lengthen the proceedings, whether any step was improper or unnecessary, the experience of counsel, the time spent by counsel, and the result achieved. The court has broad discretion and is not limited to considering only counsel rates or Tariff A amounts.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.01(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 — Costs | Partial Indemnity Costs — Factors Under r.57.01

text:
A successful plaintiff has obtained a costs order at trial. Rather than having the court fix costs immediately, the judge directs the plaintiff to proceed to assessment of costs. The plaintiff's counsel is preparing the documentation required for the assessment process.

Under r.58.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what document must a party prepare to initiate an assessment of costs?
opts: A costs outline setting out an estimate of anticipated fees and disbursements.
A bill of costs, which is a detailed statement of all services performed and disbursements incurred by the party entitled to costs.
An affidavit from the client confirming the legal fees actually paid.
A request for assessment, accompanied by a copy of the order granting costs, filed directly with the Court of Appeal.
expl: Under r.58.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a party who is entitled to costs of a proceeding shall prepare a bill of costs, which is assessed by an assessment officer. The bill of costs is a detailed itemized statement setting out all services performed by counsel and all disbursements incurred in the proceeding, to be assessed against the applicable tariff or as ordered by the court. The assessment officer reviews the bill of costs and issues a certificate of assessment.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.58.02
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 — Costs | Bill of Costs and Assessment — r.58.02

text:
A defendant in a commercial action brings a motion for security for costs against the corporate plaintiff. The defendant argues that the plaintiff corporation has no assets in Ontario and no realistic prospect of satisfying a costs award if the action is dismissed.

Under r.56.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, in what circumstances may a court order a plaintiff to post security for costs?
opts: Security for costs may be ordered against any plaintiff who is not ordinarily resident in Ontario or who is a corporation without sufficient assets in Ontario to satisfy a costs award.
Security for costs can only be ordered against foreign corporations; Ontario corporations are exempt.
Security for costs may only be ordered where the plaintiff has previously been found to be a vexatious litigant by court order.
Security for costs is ordered automatically whenever a corporate plaintiff fails to post a $50,000 bond with the court at the time the action is commenced.
expl: Under r.56.01(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a court may order any party to an action to give security for costs, and specifically enumerates grounds including: (a) the plaintiff is ordinarily not resident in Ontario; (b) the plaintiff has another proceeding pending for the same or related claim; (c) the plaintiff is a corporation without sufficient assets in Ontario to satisfy a costs award; (d) the plaintiff appears to be acting as a nominal plaintiff for another person; or (e) there is good reason to believe the action is frivolous and vexatious. The grounds are not limited to foreign corporations; an Ontario corporation with no Ontario assets also qualifies.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.56.01(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 — Costs | Security for Costs — r.56.01

text:
A plaintiff in a fraud action was deliberately misled by the defendant, who maintained a false defence throughout the proceedings — even after internal documents proved the fraud. At trial, the judge finds in favour of the plaintiff and determines that the defendant's litigation conduct was reprehensible and deserving of sanction.

In what circumstances will a court award substantial indemnity costs rather than partial indemnity costs, and approximately what scale does substantial indemnity represent?
opts: Substantial indemnity costs are awarded whenever the plaintiff succeeds on any ground; they represent 100% of actual legal fees.
Substantial indemnity costs are awarded in circumstances such as reprehensible, scandalous, or outrageous conduct by a party; they represent approximately 1.5 times the partial indemnity rate.
Substantial indemnity costs are awarded only when the court makes a contempt of court finding; they represent the same quantum as solicitor-client costs.
Substantial indemnity costs are awarded whenever a party beats a settlement offer under r.49.10; in all other cases only partial indemnity is available.
expl: Under r.57.01(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure and the associated practice directives, substantial indemnity costs are awarded in circumstances where a party's conduct in the litigation has been reprehensible, scandalous, or outrageous — conduct that deserves judicial sanction beyond the ordinary partial indemnity scale. Substantial indemnity costs represent approximately 1.5 times the partial indemnity rate. They may also be awarded pursuant to the cost consequences of r.49.10(2) when a plaintiff beats their own offer to settle. The deliberate maintenance of a fraudulent defence in the face of contrary evidence is a paradigm case for substantial indemnity costs.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.01(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 — Costs | Substantial Indemnity Costs — When Awarded

text:
Counsel for the plaintiff makes a series of highly inflammatory and irrelevant accusations against opposing counsel in written submissions to the court. The accusations are unfounded and appear to be designed to harass the defendant's counsel. The defendant brings a motion for solicitor-client costs against the plaintiff's counsel personally.

Under r.57.07 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, in what circumstances may the court order that costs be paid personally by a lawyer, and what is the costs scale that may be awarded?
opts: A lawyer may be ordered to pay costs personally only where the lawyer has been found in contempt of court by a judge.

Under r.57.07, a court may order a lawyer to personally pay costs — including on a solicitor-client basis — where the lawyer has caused costs to be incurred without reasonable cause or to be wasted by failing to provide legal services with reasonable care, skill, and efficiency, or by engaging in improper or abusive conduct.
A lawyer may be ordered to pay costs personally, but only partial indemnity costs; solicitor-client costs cannot be ordered against a lawyer.
A lawyer may only be required to pay costs personally through a Law Society complaint process, not through a court order.
expl: Under r.57.07 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the court has power to order that a lawyer personally pay costs where the lawyer has caused costs to be incurred without reasonable cause or to be wasted by unreasonable conduct, or by failing to provide legal services with reasonable competence. This includes situations where a lawyer engages in inflammatory, irrelevant, or abusive litigation conduct. The costs may be ordered on a solicitor-client basis, representing full indemnity for the costs caused by the lawyer's conduct. This is the most punitive costs sanction available and is reserved for the most egregious conduct.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.07
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 — Costs | Solicitor-Client Costs — Full Indemnity and Improper Conduct

text:
A party wishes to appeal an interlocutory order of the Superior Court of Justice. Counsel advises that leave to appeal is required. The party asks what test the appellate court applies when determining whether to grant leave to appeal from an interlocutory order.

Under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act, what is the test for obtaining leave to appeal an interlocutory order of the Superior Court of Justice to the Divisional Court?
opts: Leave is granted as a matter of right for all interlocutory orders; the only requirement is that the appeal be filed within 30 days.

Leave is granted where the proposed appeal raises an arguable point of law of general importance that ought to be decided by an appellate court, or there are conflicting decisions that need to be resolved.
Leave is granted only where the interlocutory order causes irreparable harm to the moving party pending trial.
Leave to appeal is not available for interlocutory orders; only final orders of the Superior Court may be appealed.
expl: Under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, an appeal lies to the Divisional Court from an interlocutory order of the Superior Court of Justice, with leave. Courts have consistently applied a test for leave that asks whether the proposed appeal raises an arguable point of law of general importance to the administration of justice, or whether there are conflicting decisions among trial courts that ought to be resolved, or whether the order is clearly wrong. The test is designed to filter out appeals that do not raise issues of broader importance while permitting genuine legal questions to proceed.
caseRef: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.6(1)(b); s.19(1)(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Appeals | Leave to Appeal — When Required and the Test

text:
After losing at trial, the appellant discovers a document that was not produced at trial and now seeks to introduce it on appeal as fresh evidence. The document was in the possession of a third party and was not obtained by the appellant before trial, although the appellant acknowledges that a request for it could have been made during discovery.

Under the Palmer test for the admission of fresh evidence on appeal, which criterion is the appellant most likely to fail to satisfy?
opts: The credibility criterion — the fresh evidence must be credible and probably accurate.
The relevance criterion — the fresh evidence must be relevant to a decisive issue at trial.

The due diligence criterion — the fresh evidence could not have been obtained with reasonable diligence before trial; the appellant's failure to request the document during discovery means this criterion is not met.
The decisive criterion — the fresh evidence must be conclusive on a key issue and is therefore too speculative to meet the standard.
expl: Under the Palmer test, derived from Palmer v. The Queen, [1980] 1 SCR 759 and applied in Ontario civil appeals, fresh evidence must satisfy four criteria to be admitted on appeal: (1) due diligence — the evidence could not have been obtained with reasonable diligence before trial; (2) relevance — the evidence must be relevant to a decisive issue; (3) credibility — the evidence must be apparently credible; and (4) decisiveness — the evidence must be such that if believed it could reasonably be expected to have affected the result. In the scenario, the appellant acknowledges that a request for the document could have been made during discovery. This failure to exercise due diligence is fatal to the first Palmer criterion and will likely result in refusal to admit the fresh evidence.
caseRef: Palmer v. The Queen, [1980] 1 SCR 759; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.61
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Appeals | Fresh Evidence on Appeal — Palmer Test

text:
On appeal, the appellant argues that the trial judge made two errors: (1) incorrectly applying the legal test for breach of fiduciary duty — a question of law; and (2) incorrectly finding that the defendant was aware of the plaintiff's reliance — a finding of fact. Counsel must advise on the appropriate standard of review for each issue.

Under Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33, what standard of review applies to questions of law on appeal compared to questions of fact?
opts: Both questions of law and questions of fact are reviewed on a standard of correctness on appeal.
Questions of law are reviewed on a standard of reasonableness, while questions of fact are reviewed on a standard of correctness.
Questions of law are reviewed on a standard of correctness; questions of fact are reviewed on a standard of palpable and overriding error, which is a highly deferential standard.
Both questions of law and questions of fact are reviewed on a standard of palpable and overriding error.
expl: Under the standard of review framework established by the Supreme Court of Canada in Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33, questions of law are reviewed on correctness — the appellate court owes no deference to the trial judge's legal conclusions and may substitute its own view. Questions of fact are reviewed on a standard of palpable and overriding error — the appellate court must identify a clear, plain, and determinative error that has affected the outcome before overturning a factual finding. This is a highly deferential standard that reflects the trial judge's privileged position as fact-finder.
caseRef: Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.61
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Appeals | Standard of Review on Appeal — Questions of Law vs Fact

text:
On appeal, the appellant challenges the trial judge's determination that the defendant's conduct constituted negligence. The trial judge applied the correct legal test for negligence (duty, breach, causation, damage) but the appellant argues the judge misapplied the test to the specific facts found. Counsel must identify the applicable standard of review.

Under Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33, what standard of review applies to mixed questions of fact and law?
opts: Mixed questions of fact and law are always reviewed on a correctness standard because they involve an element of legal reasoning.

Mixed questions of fact and law are reviewed on the palpable and overriding error standard as a default; however, if a legal error can be extracted and isolated from the factual findings, that extracted legal question is reviewed on correctness.
Mixed questions of fact and law are reviewed on the reasonableness standard, giving deference to the trial judge's balancing of legal and factual considerations.
Mixed questions of fact and law are reviewed on a balance of probabilities standard by the appellate court.
expl: Under the framework in Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33, mixed questions of fact and law are reviewed on palpable and overriding error as the default standard, because such questions are typically dominated by factual findings and trial courts are best positioned to make them. However, where an extricable legal question can be isolated from the factual findings — for example, where the trial judge has incorrectly defined the legal test or applied an incorrect legal principle — that extracted legal question is reviewed on correctness. The challenge in the fact pattern involves the application of a correct legal test to facts, which is a classic mixed question reviewed on palpable and overriding error absent an extricable legal error.
caseRef: Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Appeals | Standard of Review — Mixed Questions of Fact and Law

text:
A proposed class action is brought against a pharmaceutical company alleging that a widely-distributed medication caused harmful side effects. The plaintiff moves for certification under the Class Proceedings Act, 1992. The defendant argues that the proceeding should not be certified.

Under s.5 of the Class Proceedings Act, 1992 (Ontario), what are the five criteria that must be satisfied for a class proceeding to be certified?
opts: The court must find: (1) the claim is meritorious; (2) the class size exceeds 100 persons; (3) the representative plaintiff has legal standing; (4) all class members have identical claims; and (5) a class proceeding is less expensive than individual litigation.

The court must find: (1) the pleadings disclose a cause of action; (2) there is an identifiable class of two or more persons; (3) the claims raise common issues; (4) a class proceeding would be the preferable procedure; and (5) there is a representative plaintiff who would fairly and adequately represent the class.

The court must find: (1) the plaintiff has suffered damages; (2) the defendant was negligent; (3) there are more than 50 class members; (4) common issues predominate over individual issues; and (5) the plaintiff has resources to fund the litigation.

The court must find: (1) the pleadings are not frivolous; (2) there is a certified class representative; (3) the defendant has insurance coverage for the claim; (4) all class members are Ontario residents; and (5) the class action will proceed to trial within three years.
expl: Under s.5(1) of the Class Proceedings Act, 1992, S.O. 1992, c. 6, the court shall certify a class proceeding if: (a) the pleadings disclose a cause of action; (b) there is an identifiable class of two or more persons who would be represented by the representative plaintiff; (c) the claims of the class members raise common issues, whether or not those common issues predominate over issues affecting only individual class members; (d) a class proceeding would be the preferable procedure for the resolution of the common issues; and (e) there is a representative plaintiff who would fairly and adequately represent the interests of the class, has produced a plan for the proceeding, and does not have a conflict of interest with other class members on the common issues.
caseRef: Class Proceedings Act, 1992, S.O. 1992, c. 6, s.5(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 16 — Class Proceedings | Certification — Five-Part Test Under s.5 of the Class Proceedings Act

text:
A class action against a national retailer for privacy breaches has been certified. The court approves a notice plan to inform class members of their rights.
