A class member, Darius, receives the notice and wants to pursue his own individual lawsuit against the retailer rather than participate in the class action.

Under s.9 of the Class Proceedings Act, 1992, what right does Darius have and what must he do to exercise it?
opts: Darius has no right to opt out of a certified class proceeding; once certified, all members of the defined class are automatically bound.

Darius may opt out of the class proceeding by giving notice to the defendant's counsel within the opt-out period specified in the court's notice order; upon opting out, he is no longer bound by any judgment in the class proceeding.

Darius may opt out of the class proceeding by filing a notice of opt-out with the court administrator within the opt-out period set by the court; upon opting out, he is not bound by any judgment in the class action and may pursue his own individual claim.
Darius may opt out but only with the permission of the court, which will grant permission only if Darius can demonstrate that his individual claim has a higher value than the class average.
expl: Under s.9(1) of the Class Proceedings Act, 1992, S.O. 1992, c. 6, any member of a class may opt out of the class proceeding by giving notice of election to opt out in the manner and within the time specified in the notice. The court's notice order sets out the opt-out period and the method for exercising the opt-out right. A class member who opts out is not bound by any judgment in the class proceeding — whether favourable or unfavourable — and retains the right to pursue an individual claim, subject to any applicable limitation period. The opt-out notice is typically filed with the class counsel or an administrator designated by the court, not with opposing counsel.
caseRef: Class Proceedings Act, 1992, S.O. 1992, c. 6, s.9
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 16 — Class Proceedings | Opt-Out Right — Notice and Procedure Under s.9 of the CPA

text:
A client is charged with theft under $5,000 contrary to s. 322 of the Criminal Code. The client asks her lawyer which court will try the case and whether she has the right to elect her mode of trial.

Which court has jurisdiction to try this charge, and does the accused have the right to elect her mode of trial?
opts: The Ontario Court of Justice has absolute jurisdiction over the charge under s. 553 of the Criminal Code; the accused has no right to elect her mode of trial.
The accused must be tried in the Superior Court of Justice because all theft charges carry a potential penalty exceeding six months.
The accused may elect to be tried by a superior court judge alone or by a superior court judge and jury.
The Ontario Court of Justice has jurisdiction only if the Crown elects to proceed summarily; if the Crown proceeds by indictment, the accused may elect her mode of trial.
expl: Under s. 553 of the Criminal Code, certain indictable offences fall within the absolute jurisdiction of a provincial court judge (in Ontario, the Ontario Court of Justice). Theft under $5,000 (s. 322 where the value does not exceed $5,000) is listed in s. 553(a). Where an offence falls within the absolute jurisdiction of a provincial court judge, the accused has no right to elect her mode of trial — the case is tried by a provincial court judge without a jury and without a preliminary inquiry. This is distinct from hybrid offences or other indictable offences where election rights arise.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 553(a)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Court Structure and Jurisdiction | Absolute and Exclusive Jurisdiction

text:
Raj is charged with assault causing bodily harm, which is a hybrid offence under s. 267 of the Criminal Code. The Crown has not yet made its election. Raj's defence counsel advises him that the Crown's choice will significantly affect the available penalties and the court in which Raj will be tried.

Who elects the mode of proceeding for a hybrid offence, and when must that election be made?
opts: The accused elects whether to proceed summarily or by indictment at the time of arraignment.
The Crown elects whether to proceed summarily or by indictment; it must make this election before the accused is required to plead.
The court decides whether to proceed summarily or by indictment based on the seriousness of the offence as disclosed in the information.
The accused and Crown jointly elect the mode of proceeding, and the court must approve the joint election.
expl: For hybrid offences (also called Crown election offences or dual procedure offences), it is the Crown — not the accused — that elects whether to proceed summarily or by indictment. The Crown must make this election before the accused is required to plead to the charge. Once the Crown elects, the offence is treated as either a summary conviction or indictable offence for all subsequent purposes, including jurisdiction, limitation periods, mode of trial, and available penalties. If the Crown proceeds summarily on assault causing bodily harm, the maximum is 18 months imprisonment; by indictment, the maximum is 10 years.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 267; interpretation of hybrid offences
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Court Structure and Jurisdiction | Summary, Indictable, and Hybrid Offences

text:
Marcus is charged with first degree murder under s. 235 of the Criminal Code. His defence counsel explains that the case cannot be tried by a provincial court judge and that the accused's election is also limited in this case.

Which court has exclusive jurisdiction to try first degree murder, and what election right does the accused have?
opts: The Ontario Court of Justice has jurisdiction if the Crown elects to proceed by indictment; the accused may elect a jury or judge-alone trial.
The Superior Court of Justice has exclusive jurisdiction under s. 469 of the Criminal Code; the accused may still elect between a judge-alone trial and a jury trial.

The Superior Court of Justice has exclusive jurisdiction under s. 469 of the Criminal Code; the accused has no right to elect — the trial must be by judge and jury unless the accused and Crown jointly apply for a judge-alone trial.
The Court of Appeal has original jurisdiction for murder charges because of their seriousness.
expl: Under s. 469 of the Criminal Code, certain offences — including murder, treason, alarming Her Majesty, seditious offences, piracy, and others — fall within the exclusive jurisdiction of a superior court. First degree murder under s. 235 is an s. 469 offence. When an accused is charged with an s. 469 offence, there is no standard election under s. 536. The trial must proceed before a superior court judge and jury unless the accused and the Attorney General both consent and the judge approves a judge-alone trial under s. 473. This is the reverse of the usual rule: for most indictable offences, a jury trial is an option the accused chooses; for s. 469 offences, a jury trial is the default and a judge-alone trial requires consent from both sides.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, ss. 469, 473
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Court Structure and Jurisdiction | Absolute and Exclusive Jurisdiction

text:
Samira is charged with fraud over $5,000 contrary to s. 380(1)(a) of the Criminal Code. The offence is not listed in s. 553 (absolute provincial court jurisdiction) and is not an s. 469 offence. Samira asks her lawyer to explain the three options available to her for mode of trial.

Which statement correctly describes the three modes of trial available to Samira under s. 536 of the Criminal Code?
opts: Samira may elect: (1) Ontario Court of Justice judge without a jury and without a preliminary inquiry; (2) Ontario Court of Justice judge without a jury but with a preliminary inquiry; (3) Superior Court judge and jury.
Samira may elect: (1) Ontario Court of Justice judge without a jury; (2) Superior Court of Justice judge without a jury; (3) Superior Court of Justice judge and jury.
Samira may elect: (1) summary conviction trial in the Ontario Court of Justice; (2) indictable trial before a superior court judge alone; (3) jury trial in the Superior Court.
Samira has no right to elect — for fraud over $5,000, only the Crown determines the mode of trial.
expl: For indictable offences that are neither within the absolute jurisdiction of a provincial court judge (s. 553) nor within the exclusive jurisdiction of a superior court (s. 469), the accused has the right under s. 536(2) to elect the mode of trial. The three modes are: (1) trial by a provincial court judge sitting alone without a jury (Ontario Court of Justice); (2) trial by a superior court judge sitting alone without a jury (Superior Court of Justice); and (3) trial by a superior court judge sitting with a jury. Each mode has different implications for preliminary inquiries and procedural steps. Fraud over $5,000 is a non-s. 553, non-s. 469 indictable offence, so Samira has a full election under s. 536.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 536(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Court Structure and Jurisdiction | Accused's Election — Three Modes of Trial

text:
Police are investigating a suspected drug trafficking operation. A detective prepares a sworn document describing her grounds for believing that controlled substances and cash proceeds will be found at a specific residential address. She attends before a judicial officer to obtain authority to search the premises.

Under s. 487 of the Criminal Code, who has jurisdiction to issue a search warrant for a residential dwelling?
opts: Only a Superior Court judge.
A justice of the peace or a judge.
Any peace officer of the rank of sergeant or above.
A justice of the peace only, not a judge.
expl: Section 487(1) of the Criminal Code authorizes a justice (which includes both a justice of the peace and a judge per the Code's definition) to issue a search warrant. The applicant must satisfy the justice on reasonable grounds to believe: (a) an offence has been or is suspected of having been committed; (b) evidence of the offence, or something that will afford evidence, will be found at the place; and (c) the thing sought will afford evidence. Justices of the peace routinely issue search warrants; superior court judges may also do so.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 487(1); Interpretation Act definition of 'justice'
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | Search Warrant Issuance — s. 487

text:
Detective Cho applies for a search warrant under s. 487 of the Criminal Code to search a business premises for documents relating to a suspected fraud. In her Information to Obtain (ITO), she states only that she 'suspects' the documents will be found there because an informant told her so. She does not provide the informant's basis of knowledge or any corroborating evidence.

Is Detective Cho's ITO sufficient to support issuance of the search warrant?
opts: Yes — suspicion is sufficient; the justice of the peace can infer reasonable grounds from the bare suspicion.
No — the ITO must establish reasonable grounds to believe, not merely reasonable grounds to suspect, and bare suspicion without corroboration is insufficient.
Yes — a confidential informant tip is always sufficient to establish reasonable grounds to believe.
No — s. 487 requires proof beyond a reasonable doubt before a warrant may issue.
expl: Section 487(1) requires the affiant to satisfy the justice that there are reasonable grounds to believe that the thing sought will be found at the specified place and will afford evidence. 'Reasonable grounds to believe' is a higher standard than 'reasonable grounds to suspect.' A bare assertion of suspicion, without establishing the informant's reliability and basis of knowledge (the Debot/Garofoli framework), is insufficient. The ITO must contain enough detail for the issuing justice to independently assess whether the threshold is met. Mere suspicion, even from an informant, does not satisfy the s. 487 standard.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 487(1); R. v. Garofoli, [1990] 2 SCR 1421; R. v. Debot, [1989] 2 SCR 1140
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | Search Warrant Issuance — ITO Requirements

text:
A search warrant is issued and executed at a residence at 11:30 p.m. The warrant itself contains no provision authorizing nighttime execution. Police recover valuable evidence. The accused applies to exclude the evidence under s. 24(2) of the Charter, arguing the search was unlawful because it was conducted at night without authorization.

Is the nighttime execution of the warrant a breach of s. 8 of the Charter?
opts: No — once a valid warrant issues, it may be executed at any time of day or night.
No — s. 8 only requires that a warrant exist; the time of execution is irrelevant to the constitutional analysis.

Yes — s. 488 of the Criminal Code requires search warrants to be executed during the daytime unless the warrant expressly authorizes nighttime execution, and failure to comply renders the search unreasonable under s. 8.
Yes — a nighttime search of a dwelling-house is always a per se violation of s. 8, regardless of what the warrant says.
expl: Section 488 of the Criminal Code provides that a search warrant shall be executed by day (6 a.m. to 9 p.m.) unless the justice is satisfied there are reasonable grounds to execute it by night and the warrant expressly authorizes nighttime execution. Executing a warrant at 11:30 p.m. without express nighttime authorization violates s. 488. Because s. 8 of the Charter requires that searches be conducted in a reasonable manner, a search that violates the statutory procedural safeguards in s. 488 is presumptively unreasonable under s. 8. The remedy under s. 24(2) then depends on the Grant analysis.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 487(1), s. 488; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 8, s. 24(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | Search Warrant Execution — s. 488

text:
Police obtain a search warrant for a dwelling-house on January 10. The warrant is not executed until February 28 — 49 days later. The warrant contains no express extended execution period. At the accused's trial, defence counsel argues the search was unlawful because the warrant had expired. Crown counsel responds that the Criminal Code places no time limit on execution of a search warrant.

Who is correct about the validity of the warrant execution?
opts: Crown counsel — the Criminal Code imposes no expiry date on search warrants; they remain valid indefinitely.
Defence counsel — search warrants automatically expire 30 days after issuance under the Criminal Code.
Defence counsel — a search warrant for a dwelling-house must be executed within 30 days under s. 487.1 of the Criminal Code.

Defence counsel — the Criminal Code does not impose a statutory 30-day expiry on s. 487 warrants; under common law, a search warrant must be executed within a reasonable time after issuance, and an unexplained 49-day delay may support a Charter s. 8 challenge.
expl: The Criminal Code, s. 487 does not establish a universal statutory expiry period for search warrants. Option B's claim that warrants automatically expire at 30 days under the Criminal Code is unsupported — no such provision exists in s. 487 itself. Option C erroneously cites s. 487.1, which governs telewarrants (obtaining search warrants by telephone or radio) and has no bearing on dwelling-house warrant expiry. Under common law, a search warrant must be executed within a reasonable time, having regard to all the circumstances. Courts assess: whether the ITO grounds establishing probable cause remained fresh at the time of execution; the nature of the offence; any explanation for the delay; and prejudice caused. A 49-day unexplained delay could support a defence argument that the warrant was executed unreasonably late, rendering the search unreasonable under Charter s. 8. Note: the CDSA, s. 11(5) provides a 15-day statutory expiry for drug-search warrants under that statute, which illustrates that specific statutory expiry rules exist in other regimes but not in Criminal Code s. 487.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 487, s. 488; Controlled Drugs and Substances Act, S.C. 1996, c. 19, s. 11(5)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | Search Warrant — Execution Timing

text:
David is charged with robbery (an offence not listed in s. 553 or s. 469 of the Criminal Code). At his first appearance in the Ontario Court of Justice, the judge asks David how he elects to be tried.

Under s. 536(2) of the Criminal Code, which of the following correctly describes the three modes of trial available to David?
opts: Provincial court judge alone; Superior Court judge alone; Superior Court judge and jury.
Provincial court judge alone; Superior Court judge and jury; Court of Appeal.
Ontario Court of Justice jury; Superior Court judge alone; Superior Court judge and jury.
Federal court judge alone; Superior Court judge alone; Superior Court judge and jury.
expl: Section 536(2) of the Criminal Code provides that an accused charged with an indictable offence (other than one listed in s. 553 or s. 469) may elect to be tried by: (1) a provincial court judge (Ontario Court of Justice) sitting alone; (2) a judge of a superior court of criminal jurisdiction sitting alone (without a jury); or (3) a court composed of a judge of a superior court and a jury. These are the only three modes available under s. 536. The Court of Appeal, federal courts, and provincial court juries are not modes of trial election under s. 536.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 536(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5: Pre-Trial Procedure | Mode of Trial Election — s. 536

text:
Fatima is charged with an offence listed in s. 536(2) of the Criminal Code. She initially elects trial by provincial court judge. Four months later, before the preliminary inquiry stage has concluded, she wishes to change her election to trial by superior court judge alone.

Under the Criminal Code, may Fatima change her election, and if so, what is required?
opts: No — an election is irrevocable once made; Fatima must proceed before the provincial court judge.
Yes — Fatima may re-elect as of right at any time before the trial commences, without conditions.
Yes — under s. 561, Fatima may re-elect to a superior court mode before the end of the preliminary inquiry with the consent of the Crown, or after the preliminary inquiry is complete.
Yes — Fatima may re-elect at any time after indictment, but only with leave of the court.
expl: Section 561 of the Criminal Code governs re-election of mode of trial. An accused who has elected trial by provincial court judge may re-elect to a superior court mode (judge alone or judge and jury) at various stages. Where a preliminary inquiry has not been waived, s. 561 permits re-election before the end of the preliminary inquiry with the consent of the Crown, or without Crown consent after the preliminary inquiry is completed and before the trial begins. The precise procedural steps and timing are governed by s. 561 and related provisions. An election is not irrevocable; however, re-election requires compliance with the statutory process in s. 561, not an unrestricted as-of-right change at any time.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 536(2), s. 561
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5: Pre-Trial Procedure | Re-Election of Mode of Trial — s. 561

text:
Marcus is charged with fraud over $5,000. At his first appearance in the Ontario Court of Justice, he tells the judge he wants to plead guilty. Defence counsel advises that the court must conduct an inquiry before accepting the plea.

Under s. 606(1.1) of the Criminal Code, which of the following must the court be satisfied of before accepting a guilty plea?
opts: That the accused has obtained independent legal advice and that the Crown consents to the plea.
That the accused is making the plea voluntarily, understands the nature of the charge, understands the consequences of the plea, and that the facts admitted are sufficient to support a conviction.
That the accused's counsel has reviewed the disclosure and that the accused has no outstanding Charter applications.
That the accused understands the nature of the charge and that the Crown has provided a sentencing recommendation.
expl: Section 606(1.1) of the Criminal Code requires the court to be satisfied of four things before accepting a guilty plea: (a) the accused is making the plea voluntarily; (b) the accused understands the nature of the charge; (c) the accused understands that the plea is an admission of the essential elements of the offence; (d) the accused understands the nature and consequences of the plea, including any direct consequences. The court must also be satisfied that the facts admitted by the accused are sufficient to support a conviction for the offence to which the accused is pleading guilty. Crown consent and independent legal advice are not statutory prerequisites under s. 606(1.1).
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 606(1) and s. 606(1.1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5: Pre-Trial Procedure | Guilty Plea Requirements — s. 606

text:
Priya is charged with assault causing bodily harm. She wishes to plead guilty to the lesser offence of common assault. The Crown opposes the proposed plea to the lesser offence.

Under the Criminal Code, may the court accept Priya's guilty plea to the lesser offence of common assault over the Crown's objection?
opts: Yes — the court has an inherent jurisdiction to accept any plea the accused offers, regardless of Crown consent.
Yes — s. 606(4) permits the court to accept a plea to a lesser included offence at any time, without requiring Crown consent.
No — under s. 606(4), acceptance of a guilty plea to a lesser or included offence requires the consent of the Crown.
No — the court must enter a not guilty plea on behalf of the accused and proceed to trial if the Crown refuses to consent.
expl: Section 606(4) of the Criminal Code provides that if an accused pleads not guilty to the offence charged but guilty to some other offence arising out of the same transaction, whether or not it is an included offence, the court may, with the consent of the Crown, accept such a plea. Crown consent is a mandatory prerequisite under s. 606(4). The court cannot impose acceptance of the lesser plea over the Crown's objection. If Crown consent is withheld, the court enters a plea of not guilty to the charge as laid.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 606(4)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5: Pre-Trial Procedure | Guilty Plea to Lesser Offence — s. 606(4)

text:
At the conclusion of a preliminary inquiry for a murder charge, defence counsel argues that the Crown's evidence is insufficient to commit the accused to trial. The presiding judge reviews the evidence and concludes that a properly instructed jury, acting reasonably, could return a verdict of guilty.

Based on this conclusion, what order must the preliminary inquiry judge make?
opts: Discharge the accused, because the evidence does not prove guilt beyond a reasonable doubt.
Commit the accused to stand trial, because the evidence meets the standard of whether a properly instructed jury could return a verdict of guilty.
Adjourn the hearing and direct the Crown to produce additional evidence before a committal order may be made.
Reserve judgment and refer the matter to the Superior Court for a determination of sufficiency.
expl: The committal standard at a preliminary inquiry is not proof beyond a reasonable doubt. Under s. 548(1) of the Criminal Code, the justice shall order the accused to stand trial if there is sufficient evidence — meaning evidence that, if believed, a jury properly instructed could return a verdict of guilty. The standard is whether the evidence is capable of supporting a conviction, not whether it will or should do so. Because the judge concluded that a properly instructed jury acting reasonably could convict, the committal standard is met and the accused must be committed to stand trial.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 548(1); United States of America v. Shephard, [1977] 2 SCR 1067
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5: Pre-Trial Procedure | Preliminary Inquiry — Committal Standard

text:
The accused is charged with break and enter. At the close of the Crown's case, all witnesses have testified. The trial judge must instruct the jury on the standard of proof required for a conviction.

What standard of proof must the Crown satisfy to obtain a conviction in a criminal trial?
opts: Balance of probabilities — the Crown must show it is more likely than not that the accused committed the offence.
Clear and convincing evidence — the Crown must satisfy the jury with a high degree of certainty.
Proof beyond a reasonable doubt — the highest standard in Canadian law, falling short of absolute certainty.
Prima facie case — the Crown need only establish enough evidence to go to the jury.
expl: In criminal proceedings, the Crown bears the burden of proving every element of the offence beyond a reasonable doubt. As the Supreme Court of Canada held in R. v. Lifchus, [1997] 3 SCR 320, proof beyond a reasonable doubt is not based on sympathy or prejudice but on reason and common sense. It is not proof to an absolute certainty — because that is impossible in most cases — but it falls far short of a balance of probabilities. A reasonable doubt is a doubt based on reason and common sense, logically connected to the evidence.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 11(d); R. v. Lifchus, [1997] 2 SCR 1; Woolmington v. DPP, [1935] AC 462
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | Burden of Proof

text:
The accused is charged with assault. She raises self-defence. The trial judge instructs the jury: 'If you believe on a balance of probabilities that the accused acted in self-defence, you must acquit.' Defence counsel objects to this instruction.

Is the trial judge's instruction on self-defence correct?
opts: Yes — the accused bears the burden of proving an affirmative defence on a balance of probabilities.
Yes — once the accused raises a defence, the jury may acquit on a balance of probabilities.
No — the accused bears only an evidential burden to raise the defence; the Crown then bears the persuasive burden of disproving self-defence beyond a reasonable doubt.
No — the accused must prove self-defence beyond a reasonable doubt to satisfy the jury.
expl: Under the Criminal Code s.34 (self-defence), the accused need only raise an air of reality to the defence — an evidential burden, not a persuasive burden. Once the defence has an air of reality, the Crown must disprove the defence beyond a reasonable doubt. The instruction that the jury must acquit on a balance of probabilities is incorrect because it places the persuasive burden on the accused rather than requiring the Crown to disprove the defence beyond a reasonable doubt. The Woolmington principle (as applied in Canada through the Charter, s.11(d)) requires that the Crown disprove defences it cannot exclude beyond a reasonable doubt.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 34; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 11(d); R. v. Cinous, [2002] 2 SCR 3 (air of reality test)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | Burden of Proof — Defences

text:
At the close of the Crown's case in a jury trial for robbery, defence counsel brings a motion for a directed verdict of acquittal. Counsel argues that the Crown has led no direct evidence that the accused used or threatened violence, which is an essential element of robbery.

What standard does the trial judge apply when deciding a motion for a directed verdict of acquittal?
opts: Whether the Crown has proved every element of the offence beyond a reasonable doubt.
Whether there is any evidence — no matter how weak — on which a properly instructed jury could return a verdict of guilty.
Whether the Crown's evidence, if believed, makes a conviction more likely than not.
Whether the Crown has led direct evidence on every essential element of the offence.
expl: On a motion for a directed verdict of acquittal, the trial judge applies the standard from United States of America v. Shephard and R. v. Monteleone: whether there is any evidence — which, if believed by a properly instructed jury acting reasonably, could support a verdict of guilty on every essential element of the offence. The test is not whether the evidence is strong, credible, or sufficient to convict; it is whether there is any evidence on which a verdict of guilty could rationally be based. If the answer is yes, the motion must be dismissed and the case goes to the jury. Only if there is a complete absence of evidence on an essential element must the judge direct an acquittal.
caseRef: R. v. Monteleone, [1987] 2 SCR 154; United States of America v. Shephard, [1977] 2 SCR 1067
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | Directed Verdict of Acquittal

text:
Police stop a driver for a minor traffic infraction. Without any basis for suspicion of impairment, the officer demands the driver blow into a screening device. The driver registers a 'warn.' He is then arrested for impaired driving and his blood alcohol content is later measured at 145 mg/100 ml. The accused applies to exclude the breath evidence under s. 24(2) of the Charter, arguing the roadside demand violated s. 9 (arbitrary detention).

Under the Grant test (R. v. Grant, 2009 SCC 32), which of the following is NOT one of the three lines of inquiry for s. 24(2)?
opts: The seriousness of the Charter-infringing conduct.
The impact of the breach on the Charter-protected interests of the accused.
Society's interest in the adjudication on the merits.
The subjective intent of the police officer who committed the breach.
expl: In R. v. Grant, 2009 SCC 32, the Supreme Court of Canada replaced the Collins/Stillman test with a three-part balancing framework for s. 24(2). The three lines of inquiry are: (1) the seriousness of the Charter-infringing conduct (was the conduct deliberate, wilful, or flagrant?); (2) the impact of the breach on the Charter-protected interests of the accused (the more serious the impact on the accused's dignity, bodily integrity, or privacy, the more exclusion is favoured); and (3) society's interest in the adjudication on the merits (the truth-seeking interest, reliability of evidence, and importance of the evidence to the Crown's case). The subjective intent of the specific officer is not a freestanding line of inquiry; rather, good or bad faith is a factor under the first line (seriousness of breach), not a separate criterion.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 24(2); R. v. Grant, 2009 SCC 32
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | Exclusion of Evidence — s. 24(2) Grant Test

text:
Officers conducted a warrantless search of the accused's home, finding a significant quantity of cocaine. The Charter breach (s.8) was serious — the officers knew they needed a warrant and deliberately proceeded without one. The cocaine is reliable evidence of a serious drug trafficking offence. The accused applies to exclude the cocaine under s. 24(2).

Applying the Grant framework, which factor most strongly favours exclusion of the evidence in this scenario?
opts: The reliability of the evidence, because real evidence is always more reliable than testimonial evidence.
The seriousness of the Charter breach — deliberate police disregard of the warrant requirement is highly culpable state conduct.
Society's interest in adjudicating on the merits — the cocaine is critical to the Crown's case.
The impact on the accused's rights is minimal because the accused had no reasonable expectation of privacy in cocaine.
expl: Under the Grant framework's first line of inquiry, the seriousness of the Charter-infringing conduct weighs heavily toward exclusion when officers deliberately disregard a known constitutional requirement. Wilful or flagrant police misconduct — knowingly proceeding without a warrant — is treated as more serious than a good-faith error. The SCC in Grant noted that admitting evidence obtained through deliberate, wilful breach risks bringing the administration of justice into disrepute by appearing to condone flagrant state misconduct. While the importance of the evidence (third line) and reliability (also third line) generally favour admission for real evidence, they are outweighed in cases of deliberate, egregious breach. The most strongly pro-exclusion factor on these facts is the deliberate, wilful nature of the breach.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 8, s. 24(2); R. v. Grant, 2009 SCC 32; R. v. Côté, 2011 SCC 46
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | Exclusion of Evidence — s. 24(2) Grant Analysis

text:
The accused, while in police custody, made an inculpatory statement to police. The Crown seeks to introduce the statement at trial. Defence counsel objects, arguing the statement was made involuntarily because the accused was held in a small room for six hours without food or water, and police told him that 'things would go easier' for him if he cooperated.

What procedural step must the trial judge take before admitting the accused's statement into evidence?
opts: The trial judge must instruct the jury to disregard the statement if the jury finds the accused was not properly cautioned.
The trial judge must conduct a voir dire to determine whether the statement was voluntarily made, applying the confessions rule.
The trial judge may admit the statement provisionally and leave the question of voluntariness to the jury.
The trial judge must exclude the statement as a matter of law whenever police made a promise or threat during an interrogation.
expl: When the admissibility of an accused's out-of-court statement to a person in authority is challenged, the trial judge must conduct a voir dire — a trial within a trial — to determine whether the statement was voluntarily made. The confessions rule (R. v. Oickle, 2000 SCC 38) requires the Crown to prove voluntariness beyond a reasonable doubt. The judge considers whether the statement was obtained by threats, promises, oppression, operating mind, or police trickery. Only if the judge finds the statement voluntary on the voir dire may it be admitted into evidence. The trier of fact (jury or judge) does not determine voluntariness.
caseRef: R. v. Oickle, 2000 SCC 38; R. v. Ibrahim, [1914] AC 599; Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | Voir Dire — Confessions Rule

text:
During a voir dire, the Crown proves that police told the accused: 'If you give us a statement, we'll put in a good word with the Crown attorney about your cooperation.' The accused then made a detailed inculpatory statement. The trial judge must decide whether the statement is admissible.

Under the Oickle confessions rule, what is the legal effect of this police promise on the admissibility of the accused's statement?
opts: The statement is automatically inadmissible because any promise by police renders a statement involuntary as a matter of law.
The statement is admissible because the police promise related only to cooperation, not to the sentence itself.
The statement is admissible unless the accused can show the promise actually caused the confession.

The promise is a relevant factor that may render the statement inadmissible if, viewed objectively, it was sufficient to raise a reasonable doubt about voluntariness — the Crown must disprove the coercive effect beyond a reasonable doubt.
expl: Under R. v. Oickle, 2000 SCC 38, no single factor automatically renders a statement involuntary. A promise or inducement — such as promising to 'put in a good word' — is a significant factor but not an automatic bar to admissibility. The trial judge must assess all the circumstances: the nature of the promise, the accused's vulnerability, the duration and conditions of detention, and the overall atmosphere of the interrogation. The Crown bears the burden of proving voluntariness beyond a reasonable doubt. If, considering all the circumstances, a reasonable doubt arises as to whether the accused's will to remain silent was overborne by the promise, the statement must be excluded. The test is not automatic exclusion for any promise, but contextual assessment of whether the promise overbore the accused's free will.
caseRef: R. v. Oickle, 2000 SCC 38; R. v. Spencer, 2007 SCC 11
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | Confessions Rule — Oickle Factors

text:
The accused made a statement to an undercover police officer who was posing as a fellow prisoner in a cellblock. The officer did not identify himself as police. The statement was made freely and without any threats or promises. At trial, the Crown seeks to admit the statement.

Is the confessions rule (voluntariness) applicable to the accused's statement made to the undercover officer?
opts: Yes — the confessions rule applies to all statements made by an accused while in custody, regardless of who the recipient is.

No — the confessions rule only applies to statements made to known peace officers; statements to undercover agents are governed by the s. 7 Charter right against self-incrimination under R. v. Hebert.
Yes — the use of undercover agents in cellblocks is never permissible under the Charter regardless of what was said.
No — the accused's statement to the undercover officer is always admissible because the accused was not detained at the time.
expl: The confessions rule (R. v. Oickle) applies to statements made to a 'person in authority' — someone the accused reasonably believes has authority over them or can influence their prosecution, such as a known police officer or Crown attorney. An undercover police officer posing as a fellow prisoner is not subjectively perceived by the accused as a person in authority, so the common-law confessions rule does not apply. However, the statement is subject to scrutiny under s.7 of the Charter and the right to silence developed in R. v. Hebert, [1990] 2 SCR 151. Under Hebert, police may not use active elicitation by undercover operatives to deliberately undermine the accused's right to remain silent. If the undercover officer merely listened without actively questioning or eliciting, the statement may be admissible; if the officer actively elicited the statement, it may be excluded under s.7.
caseRef: R. v. Oickle, 2000 SCC 38; R. v. Hebert, [1990] 2 SCR 151; R. v. Liew, [1999] 3 SCR 227 (person in authority)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | Confessions Rule — Person in Authority; Undercover Operations

text:
The accused is charged with fraud. The Crown seeks to introduce evidence that the accused committed three similar frauds five years ago for which he was convicted. The Crown argues the evidence is relevant to show a 'system' or 'design' and to rebut the accused's claim that the current alleged fraud was an innocent mistake.

Under the similar fact evidence rule (R. v. Handy, 2002 SCC 56), the trial judge must determine whether to admit this evidence. Which of the following correctly states the admissibility test?
opts: Similar fact evidence is automatically admissible when the accused has prior convictions for the same type of offence.
Similar fact evidence is admissible only if the accused takes the stand and puts their character in issue.
The trial judge must weigh the probative value of the evidence against its prejudicial effect; it is admissible only if the probative value substantially outweighs the prejudicial effect.
Similar fact evidence is always inadmissible in fraud cases because of the risk of moral reasoning (propensity reasoning).
expl: In R. v. Handy, 2002 SCC 56, the Supreme Court of Canada set out the framework for similar fact evidence. The general rule is that evidence of prior bad acts is inadmissible if its only relevance is to show disposition or propensity ('this person did it before; therefore they did it again'). However, similar fact evidence is admissible if its probative value substantially outweighs its prejudicial effect. Probative value includes the strength of the inference the evidence supports beyond mere propensity, such as proving system, design, identity, or rebutting accident/mistake. Prejudicial effect includes the risk that the jury will reason from general bad character rather than the specific evidence. The judge conducts this balancing on a voir dire and admits the evidence only where probative value substantially outweighs prejudice.
caseRef: R. v. Handy, 2002 SCC 56; R. v. Makin, [1894] AC 57
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Similar Fact Evidence

text:
At trial for assault, the Crown seeks to call a witness to testify that the victim told her, one hour after the alleged assault, 'He hit me in the face.' The victim is deceased and unavailable to testify. Defence objects that the statement is hearsay.

Under the principled approach to hearsay (R. v. Khan), on what basis may the trial judge admit this out-of-court statement?
opts: The statement is automatically admissible because the victim is unavailable to testify.
The statement may be admitted under the principled approach if the Crown demonstrates that it meets the twin criteria of necessity and threshold reliability.
The statement is admissible only if it falls within a recognized traditional hearsay exception such as dying declarations.
The statement must be excluded because the accused has the right under s. 7 of the Charter to confront all witnesses who give evidence against them.
expl: Under the principled approach to hearsay developed in R. v. Khan, [1990] 2 SCR 531 and refined in R. v. Starr, 2000 SCC 40, out-of-court statements that do not fall within a traditional exception may nonetheless be admitted if the party seeking admission establishes: (1) necessity — the declarant is unavailable or it would be reasonably impractical to call them, and (2) threshold reliability — sufficient guarantees of reliability to justify admission without cross-examination (either circumstantial reliability or procedural reliability). The unavailability of the victim satisfies necessity. The court must then assess threshold reliability based on the circumstances of the statement.
caseRef: R. v. Khan, [1990] 2 SCR 531; R. v. Starr, 2000 SCC 40; R. v. Khelawon, 2006 SCC 57
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Hearsay — Principled Approach

text:
The Crown calls a key witness who, at trial, gives testimony that contradicts a statement he gave police six months earlier. The Crown wishes to use the earlier police statement to challenge the witness's credibility and potentially to prove the truth of its contents.

Under R. v. K.G.B., [1993] 1 SCR 740, under what circumstances may the prior inconsistent statement be used as substantive evidence (i.e., for the truth of its contents)?
opts: Never — prior inconsistent statements can only be used to impeach credibility, never for the truth of their contents.
Only if the witness adopts the prior statement as true at trial.
If the prior statement was made under oath or solemn affirmation and was video-recorded or otherwise memorialized, and the witness has the opportunity to explain the inconsistency.
If the trial judge finds, on a balance of probabilities, that the prior statement is more reliable than the in-court testimony.
expl: In R. v. K.G.B., [1993] 1 SCR 740, the Supreme Court of Canada held that a prior inconsistent statement may be admitted as substantive evidence — for the truth of its contents — under the principled approach to hearsay if two conditions are met: (1) the statement was made under oath or solemn affirmation (or the equivalent), and (2) the statement was video-recorded or there is a reliable record of it; AND the witness has a meaningful opportunity to explain or deny the inconsistency in cross-examination. These requirements are functionally equivalent to the oath, observation by the trier of fact, and the ability to cross-examine — the traditional guarantees of reliability that are absent from simple out-of-court statements. Where these criteria are met, the statement may go to the truth of its contents, not just to impeach credibility.
caseRef: R. v. K.G.B., [1993] 1 SCR 740; Canada Evidence Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-5, s. 9-11
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Prior Inconsistent Statements — K.G.B.

text:
At trial for robbery, the Crown calls the complainant. The complainant now testifies that he is 'not sure' who robbed him, contradicting a detailed statement he gave police four days after the robbery, which was neither made under oath nor video-recorded. The Crown applies under s. 9(2) of the Canada Evidence Act to cross-examine its own witness on the prior inconsistent statement.

If the trial judge permits cross-examination under s. 9(2), to what use may the prior inconsistent statement be put?
opts: It may be used as substantive evidence of the truth of its contents, because s. 9(2) operates as a K.G.B. exception.
It may be used only to impeach the credibility of the Crown's own witness, not for the truth of its contents.
It may be used for both credibility and substantive purposes if the trial judge finds it more reliable than the in-court testimony.
It may not be used at all once the witness recants.
expl: Section 9(2) of the Canada Evidence Act permits the Crown (or any party) to cross-examine its own witness on a prior inconsistent written statement or one proved to have been made, after obtaining leave of the court and establishing the inconsistency. However, a prior inconsistent statement used under s. 9(2) does not automatically become substantive evidence (admitted for the truth of its contents). It may be used only to impeach credibility — to demonstrate that the witness gave a prior inconsistent account and therefore to diminish the reliability of their current testimony. To be used substantively, the statement would need to meet the K.G.B. principled approach criteria (oath + record), which this statement does not, since it was not made under oath or video-recorded.
caseRef: Canada Evidence Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-5, s. 9(2); R. v. K.G.B., [1993] 1 SCR 740; R. v. Milgaard, [1971] 2 CCC (2d) 206
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Prior Inconsistent Statements — s. 9(2) Canada Evidence Act

text:
In a sexual assault trial, the accused brings a s. 276 application to admit evidence that physical injuries to the complainant's body were caused by her other consensual sexual activity with a third party occurring hours before the alleged assault. The accused argues the evidence directly rebuts the Crown's use of the injuries as proof of non-consensual force.

Which s. 276(2) criterion is MOST important to the admissibility of this specific prior sexual activity evidence?
opts: That the prior sexual activity involved the same accused.
That the evidence has significant probative value that is not substantially outweighed by the danger of prejudice to the proper administration of justice.
That the complainant consented to disclosure of the prior sexual activity.
That the evidence is corroborated by independent witnesses.
expl: Section 276(2)(c) requires that the evidence have 'significant probative value that is not substantially outweighed by the danger of prejudice to the proper administration of justice.' On these facts, where the prior sexual activity may directly explain physical evidence used by the Crown as proof of non-consensual force, the probative value of explaining the injuries is highly significant. This is an alternative explanation for physical evidence — not a twin myths argument — and may satisfy s. 276(2)(b) (relevance to an issue at trial) and s. 276(2)(c) (probative value). The balancing of significant probative value against danger of prejudice is the critical criterion in this context, as the defence is not seeking to attack credibility but to rebut specific physical evidence.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 276(2)(b), s. 276(2)(c); R. v. Darrach, 2000 SCC 46
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Prior Sexual Activity — s. 276(2) Criteria

text:
The accused, acting as a getaway driver for an armed robbery, claims he participated only because his co-accused threatened to kill his family if he refused. He was present at the scene. The accused seeks to rely on the statutory defence of duress under s. 17 of the Criminal Code.

Which of the following correctly describes a key limitation on the s. 17 statutory duress defence as it applies to these facts?
opts: Section 17 duress is unavailable because the accused was not threatened with immediate death or grievous bodily harm to himself personally.
Section 17 duress is unavailable to a person who is a party to the offence alongside the person who made the threat.
Section 17 duress is unavailable because armed robbery is listed as one of the excluded offences for which duress is no defence.
Section 17 duress is unavailable because the threat was to a third party (the accused's family) and not to the accused himself.
expl: Section 17 of the Criminal Code provides a statutory duress defence for a person who commits an offence under compulsion by threats of immediate death or grievous bodily harm. However, s. 17 contains an explicit list of excluded offences for which duress is no defence, regardless of the compulsion. Robbery is one of the excluded offences. Because the accused is charged as a party to an armed robbery, s. 17 duress is simply unavailable on the face of the statute. Note also that s. 17 as written requires the threat to be immediate and to apply to the accused or their spouse — threats to other family members may not qualify. The accused may potentially rely on the common-law defence of duress (which is broader than s.17), since s.17 only applies to the principal offender and common-law duress may apply to parties.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 17; R. v. Ruzic, 2001 SCC 24 (common-law duress)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8: Criminal Defences | Duress — s. 17 Excluded Offences

text:
The accused is charged with robbery. During pretrial proceedings, the accused's counsel raises concerns about whether the accused understands the proceedings. The court must assess fitness to stand trial.

Under s. 2 of the Criminal Code, which of the following is the correct definition of 'unfit to stand trial'?
opts: The accused is incapable of understanding the nature and consequences of the criminal proceedings against them due to mental disorder.
The accused is mentally ill and a danger to themselves or others if they attend court.

The accused is unable to understand the nature or object of the proceedings, or to understand the possible consequences of the proceedings, or to communicate with counsel because of a mental disorder.
The accused lacks the general intelligence required to participate meaningfully in their trial.
expl: Section 2 of the Criminal Code defines 'unfit to stand trial' as unable on account of mental disorder to conduct a defence at any stage of the proceedings before a verdict is rendered, specifically because of inability to: (a) understand the nature or object of the proceedings, (b) understand the possible consequences of the proceedings, or (c) communicate with counsel. All three limbs are in the alternative (inability to satisfy any one is sufficient). The definition focuses on the accused's capacity to participate in their own defence and understand the proceedings, not on dangerousness or general intelligence. The Pritchard criteria from English law have been incorporated into this Canadian statutory definition.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 2 (definition of 'unfit to stand trial'), s. 672.22-672.26; R. v. Taylor (1992), 77 CCC (3d) 551 (Ont. CA)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8: Criminal Defences | Fitness to Stand Trial — s. 2 Definition

text:
The accused is charged with assault causing bodily harm, which is a general intent offence. He was extremely drunk at the time of the offence. Defence counsel wishes to rely on the accused's intoxication as a defence.

In the context of this general intent offence, which of the following correctly states the availability of intoxication as a defence?
opts: Intoxication is a complete defence to general intent offences if the accused was so drunk he did not form the minimal intent required.
Intoxication is not available as a defence to general intent offences, except in cases of extreme intoxication akin to automatism.
Intoxication is always a complete defence if the accused's blood alcohol concentration exceeded the legal limit for driving.
Intoxication reduces the charge from assault causing bodily harm to common assault automatically.
expl: Canadian law draws a distinction between specific intent offences (where intoxication may be raised to negate the specific intent) and general intent offences (where intoxication is generally not available as a defence). For general intent offences, the required mental element (basic intent to commit the act) is so minimal that voluntary intoxication cannot negate it — the accused's act of drinking provides a substitute form of fault (Beard rules as adopted in Canada). However, following R. v. Daviault, [1994] 3 SCR 63, a very narrow exception exists: intoxication akin to automatism (extreme intoxication causing a complete loss of voluntariness) may be a defence to a general intent offence. This exception is extremely narrow and Parliament responded with s. 33.1 of the Criminal Code, which eliminates the Daviault defence for offences involving interference with the bodily integrity of another person.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 33.1; R. v. Daviault, [1994] 3 SCR 63; DPP v. Beard, [1920] AC 479
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8: Criminal Defences | Intoxication — General vs Specific Intent

text:
The accused is charged with murder (a specific intent offence). He was heavily intoxicated at the time of the killing. Defence counsel argues that his intoxication prevented him from forming the specific intent to kill (s. 229(a)(i) — intent to cause death).

If the jury accepts that the accused's intoxication prevented him from forming the specific intent to cause death, what is the proper verdict?
opts: Not guilty — the accused lacked the mens rea for murder and must be acquitted outright.
Guilty of manslaughter — if the accused lacked the specific intent for murder due to intoxication, the jury may convict of the included general intent offence of manslaughter.
Not criminally responsible by reason of mental disorder — intoxication is treated as a mental disorder in Canadian law.
The jury must acquit on murder and the Crown must re-charge the accused with manslaughter in separate proceedings.
expl: For specific intent offences, voluntary intoxication may negate the specific intent required. However, this does not result in an outright acquittal because the specific intent offence (murder) contains a general intent included offence (manslaughter — unlawful killing without the specific intent for murder). If the jury finds that intoxication prevented the formation of the specific intent to kill, they may (and generally should, if the actus reus is proven) convict of the included offence of manslaughter. Section 662(3) of the Criminal Code provides that if an accused charged with murder is found not guilty of murder but the jury is satisfied the offence of manslaughter was committed, they shall find the accused guilty of manslaughter.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 662(3), s. 229(a); R. v. George, [1960] SCR 871; DPP v. Beard, [1920] AC 479
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8: Criminal Defences | Intoxication — Murder and Included Offences

text:
At trial, the accused raises the NCR defence. The judge must instruct the jury on who bears the burden of proving the mental disorder defence.

Under s. 16(3) of the Criminal Code, who bears the burden of proving the NCR defence, and to what standard?
opts: The Crown bears the burden of disproving the NCR defence beyond a reasonable doubt, once the accused raises an air of reality.
The party who raises the defence of mental disorder bears the burden of proving it on a balance of probabilities.
The accused bears the burden of proving the NCR defence beyond a reasonable doubt.
The Crown bears the burden of proving the accused did not suffer from a mental disorder, on a balance of probabilities.
expl: Section 16(3) of the Criminal Code provides that the burden of proof that a person was, by reason of mental disorder, exempt from criminal responsibility is on the party who raises that issue — and that the standard is balance of probabilities, not beyond a reasonable doubt. This is an express statutory exception to the general rule in criminal law that the accused need only raise an air of reality for a defence. The Supreme Court upheld the constitutionality of this provision in R. v. Chaulk, finding it justified under s.1 of the Charter. Critically, either the accused or the Crown may raise the NCR defence (s.16(4)), and whichever party raises it bears the balance of probabilities burden.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 16(3), s. 16(4); R. v. Chaulk, [1990] 3 SCR 1303
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8: Criminal Defences | NCR Defence — Burden of Proof

text:
During a bar fight, the accused struck the complainant with his fist, breaking the complainant's jaw. The accused testifies he believed the complainant was about to headbutt him and struck first. He was not the initial aggressor. The trial judge must determine whether the accused's act of self-defence was justified under s. 34.

Under s. 34(1) of the Criminal Code, which of the following are the three essential elements the accused must establish to raise self-defence?
opts: The accused was not the initial aggressor; the accused believed their life was in danger; the force used was proportionate.

The accused believed on reasonable grounds that force was being used or threatened against them; the act was committed for the purpose of defending themselves; and the act was reasonable in the circumstances.
The complainant struck first; the accused believed they would be seriously harmed; the accused used the minimum force necessary.
The accused had no other option but to use force; the accused acted immediately and without premeditation; the force was not excessive.
expl: Section 34(1) of the Criminal Code (as amended in 2012) provides that a person is not criminally responsible for an act committed in defence of themselves or another if: (a) they believe on reasonable grounds that force is being used or threatened against them or another; (b) the act that constitutes the offence is committed for the purpose of defending or protecting themselves or the other person from that use or threat of force; and (c) the act committed is reasonable in the circumstances. Section 34(2) lists non-exhaustive factors for assessing reasonableness. The prior requirement of imminence and the initial-aggressor bar have been replaced by the broader 'reasonable in the circumstances' framework.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 34(1), s. 34(2); R. v. Lavallee, [1990] 1 SCR 852
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8: Criminal Defences | Self-Defence — s. 34 (2012 amendment)

text:
The accused is convicted of fraud over $5,000. The sentencing judge is considering whether to impose a conditional sentence of imprisonment (house arrest). The accused has no prior record and has expressed genuine remorse.

Under s. 742.1 of the Criminal Code, which of the following is a threshold requirement that MUST be met before a conditional sentence of imprisonment can be imposed?
opts: The offence must be a summary conviction offence.
The accused must consent to serving the sentence in the community.

The sentence imposed must be less than two years, the offence must not carry a minimum term of imprisonment, and the court must be satisfied that serving the sentence in the community is consistent with the fundamental purpose and principles of sentencing.
The sentencing judge must first impose a penitentiary sentence and then suspend it on conditions.
expl: Section 742.1 of the Criminal Code sets out the conditions for a conditional sentence: (1) the court imposes a sentence of imprisonment of less than two years; (2) the offence is not one for which a minimum term of imprisonment is prescribed; (3) the offence does not fall within the excluded categories added by the Safe Streets and Communities Act (e.g., s.742.1(c): no CSO if offence resulted in bodily harm, s.742.1(d) terrorism, etc.); and (4) the court is satisfied that serving the sentence in the community would not endanger the safety of the community and would be consistent with the fundamental purpose and principles of sentencing. The sentence length threshold (under two years), the absence of a mandatory minimum, and the community safety/consistency finding are all prerequisites.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 742.1; Safe Streets and Communities Act, S.C. 2012, c. 1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Conditional Sentence of Imprisonment — s. 742.1

text:
The accused pleads guilty to assault. At the sentencing hearing, the Crown seeks to rely on facts that aggravate the sentence (that the assault was premeditated). The accused disputes this characterization, asserting the assault was spontaneous. The sentencing judge must resolve the factual dispute.

Under R. v. Gardiner, [1982] 2 SCR 368, what standard of proof applies when the Crown seeks to prove aggravating facts at a sentencing hearing that the offender disputes?
opts: Balance of probabilities — the lesser standard applies because the conviction has already been entered.
Beyond a reasonable doubt — the criminal standard applies to all disputed facts at sentencing that the Crown relies on to aggravate the sentence.
Clear and convincing evidence — an intermediate standard applies at the sentencing stage.
The sentencing judge may find any disputed fact as aggravating without evidence, relying on pre-sentence report summaries.
expl: In R. v. Gardiner, [1982] 2 SCR 368, the Supreme Court of Canada held that when the Crown seeks to rely on disputed facts to aggravate a sentence, those facts must be proved beyond a reasonable doubt — the criminal standard. The Court reasoned that sentence is part of the criminal process, and the accused's right to liberty is directly at stake. Where facts are aggravating and disputed, the Crown bears the burden of proving them beyond a reasonable doubt. However, mitigating facts put forward by the defence (in the accused's favour) need only be established on a balance of probabilities. This asymmetric burden reflects the liberty interest at stake and the presumption of innocence.
caseRef: R. v. Gardiner, [1982] 2 SCR 368; Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 724
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Disputed Facts at Sentencing — Gardiner

text:
Prior to sentencing the accused for robbery, the judge orders a pre-sentence report (PSR). The PSR is completed by a probation officer who interviews the accused, family members, and employers. The PSR contains information from a social worker who describes the accused's troubled childhood but is not called to testify at the sentencing hearing.

Regarding the contents and use of the pre-sentence report, which of the following correctly states the applicable law?
opts: The information in the PSR is subject to the ordinary rules of evidence; hearsay information is inadmissible.
The sentencing judge may consider the information in the PSR, including hearsay information, as part of the sentencing record; either party may cross-examine the author of the PSR.
The PSR binds the sentencing judge to the sentence recommended by the probation officer.
The PSR is exclusively for the judge's use; defence counsel does not have a right to see or respond to its contents.
expl: Under s. 723-724 of the Criminal Code and general sentencing principles, the pre-sentence report is received by the court as part of the sentencing record. The ordinary rules of evidence regarding hearsay do not apply with full rigour at sentencing; the judge may consider hearsay and other information in the PSR that would not be admissible at trial. However, either party has the right to cross-examine the person who prepared the report on its contents (s. 723(3)). The sentencing judge is not bound by any recommendation in the PSR and exercises independent discretion in imposing sentence. Both parties have the right to see and respond to the PSR (s. 722.1).
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 721-724
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Pre-Sentence Report

text:
A judge is sentencing an offender for a first offence of fraud over $5,000. Counsel on both sides make submissions on the applicable principles. The judge must apply the objectives of sentencing set out in the Criminal Code.

Under s. 718 of the Criminal Code, which of the following is NOT a recognized fundamental purpose or objective of sentencing?
opts: To denounce unlawful conduct and the harm done to victims or to the community.
To deter the offender and other persons from committing offences.
To guarantee that the victim receives financial compensation from the offender.
To separate offenders from society, where necessary.
expl: Section 718 of the Criminal Code sets out the fundamental purposes of sentencing as: (a) denouncing unlawful conduct; (b) deterring the offender and others; (c) separating offenders from society where necessary; (d) assisting in rehabilitating offenders; (e) providing reparations for harm done to victims or the community; and (f) promoting a sense of responsibility in offenders. While s.718.2(e.1) allows victim surcharge orders and s.738 allows restitution orders, a 'guarantee' of financial compensation is not a purpose of sentencing under s.718. Victim compensation through restitution orders is a tool, not a guaranteed purpose.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 718, s. 718.1, s. 738
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Purposes of Sentencing — s. 718

text:
A 17-year-old is charged with first degree murder. The Crown applies for an adult sentence under the YCJA, arguing the youth sentence would not be sufficient to hold the accused accountable.

Under the YCJA, which party bears the burden of proof on an adult sentence application, and what is the test the court applies?
opts: The Crown bears the burden of proving that a youth sentence would not be adequate to hold the young person accountable, on a balance of probabilities.
The young person bears the burden of proving that a youth sentence is adequate, on a balance of probabilities.
The burden shifts to the young person once the Crown establishes a prima facie case for adult sentencing.
There is no burden of proof — the court makes the determination based on the pre-sentence report.
expl: Under s. 72(1) of the YCJA (as amended), the youth justice court shall order that an adult sentence be imposed if it is satisfied that: (a) the presumption of diminished moral blameworthiness or culpability is rebutted; and (b) a youth sentence imposed under s.42 would not be of sufficient length to hold the young person accountable for their offending behaviour. The burden of proof on an adult sentence application falls on the Crown, who must prove, on a balance of probabilities, that a youth sentence would be inadequate. This reflects the YCJA's presumption that youth sentences are generally appropriate and that adult sentences are the exception.
caseRef: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 64, s. 72(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10: Youth Criminal Justice | Adult Sentence Application — YCJA s. 64, s. 72

text:
A 17-year-old is arrested and charged with theft over $5,000. The question arises whether the Youth Criminal Justice Act applies to this accused.

Under s. 2(1) of the Youth Criminal Justice Act, who qualifies as a 'young person' subject to the Act?
opts: A person who is 12 years of age or older but under 16 years of age at the time the alleged offence was committed.
A person who is 12 years of age or older but under 18 years of age at the time the alleged offence was committed.
A person who is 14 years of age or older but under 18 years of age at the time the alleged offence was committed.
A person who is under 18 years of age at the time of sentencing, regardless of age at the time of the offence.
expl: Section 2(1) of the Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1 defines 'young person' as a person who is or was, while being proceeded against under the Act, twelve years of age or older but under eighteen years of age. The Act applies based on age at the time the alleged offence was committed, not at the time of trial or sentencing. The minimum age of criminal responsibility in Canada is 12 years — a child under 12 cannot be charged with a criminal offence. A 17-year-old at the time of the offence is a 'young person' within the YCJA.
caseRef: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 2(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10: Youth Criminal Justice | Definition of Young Person — YCJA s. 2(1)

text:
A 14-year-old is caught shoplifting for the first time. The value of the stolen goods was $30. The investigating police officer is considering how to handle the matter without a formal charge.

Under the YCJA, which of the following best describes the framework for extra-judicial measures that a police officer may use in this situation?
opts: A police officer has no authority to deal with a young person without laying a formal charge; all youth matters must proceed to court.
Extra-judicial measures are available only if the young person has no prior criminal record.
Extra-judicial measures — such as warnings, cautions, and referrals to programs — are available and are preferred over charges for first-time and minor offenders; a charge should be a last resort.
Extra-judicial measures require the consent of the victim before they can be used.
expl: Sections 4-12 of the YCJA establish a robust framework of extra-judicial measures (EJMs) as the preferred response for young persons, particularly first-time and minor offenders. Section 4 states that extra-judicial measures are often the most appropriate and effective way to address young persons' offending behaviour without formal court proceedings. EJMs include police warnings, police cautions, Crown cautions, referrals to community programs, and extrajudicial sanctions (EJS) for more serious cases. Section 6 emphasizes that police should first consider whether an EJM is sufficient. The YCJA philosophy is that a charge should be a last resort, reserved for cases where EJMs are inadequate.
caseRef: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 4-12
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10: Youth Criminal Justice | Extra-Judicial Measures — YCJA s. 4-12

text:
A 16-year-old is convicted of robbery under the YCJA. The sentencing judge is deciding among available youth sentences. Crown counsel submits that the appropriate sanction is a custodial youth sentence.

Under s. 39(1) of the YCJA, which of the following is the threshold requirement before a youth court may impose a custodial sentence?
opts: The young person must have a prior record of at least one previous youth sentence.
The offence must be a designated violent offence as listed in the schedule to the YCJA.

The court must be satisfied that the offence is serious enough, the young person has a history that indicates a pattern of offending, or the exceptional circumstances of the case warrant custody; non-custodial sentences must have been considered inadequate.
The court requires the written consent of the youth's parents before imposing custody.
expl: Section 39(1) of the YCJA restricts custodial sentences for young persons. A youth court shall not commit a young person to custody unless one of the following conditions is met: (a) the young person has committed a violent offence; (b) the young person has failed to comply with non-custodial sentences; (c) the young person has committed an indictable offence for which an adult would be liable to imprisonment for more than two years and has a history indicating a pattern of extrajudicial sanctions or non-custodial sentences; or (d) in exceptional cases where the young person has committed an indictable offence and the aggravating circumstances of the offence justify custody. Non-custodial sentences must have been considered and found inadequate before custody may be ordered (s.39(2)).
caseRef: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 39(1), s. 39(2), s. 42
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10: Youth Criminal Justice | Youth Custodial Sentence Threshold — YCJA s. 39

text:
A 15-year-old suspect is taken to the police station for questioning regarding an armed robbery. Before questioning begins, police explain the caution and the right to retain and instruct counsel. The youth says he understands but declines a lawyer. He then makes an inculpatory statement.

Under s. 146 of the YCJA, what additional step was required before police could take this young person's statement?
opts: Police must have a parent or adult relative present during questioning, and if no parent is available, a responsible adult must be contacted.
No additional steps were required beyond the standard police caution and s. 10(b) Charter right to counsel.
Police must have obtained written consent from a parent or guardian before questioning a young person.
Police must advise the young person of the right to have a parent or other adult person present during questioning, and give the young person a reasonable opportunity to consult with that person.
expl: Section 146(2) of the YCJA establishes specific requirements for a young person's statement to be admissible. Before the statement is made, the person who took the statement must have clearly explained to the young person, in language appropriate to their age and understanding: (a) the nature of the offence and the circumstances giving rise to it, (b) the right to retain and instruct counsel, (c) the right to have a parent or other appropriate person present, and (d) that the statement may be used as evidence. Section 146(2)(b) requires that the young person be given a reasonable opportunity to consult with counsel AND with a parent or other appropriate adult before making the statement. The failure to advise of and provide this opportunity is a basis to exclude the statement.
caseRef: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 146(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10: Youth Criminal Justice | Statement of Young Person — YCJA s. 146

text:
The accused was convicted of fraud at trial. On appeal, he seeks to introduce new affidavit evidence from a forensic accountant who was retained after the trial. The forensic accountant's opinion contradicts the Crown's expert evidence at trial. The accused did not call an expert accountant at trial even though defence counsel was aware such evidence might be helpful.

Under the Palmer test for fresh evidence on criminal appeals, which requirement is MOST likely to defeat the accused's application to introduce this evidence?
opts: The evidence must be credible in the sense that it is reasonably capable of belief.
The evidence must be relevant and bear on a decisive or potentially decisive issue.
The evidence could not have been obtained with reasonable diligence at trial — the due diligence requirement.
The evidence must, if believed, have affected the verdict.
expl: In Palmer v. The Queen, [1980] 1 SCR 759, the Supreme Court set out the four-part test for admitting fresh evidence on appeal: (1) the evidence could not, by the exercise of reasonable diligence, have been obtained for use at trial (due diligence); (2) the evidence must be relevant in the sense that it bears on a decisive or potentially decisive issue; (3) the evidence must be credible in the sense that it is reasonably capable of belief; and (4) the evidence, if believed, could reasonably be expected to have affected the result. On these facts, the due diligence requirement is the most problematic criterion. Defence counsel was aware of the potential need for a forensic accountant but did not retain one. The failure to call available expert evidence at trial is a strategic choice that the accused cannot revisit on appeal under the guise of fresh evidence.
caseRef: Palmer v. The Queen, [1980] 1 SCR 759; Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 683(1)(d)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11: Appeals | Fresh Evidence on Appeal — Palmer Test

text:
The accused is convicted of a summary conviction offence in the Ontario Court of Justice. He wishes to appeal the conviction on the basis that the trial judge misapplied the law.

Under s. 813 of the Criminal Code, to which court does the accused appeal from a summary conviction?
opts: The Court of Appeal for Ontario.
The Superior Court of Justice (as a summary conviction appeal court).
The Supreme Court of Canada.
The Divisional Court, which has exclusive jurisdiction over all criminal appeals.
expl: Section 813 of the Criminal Code provides that the defendant or the informant may appeal to the summary conviction appeal court against a conviction, acquittal, or sentence. In Ontario, the summary conviction appeal court is the Superior Court of Justice (pursuant to the provincial designation). The appeal from a summary conviction goes first to the Superior Court of Justice, not to the Court of Appeal. A further appeal on a question of law lies to the Court of Appeal for Ontario with leave, under s. 839 of the Criminal Code.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 813, s. 839
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11: Appeals | Summary Conviction Appeals — s. 813

text:
Karen and Paul are divorcing after a 10-year marriage with two children. In addition to the divorce itself, Karen seeks orders for parenting arrangements, child support, and spousal support within the divorce proceedings. Paul argues that property division cannot be addressed in the divorce proceedings and must be brought in a separate application under the Family Law Act.

Which of the following accurately describes the corollary relief available in divorce proceedings under the Divorce Act?
