opts: In divorce proceedings, the court may grant only the divorce itself; all ancillary matters such as support and parenting must be dealt with in separate Family Law Act proceedings.

The Divorce Act permits the court to make corollary orders for parenting arrangements, child support, and spousal support in the same proceedings as the divorce; property division under the Family Law Act is not available as corollary relief in federal divorce proceedings and must be pursued separately under provincial legislation.
The Divorce Act permits the court to make orders for all matters including property division, spousal support, child support, and parenting arrangements in a single proceeding.
Corollary relief is only available after the divorce has been granted; the parties must wait until the divorce is final before applying for support or parenting orders.
expl: Under Divorce Act ss.15.1 (child support), 15.2 (spousal support), and 16 (parenting orders), a court may make corollary relief orders in conjunction with a divorce application. The Divorce Act does not confer jurisdiction over property division, which remains a matter of provincial law (Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part I). Paul is correct that property equalization must be pursued under the FLA in a separate or concurrent provincial proceeding, not as corollary relief under the federal Divorce Act.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), ss. 15.1, 15.2, 16; Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part I
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Corollary Relief — DA ss.15.1, 15.2, 16

text:
Gina and her husband Robert have agreed to allege adultery by Robert so that their divorce can be granted immediately without waiting the one-year separation period. Robert has not actually committed adultery, but both parties fabricate the allegation. The court is unaware of the arrangement.

What legal doctrine bars the divorce, and what is the court's obligation under the Divorce Act?
opts: Condonation — because Gina has forgiven Robert's alleged adultery by continuing to live with him.
Connivance — because Gina has actively encouraged the alleged adulterous conduct.

Collusion — because the parties have agreed to fabricate or exaggerate the ground of divorce; under DA s.11(1)(b), the court must dismiss or stay the divorce petition if it finds collusion, and under DA s.11(1)(a), the court has a duty to satisfy itself that collusion has not occurred.
There is no bar; the parties' private agreement about how to plead the divorce cannot be reviewed by the court once proceedings are commenced.
expl: Collusion under DA s.11(1)(b) is defined as an agreement, conspiracy, or arrangement to fabricate or suppress evidence or to deceive the court in relation to the divorce proceeding. Fabricating an adultery allegation to avoid the one-year separation period is a classic example of collusion. Under DA s.11(1)(a), the court has a duty to satisfy itself that there has been no collusion. If collusion is found, the court must (s.11(3)) stay or dismiss the divorce application. Collusion is an absolute bar — unlike condonation and connivance, the court may not exercise discretion to proceed despite collusion.
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Bars to Divorce — DA s.11

text:
Natalie discovers her husband Adrian is having an affair. She wishes to obtain a divorce immediately without waiting one year. Her lawyer advises that adultery is a recognized ground for divorce that does not require a waiting period. Adrian has not admitted to the affair.

Which statement accurately describes the adultery ground for divorce under DA s.8(2)(b)?
opts: Adultery is no longer a ground for divorce in Canada since the 2020 amendments to the Divorce Act.

Adultery is a ground for divorce under DA s.8(2)(b)(i); Natalie may allege adultery and, if proven, a divorce may be granted without the one-year separation period, but the standard of proof is balance of probabilities and Adrian cannot be compelled to testify against himself.
Adultery is a ground for divorce but requires a criminal conviction for the offence of adultery before it can be pleaded in divorce proceedings.
Adultery is a ground for divorce under the Divorce Act only if the responding spouse admits the affair in writing.
expl: Under Divorce Act s.8(2)(b)(i), adultery by the respondent constitutes marriage breakdown, which is a ground for divorce that does not require the one-year waiting period. The standard of proof is the civil standard — balance of probabilities — and corroborating evidence (direct or circumstantial) may be sufficient. The respondent cannot be compelled to testify against themselves as to adultery (DA s.10(4)). In practice, adultery cases are rarely litigated because the one-year separation ground is more straightforward.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 8(2)(b)(i), s. 10(4)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Grounds for Divorce — Adultery and Cruelty

text:
Marcus and Diana divorced five years ago. The court ordered Marcus to pay Diana $3,000 per month in spousal support indefinitely. Since the order was made, Marcus has been involuntarily laid off and now earns $40,000 per year, down from $150,000. He brings a variation application. Diana argues the original order cannot be varied because the court labeled it 'final.'

On a variation application under Divorce Act s.17, what must Marcus demonstrate to obtain a reduction in his spousal support obligation?
opts: Marcus must demonstrate that he made a bona fide attempt to find equivalent employment and failed, and that his income loss will last forever.

Marcus must show a material change in circumstances — a change that, had it been known at the time of the original order, would likely have resulted in a different order; his involuntary job loss and income reduction from $150,000 to $40,000 would likely satisfy this test.
Marcus cannot vary the order because it was labeled 'final'; only a consent order or an appeal may alter a final support order.
Marcus must show that Diana's needs have decreased, not that his ability to pay has changed.
expl: Under Divorce Act s.17, a court may vary a spousal support order where there has been a 'change in the condition, means, needs or other circumstances' of either spouse since the order was made. The Supreme Court in Leskun v. Leskun, [2006] 1 SCR 920 confirmed that the change must be material — meaning that if it had been known at the time, it would likely have produced a different result. An involuntary loss of employment reducing income from $150,000 to $40,000 is a paradigm material change, regardless of whether the original order was labeled 'final.'
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17; Leskun v. Leskun, [2006] 1 SCR 920
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Spousal Support | Variation of Spousal Support — Material Change in Circumstances

text:
Elena receives spousal support of $2,500 per month under a consent order that is silent on termination events. She has been cohabiting with a new partner for two years in a marriage-like relationship. Her new partner has a high income and contributes substantially to household expenses. Elena's income has not changed. Her former husband applies to terminate support.

Which legal principle is most relevant to the former husband's application to terminate Elena's spousal support?
opts: Cohabitation with a new partner automatically terminates a spousal support order by operation of law in Ontario.
The consent order cannot be varied or terminated because Elena and her former husband agreed to it; the court has no jurisdiction to interfere.

Cohabitation in a marriage-like relationship may constitute a material change in circumstances under DA s.17 if it results in a change in Elena's means or needs; the court considers the economic impact of the new relationship, not the mere fact of cohabitation.
Spousal support is only terminated by remarriage or death; cohabitation has no legal effect on a support order.
expl: Cohabitation with a new partner does not automatically terminate spousal support in Ontario. However, under Divorce Act s.17, cohabitation may constitute a material change in circumstances if it affects the recipient's means or needs. If the new partner contributes significantly to living expenses, reducing Elena's financial need, the court may reduce or terminate support. The inquiry focuses on the economic impact of cohabitation, not cohabitation per se. Under the Family Law Act s.37(2)(c), a support obligation is also affected by a spouse's co-habitation.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17; Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 37(2)(c)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Spousal Support | Termination of Spousal Support — Events After Order

text:
A spousal support order was made two years ago requiring Roy to pay $2,000/month. Roy lost his job eight months ago but delayed bringing his variation application. He now asks the court to reduce his support retroactively to the date of his job loss and to cancel the arrears that have accumulated since that date.

What principles govern a court's discretion to make a retroactive variation of spousal support under the Divorce Act?
opts: Retroactive variation is not available under the Divorce Act; all reductions take effect only from the date of the court order.
The court has discretion to make a retroactive variation and must do so to the date of the triggering event, cancelling all arrears from that date as a matter of course.

The court has discretion under DA s.17 to make a retroactive variation; relevant considerations include the reason for the delay in bringing the application, whether the recipient was given notice of the changed circumstances, prejudice to the recipient, and the conduct of the payor.
Retroactive reduction of spousal support arrears is only possible by consent agreement; a court cannot unilaterally cancel accumulated arrears.
expl: Under Divorce Act s.17(1), a court may vary a support order and the variation may be retroactive. The court exercises discretion in determining the retroactive start date. Relevant factors include: (1) the payor's reason for the delay in bringing the application; (2) whether the recipient had notice of the changed circumstances and was able to adjust their lifestyle; (3) whether granting full retroactivity would cause hardship to the recipient; and (4) the overall fairness of the retroactive award. Courts do not automatically grant retroactivity to the triggering event; the circumstances dictate the appropriate retroactive date.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17(1); Leskun v. Leskun, [2006] 1 SCR 920
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Spousal Support | Retroactive Variation of Spousal Support

text:
A spousal support order was made five years ago awarding $1,800/month to the wife. At the time, the husband earned $100,000 and the wife earned $30,000. Today, following a promotion, the husband earns $180,000 and the wife's income remains $30,000. The wife brings a variation application and asks the court to increase support. Her lawyer argues the court should simply apply the current SSAG range to the new incomes and set support at the midpoint of that range.

When a court varies a spousal support order under DA s.17 due to changed income, how does the SSAG interact with the variation analysis?
opts: On a variation application, the court must recalculate support de novo using the SSAG as if no prior order existed; the original order is irrelevant.
The SSAG cannot be used on variation applications; they apply only to initial orders.

On a variation, once a material change is established, the court may use the SSAG to help determine the appropriate new quantum; however, the SSAG are advisory only and the court retains discretion to depart from the range based on the specific circumstances, including the amount originally ordered and the parties' reliance on it.
On a variation, the court must award the exact SSAG midpoint for the new income figures; judicial discretion is displaced once the SSAG range is calculated.
expl: On a DA s.17 variation application, the court first determines whether there has been a material change in circumstances. If so, it reconsiders quantum, and the SSAG serve as a useful — but non-binding — reference for the appropriate new amount. The SSAG do not displace judicial discretion; the court may consider the original order, the parties' expectations and reliance, and all other relevant circumstances. The court is not required to simply apply the current SSAG midpoint without reference to the history of the support obligation.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17; Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (2008, revised 2021)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Spousal Support | Variation of Spousal Support — SSAG and Material Change

text:
Sophie is 21 years old and enrolled in a four-year university program. Her parents divorced when she was 10. Her father argues that his child support obligation ended when Sophie turned 18 and he is no longer required to pay. Her mother applies for an order of continued child support.

Under the Divorce Act and Federal Child Support Guidelines, can a child support order be made or continued for an adult child attending post-secondary education?
opts: No — child support is available only for children under 18 in Canada; turning 18 terminates all child support obligations automatically.

Yes — under DA s.2(1), the definition of 'child of the marriage' includes a child who is 18 or older and unable by reason of illness, disability, or other cause to withdraw from parental charge; attendance at university is recognized as an 'other cause' preventing withdrawal from parental charge, and Sophie may qualify.
Yes — but only until Sophie turns 21; after age 21, child support for post-secondary students is unavailable.
Yes — but support for adult post-secondary students is capped at $1,000/month regardless of the payor's income.
expl: Divorce Act s.2(1) defines 'child of the marriage' to include a person who is 18 or older and 'is unable, by reason of illness, disability, or other cause, to withdraw from their charge or to obtain the necessaries of life.' Courts have consistently held that full-time post-secondary attendance is an 'other cause' within this definition, preventing a young adult from withdrawing from parental charge. Child support for post-secondary students is calculated differently from table amounts — the court looks at the child's actual expenses, the parent's ability to pay, and any contribution the child can make (FCSG s.3(2)(b)). There is no automatic cutoff at age 21 and no fixed cap.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 2(1); Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 3(2)(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Adult Children — Post-Secondary Education and Child Support

text:
Brian, who has a law degree and five years of litigation experience, voluntarily resigned from his position as an associate earning $130,000/year the day before a child support motion. He claims he is now self-employed doing occasional legal consulting, earning $20,000/year. His former spouse seeks an imputation of income. Brian argues the court must accept his actual income.

Under FCSG s.19, in what circumstances may a court impute income to a child support payor, and would Brian's circumstances warrant imputation?
opts: Courts may only impute income in cases of criminal fraud; resignation from employment, however suspicious the timing, cannot give rise to imputation.
Courts may impute income under s.19 where a spouse is intentionally under-employed or unemployed; however, Brian's right to choose his career path means no imputation is possible.

Under FCSG s.19(1)(a), a court may impute income where a spouse is intentionally under-employed or unemployed, unless the situation is appropriate given the circumstances (such as illness, disability, or reasonable career transition); Brian's sudden voluntary resignation to near-zero income the day before a child support motion, without legitimate justification, strongly supports imputation at a level reflecting his earning capacity.
The court must average Brian's income over the last three years and use that as his income for child support purposes; imputation is not available.
expl: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.19(1)(a) permits a court to impute income to a paying spouse where the spouse is intentionally under-employed or unemployed, unless the situation is reasonable in the circumstances (e.g., disability, illness, or a genuine and reasonable career change). Brian's voluntary resignation the day before a child support motion, combined with his legal qualifications and abrupt shift to $20,000 consulting income, are strong indicators of intentional income reduction for purposes of avoiding child support. Courts regularly impute income based on earning capacity in such circumstances.
caseRef: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 19(1)(a)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Imputing Income — FCSG s.19

text:
Olivia and Steve have one child, aged 7, who lives primarily with Olivia. Steve pays the table amount of child support. Olivia now asks the court to order Steve to contribute to the following additional expenses: (1) daycare costs of $1,200/month while Olivia works; (2) the child's hockey equipment and registration fees of $3,000/year; (3) private school tuition of $15,000/year. Steve argues none of these are s.7 expenses.

Which of these three items is/are properly characterized as special or extraordinary expenses under FCSG s.7?
opts: None — s.7 expenses are limited to medical and dental costs; daycare, sports, and school tuition are not covered.
Daycare costs and private school tuition are s.7 expenses; recreational sports expenses are not s.7 expenses under any circumstances.

All three may qualify as s.7 expenses: daycare (s.7(1)(a) — child care required by employment), private school tuition (s.7(1)(d) — education programs necessary for the child or exceeding what is reasonably expected at the public school level); hockey may qualify as an extraordinary expense under s.7(1)(f) if it is an 'extraordinary' extracurricular activity given the parties' circumstances.
Only private school tuition qualifies because s.7 is limited to educational expenses above the public school level.
expl: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.7(1) lists categories of special or extraordinary expenses: (a) child care required by employment/illness/disability; (b) medical/dental insurance premiums; (c) medical/dental expenses exceeding insurance; (d) education programs (including post-secondary and programs necessary for the child's needs); (e) post-secondary education expenses; (f) extraordinary extracurricular activities. Child care while Olivia works is clearly s.7(1)(a). Private school tuition may qualify under s.7(1)(d). Hockey registration may qualify as an extraordinary extracurricular activity under s.7(1)(f), assessed in the context of the parties' incomes and historical activities. All three potentially qualify; the court applies a proportionality test under s.7(2) based on income.
caseRef: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 7(1)(a), s. 7(1)(d), s. 7(1)(f), s. 7(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Special and Extraordinary Expenses — FCSG s.7

text:
Anna earns $100,000/year and Mark earns $60,000/year. They have one child, and pursuant to a parenting order, the child spends 45% of the time with Mark and 55% with Anna. Under the Ontario table, the table amount for Mark's income of $60,000 for one child is $572/month; for Anna's income of $100,000, it is $879/month. Both parents seek to pay the minimum child support possible.

How does FCSG s.9 govern child support when the child spends at least 40% of the time with each parent?
opts: When a child spends more than 40% of the time with each parent, no child support is payable by either party; the arrangement is presumed financially neutral.
FCSG s.9 creates a mandatory formula requiring the higher-earning parent to pay the lower-earning parent the full table amount.

Under FCSG s.9, where a child spends at least 40% of the time with each parent, the court must consider the table amounts for each parent, the increased cost of the shared arrangement, and the condition, means, needs, and other circumstances of each parent; the offset method (table amount for Anna minus table amount for Mark = $879 - $572 = $307/month payable by Anna) is a common starting point, but the court may adjust up or down.
Section 9 applies only where the parenting time is exactly 50/50; at 55/45 the standard table amount applies.
expl: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.9 applies where a spouse exercises parenting time with a child for not less than 40% of the time over the course of a year. In that case, the court shall take into account: (a) the amounts set out in the applicable tables for each parent; (b) the increased costs of shared parenting; and (c) the condition, means, needs and other circumstances of each spouse and the children. The offset method — comparing the table amounts and paying the difference — is frequently used as a starting point (here, $879 - $572 = $307 payable by Anna), but it is not mandatory and the court retains discretion to depart from it (see Contino v. Leonelli-Contino, [2005] 3 SCR 217).
caseRef: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 9; Contino v. Leonelli-Contino, [2005] 3 SCR 217
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Shared Parenting — FCSG s.9

text:
Daniel and Fiona separated and have two children, aged 8 and 11, who live primarily with Fiona. Daniel earns $90,000 per year gross income in Ontario. There is no shared parenting arrangement. Both parties agree that the Federal Child Support Guidelines apply.

Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines, what is the starting point for determining the amount of child support Daniel must pay?
opts: The court has broad discretion to set any amount based on the children's needs and Daniel's ability to pay, without reference to a table.
Child support is calculated as a fixed percentage of Daniel's gross income regardless of the number of children.

The starting point is the table amount specified in Schedule I of the Federal Child Support Guidelines for a payor residing in Ontario with a gross annual income of $90,000 and two children; this is the presumptive amount and is treated as the floor, not a ceiling.
Child support is set at $500 per child per month as a statutory flat rate for parents earning between $75,000 and $100,000.
expl: Under Federal Child Support Guidelines s.3(1)(a), where a child resides primarily with one parent, the amount of a child support order for that parent is the applicable table amount for the payor's province of residence, based on the payor's annual income and the number of children. The table amounts in Schedule I are the presumptive starting point and are treated as the appropriate amount in the absence of factors justifying departure. There is no fixed flat rate or open-ended judicial discretion to ignore the table amount.
caseRef: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 3(1)(a), Schedule I
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Federal Child Support Guidelines — Table Amount

text:
Victor is self-employed operating a consulting business. His corporation reports pre-tax income of $200,000, but Victor pays himself a salary of $80,000 and reinvests the remaining $120,000 in the corporation. He argues that child support should be based only on his $80,000 salary. His former spouse argues that the court should look beyond the corporate salary.

How is Victor's income determined for purposes of the Federal Child Support Guidelines when he is a self-employed shareholder of a private corporation?
opts: Victor's income is his declared salary of $80,000; the court cannot look behind the corporate structure.
Victor's income is the pre-tax corporate income of $200,000 because the Guidelines always attribute the entire corporate income to the shareholder-payor.

Under FCSG Schedule III and s.18, where a payor controls a private corporation, the court may include in income amounts that the corporation earned but did not distribute as salary, where those amounts are not required for business operations and the retention constitutes an artificial reduction of the payor's income.
Victor's income is determined by averaging his salary over the last five years, ignoring corporate profits entirely.
expl: Under Federal Child Support Guidelines s.18(1), where a paying spouse controls a corporation, the court may determine the spouse's annual income to include the pre-tax income of the corporation, less any amounts that are reasonably required for the operation of the business. This prevents artificial suppression of income by retaining earnings in a corporation. The court does not automatically attribute all corporate income, but will look behind the salary when the retention of earnings is not required for business purposes and results in an artificial understatement of the payor's income.
caseRef: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 18(1), Schedule III
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Determination of Income — FCSG s.18 and Schedule III

text:
A child support order was made two years ago based on the payor's income of $75,000. The payor's income has now increased to $100,000. The recipient applies to vary the order to increase the table amount. The payor argues that the income increase alone is insufficient to constitute a 'material change in circumstances.'

What is the standard for varying a child support order under the Divorce Act, and does an increase in the payor's income satisfy it?
opts: The payor is correct; a change in income alone is never a material change because the Guidelines produce a new table amount automatically without a court order.
Child support orders can be varied only if the child's needs have changed, not based solely on changes to the payor's income.

Under DA s.17(4) and s.17(6.1), a change in the condition, means, needs or other circumstances of either spouse or a child constitutes a material change warranting variation; an increase in the payor's income is itself a change in the payor's 'means' and is a recognized basis for upward variation of the table amount.
The increase from $75,000 to $100,000 is too modest to qualify as material; the income must increase by at least 25% before variation is available.
expl: Under Divorce Act s.17(4), a court may vary a child support order where there has been a change in the condition, means, needs or other circumstances of the children or the spouse since the order was made. Under s.17(6.1), where the change is in income of the payor, the variation is made by recalculating the applicable table amount. A change in the payor's income is expressly recognized as a change in 'means' sufficient to constitute a material change, entitling the court to update the child support amount. There is no 25% minimum threshold.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17(4), s. 17(6.1); Contino v. Leonelli-Contino, [2005] 3 SCR 217
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Variation of Child Support — DA s.17

text:
In contested parenting proceedings under the Divorce Act, the father argues that the court must primarily consider which parent will facilitate the most contact between the child and the other parent. The mother argues that the court's primary consideration should be the child's physical, emotional, and psychological safety, security, and wellbeing.

Under the Divorce Act (as amended in 2021), what is the primary consideration governing parenting orders?
opts: The maximum contact principle — the court must maximize the child's time with both parents as the primary consideration.

The child's physical, emotional, and psychological safety, security, and wellbeing is the primary consideration under DA s.16(3); all other best interests factors are subordinate to this primary consideration.
Continuity of care — the court must primarily consider which parent has been the primary caregiver to date.
The parents' respective financial resources, as this best protects the child's security.
expl: Under the 2021 amendments to the Divorce Act, s.16(3) establishes that the best interests of the child are the only consideration, and s.16(3) specifies that the child's physical, emotional, and psychological safety, security, and wellbeing is the primary consideration in applying the best interests test. The 24 enumerated factors in s.16(3) are all relevant but are subordinate to this overriding primary consideration. The maximum contact principle in s.16.1 is a factor to consider but is not the primary consideration and is explicitly subject to the primary consideration of safety and wellbeing.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 16(3) (as amended S.C. 2019, c. 16)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Best Interests of the Child — DA s.16(3)

text:
In a contested parenting proceeding, there is credible evidence that the father engaged in intimate partner violence against the mother during the marriage. The father argues that since the violence was never directed at the child, it is irrelevant to the best interests analysis. The mother argues it is a significant factor.

How does family violence factor into the best interests of the child analysis under Divorce Act s.16(3)?
opts: Family violence is irrelevant to best interests unless the child was the direct victim of the violence.

Family violence against the other parent (intimate partner violence) is one of the enumerated factors under DA s.16(3)(j) that the court must consider in assessing best interests; a history of family violence, even not directed at the child, is directly relevant to the child's safety, security, and wellbeing.
A finding of family violence automatically results in a denial of all parenting time to the violent parent.
Family violence is only relevant if there has been a criminal conviction arising from the violence.
expl: Under DA s.16(3)(j) (as amended in 2021), family violence — including violence against another family member — is one of the enumerated factors the court must consider in determining the child's best interests. Section 16(3)(j) lists specific considerations including the nature, seriousness, and frequency of the violence, the impact on the child's safety, and the steps taken by the perpetrator to address the violence. Intimate partner violence directly affects the child's safety, security, and wellbeing even if the child was not the direct victim; exposure to domestic violence is well-recognized as harmful to children.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 16(3)(j) (as amended S.C. 2019, c. 16)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Best Interests — Family Violence Factor DA s.16(3)(j)

text:
Following a bitter divorce, the mother refuses to allow the paternal grandparents to see their grandchild. The grandparents apply for a contact order under the Divorce Act. The mother argues the Divorce Act does not confer jurisdiction to make contact orders in favour of grandparents.

Does the Divorce Act confer jurisdiction to make a contact order in favour of grandparents or other third parties?
opts: No — the Divorce Act only grants rights to parents; grandparents must proceed under the Children's Law Reform Act in Ontario.

Yes — under DA s.16.1(2), the court may make a contact order in favour of any person other than a spouse; this includes grandparents, step-parents, and other third parties, where the court determines it is in the best interests of the child.
Yes — but grandparents are automatically entitled to contact; the court must grant their application unless the child objects.

No — contact orders under the Divorce Act may only be made in favour of a person who had a prior legal relationship with the child, such as a person who previously lived with the child for at least two years.
expl: Under Divorce Act s.16.1(2) (as amended 2021), the court may make a contact order granting a person — other than a spouse — the right to have contact with a child. This expressly includes grandparents, step-parents, and other significant persons in the child's life. As with all parenting orders, the governing criterion is the best interests of the child under s.16(3). There is no automatic entitlement to contact; the applicant must satisfy the court that contact serves the child's best interests.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 16.1(2) (as amended S.C. 2019, c. 16)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Contact Orders — DA s.16.1(2)

text:
After divorce, the parties cannot agree on parenting terms. The mother seeks an order for sole decision-making responsibility. The father wants shared decision-making. The court finds that the parents have a high degree of conflict and have been unable to communicate civilly about the child's schooling, medical care, and religious upbringing.

How does the level of parental conflict affect the appropriateness of shared decision-making responsibility under the Divorce Act?
opts: Shared decision-making is always the preferred arrangement regardless of parental conflict, as it ensures both parents remain involved.
The Divorce Act mandates equal parenting time and shared decision-making in all cases; conflict is not a relevant consideration.

Under DA s.16(3), the court determines decision-making responsibility based on the best interests of the child; high conflict between parents and inability to communicate effectively are factors that weigh against shared decision-making responsibility, as shared decision-making requires a minimum level of cooperation and communication to function in the child's best interests.
Conflict between the parents automatically results in an order for sole decision-making by the mother as the primary caregiver.
expl: The Divorce Act does not presume shared decision-making; the court must determine the arrangement that is in the best interests of the child based on all s.16(3) factors. Shared decision-making requires a degree of parental cooperation and communication, as both parents must collaborate on major decisions about education, health, and religion. Where there is entrenched conflict and a demonstrated inability to communicate effectively, courts typically find that shared decision-making is not in the child's best interests, as it exposes the child to ongoing conflict. Sole decision-making by one parent may better serve the child's interests in such circumstances.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 16(3) (as amended S.C. 2019, c. 16)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Decision-Making Responsibility — Sole vs Shared

text:
A separating couple has two young children. Their lawyers are discussing whether a formal parenting plan should be used in their negotiations. One lawyer argues that parenting plans are merely informal documents and have no legal significance under the Divorce Act.

What is the legal significance of parenting plans under the Divorce Act and in Ontario family law proceedings?
opts: Parenting plans have no legal significance; only court orders can govern parenting arrangements.

Under DA s.16.2, the court is encouraged to consider existing or proposed parenting plans in determining parenting orders; a plan agreed upon by the parties and incorporated into a court order becomes legally binding and enforceable.
Parenting plans automatically become binding court orders upon signing by both parents, without the need for court approval.
Parenting plans are binding only if witnessed by a justice of the peace.
expl: The Divorce Act (as amended 2021) recognizes parenting plans as a legitimate tool for resolving parenting arrangements. Section 16.2 encourages the use of parenting plans and provides that the court shall consider any parenting plan proposed by either or both spouses. When a parenting plan agreed by the parties is incorporated into a consent order or a final parenting order by the court, it becomes legally binding and enforceable as a court order. Before court incorporation, a parenting plan is a private agreement without direct court enforcement mechanisms under the Divorce Act, though it may be enforced as a contract.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 16.2 (as amended S.C. 2019, c. 16)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Parenting Plans — DA s.16.2

text:
After separation, a parenting order grants the mother primary parenting time (70% with the mother, 30% with the father). The mother gives proper 60-day notice of her intention to relocate with the child from Ottawa to Calgary. The father objects. The question arises as to who bears the burden of proof in the relocation dispute.

Under Divorce Act ss.16.93-16.95, who bears the burden of proof where the relocating parent has primary parenting time?
opts: The burden of proof always rests with the relocating parent, regardless of the parenting arrangement.

Where the relocating parent has the majority of parenting time, the parent opposing the relocation bears the burden of proving that the relocation is not in the child's best interests under DA s.16.93(2).
The burden of proof is always shared equally between the parents in relocation disputes.
Where the relocating parent has primary parenting time, they must prove on a balance of probabilities that the relocation is necessary for a pressing and substantial reason.
expl: Divorce Act ss.16.93-16.95 establish a differential burden of proof in relocation disputes based on the existing parenting arrangement. Under s.16.93(2), where the relocating parent has the majority of parenting time, the burden falls on the objecting parent to show that the relocation is not in the child's best interests. Conversely, under s.16.93(1), where parenting time is approximately equal (around 50/50), the burden falls on the relocating parent to show the relocation is in the child's best interests. At 70/30, the father bears the burden to demonstrate the relocation should not be permitted.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 16.93(1), s. 16.93(2) (as amended S.C. 2019, c. 16)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Relocation — Burden of Proof DA ss.16.93-16.95

text:
Three siblings are made Crown wards after a lengthy protection proceeding. The two older siblings are placed in separate foster homes. Their paternal grandmother applies for access to all three children, arguing that maintaining sibling and extended family relationships is important for the children's wellbeing.

Can the paternal grandmother seek an access order following Crown wardship under the CYFSA?
opts: No — only parents may apply for access after Crown wardship; extended family members have no standing.

Yes — under CYFSA s.122(1), access may be granted to any person, not only parents, where the relationship is beneficial and meaningful to the child and the access would not impair permanent placement options.
Yes — but grandparent access after Crown wardship is automatic as of right and does not require court approval.
No — sibling access and extended family access are dealt with exclusively under the Children's Law Reform Act, not the CYFSA.
expl: CYFSA s.122(1) provides that a court may grant access to a Crown ward to 'any person,' not only to a parent. This includes grandparents, siblings, and other extended family members. The same two-part test applies: (a) there must be an ongoing relationship that is beneficial and meaningful to the child; and (b) the access must not impair the child's future opportunities for adoption or other permanent placement. Courts regularly consider the importance of sibling relationships and extended family connections in the best interests assessment following Crown wardship.
caseRef: Child, Youth and Family Services Act, 2017, S.O. 2017, c. 14, Sched. 1, s. 122(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Child Protection | Access After Crown Wardship — CYFSA s.122

text:
A child protection worker receives a report that a 7-year-old child has been regularly left alone at home for several hours after school, that the child often arrives at school without lunch, and that the child has disclosed fear of her stepfather's angry outbursts. The worker is assessing whether the child is a 'child in need of protection' under Ontario law.

Under the Child, Youth and Family Services Act, 2017 (CYFSA), which of the following best describes the threshold for a child being 'in need of protection'?
opts: A child is in need of protection only if they have suffered actual physical harm; risk of harm or emotional harm is insufficient.
A child is in need of protection only if a parent has been convicted of a criminal offence related to the child.

Under CYFSA s.74(2), a child is in need of protection if, among other grounds, the child has been or is at risk of being physically, sexually, or emotionally harmed by the child's parent or caregiver, or the child's physical, mental, or emotional development is likely to be seriously impaired due to the guardian's failure to care and protect; the facts described engage multiple potential s.74(2) grounds including emotional harm and neglect.
A child is in need of protection only if the Children's Aid Society has previously been involved with the family.
expl: CYFSA s.74(2) provides a comprehensive list of circumstances in which a child is in need of protection, including: (a) physical harm or risk thereof; (b) sexual abuse or exploitation; (c) emotional or psychological harm (s.74(2)(f)-(g)); (d) failure to provide for the child's needs (neglect — s.74(2)(b)); and (e) risk of harm from serious domestic violence (s.74(2)(j)). A child need not have suffered actual harm; risk of harm is sufficient. On the facts, being left unsupervised (neglect), insufficient food, and fear of the stepfather's anger (emotional harm risk, exposure to family violence) engage multiple potential grounds under s.74(2).
caseRef: Child, Youth and Family Services Act, 2017, S.O. 2017, c. 14, Sched. 1, s. 74(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Child Protection | Child in Need of Protection — CYFSA s.74(2)

text:
A social worker employed at a community agency becomes aware of circumstances suggesting that a child client may be suffering physical abuse at home. The social worker is unsure whether to report, thinking that CAS should only be called when abuse is proven. A colleague suggests that the duty to report arises at a lower threshold.

Under CYFSA s.125, when does a person's duty to report a child protection concern arise?
opts: The duty to report arises only after a finding of abuse by a physician or psychologist confirming the abuse.

The duty to report arises when a person has reasonable grounds to suspect that a child is or may be in need of protection; proof of abuse is not required — suspicion based on reasonable grounds is sufficient.
The duty to report applies only to health care professionals; social workers are exempt.
The duty to report is a discretionary power, not a mandatory obligation; the social worker may choose whether to report.
expl: CYFSA s.125(1) imposes a mandatory duty on every person who has reasonable grounds to suspect that a child is or may be in need of protection to forthwith report the suspicion and the information upon which it is based to a society (CAS). The threshold is reasonable grounds to suspect — not proof, not certainty, and not a professional determination. This duty applies to all persons, including professionals, and it cannot be discharged by reporting to another person such as a supervisor. The duty is personal and immediate.
caseRef: Child, Youth and Family Services Act, 2017, S.O. 2017, c. 14, Sched. 1, s. 125(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Child Protection | Duty to Report — CYFSA s.125

text:
Following a child protection hearing, the court determines that a child is in need of protection. The parents have acknowledged the concerns and have agreed to cooperate with services. CAS recommends that the child remain in the parental home under a supervision order rather than being removed from the parents' care.

Which type of order under the CYFSA allows a child found to be in need of protection to remain with the parent(s) subject to CAS supervision?
opts: Crown wardship order — this is the only order available once a child is found to be in need of protection.

Supervision order — the court may order that the child remain with or be returned to the parent, with the child and the parent being supervised by the Society, where the order is in the best interests of the child.
Temporary care and custody order — this always removes the child from the parent.
Society wardship order — this automatically terminates parental rights.
expl: Under CYFSA s.116(1)(b), where a court finds a child to be in need of protection, it may make a supervision order, directing that the child remain with or be returned to the parent(s), with the child and family being supervised by the CAS for a specified period. A supervision order is appropriate where the identified protective concerns can be addressed with services and monitoring while the child remains in the parental home. It is the least intrusive protection order and reflects the statutory principle of least restrictive intervention consistent with the child's best interests.
caseRef: Child, Youth and Family Services Act, 2017, S.O. 2017, c. 14, Sched. 1, s. 116(1)(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Child Protection | Protection Orders — CYFSA s.116

text:
A court makes an order of Crown wardship for a 5-year-old child after finding that the parents are unable to provide adequate care and there is no realistic prospect of safe return to parental care within a reasonable time. The parents seek an order for access to the child following the Crown wardship order.

What is the legal position regarding parental access following a Crown wardship order under the CYFSA?
opts: Crown wardship automatically terminates all parental access without any right to seek access from the court.
Crown wardship has no effect on parental access; the parents retain the same access rights as before.

Following a Crown wardship order, the court may make an order for access by the parent only where the court is satisfied that: (a) there is an ongoing relationship between the child and the parent that is beneficial and meaningful to the child; and (b) making the access order would not impair the child's future opportunities, including opportunities for adoption.
Parental access after Crown wardship is permitted automatically for a minimum of one hour per week until the child turns 18.
expl: Under CYFSA s.122(1)-(2), following a Crown wardship order, a court may grant access to the child by a parent only if two cumulative conditions are met: (a) the parent and child have an ongoing relationship that is beneficial and meaningful to the child; and (b) making the access order would not impair the child's future opportunities for adoption or otherwise. Both conditions must be satisfied. Crown wardship does not automatically terminate access, but the bar is high — the access must be affirmatively beneficial and must not interfere with the child's permanent placement options, particularly adoption.
caseRef: Child, Youth and Family Services Act, 2017, S.O. 2017, c. 14, Sched. 1, s. 122(1)-(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Child Protection | Crown Wardship and Access — CYFSA s.122

text:
A 15-year-old child has a severe substance use disorder and has repeatedly left voluntary treatment programs. The child's behaviour poses an imminent risk to their health or safety. CAS is considering whether to seek a secure treatment order. The child refuses to consent to treatment.

What is the purpose of a secure treatment order under the CYFSA, and what conditions must be met before such an order can be made?
opts: A secure treatment order is the same as Crown wardship and is available whenever a child is in need of protection.
Secure treatment orders do not exist in Ontario; mental health treatment of minors is addressed solely under the Mental Health Act.

A secure treatment order under the CYFSA authorizes the confinement of a child in a secure treatment program for a mental or other disorder causing serious risk of harm; conditions for the order include that the child requires treatment, the child or a mental disorder is likely to cause the child or others harm, and less restrictive alternatives are not adequate.
A secure treatment order may only be made with the consent of the child, regardless of the child's age.
expl: The CYFSA provides for secure treatment orders (Part VI) for children who require intensive treatment in a secure setting. The criteria for making a secure treatment order include: (a) the child has a mental or other disorder; (b) the child requires treatment for the disorder; (c) confinement of the child in a secure treatment program is necessary to prevent the child from causing serious bodily harm to themselves or others; and (d) no appropriate less restrictive treatment alternative is available. A secure treatment order is distinct from a protection order and is not the same as Crown wardship. It is specifically designed to address situations of mental disorder or substance use requiring involuntary confinement for treatment.
caseRef: Child, Youth and Family Services Act, 2017, S.O. 2017, c. 14, Sched. 1, Part VI (Secure Treatment)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Child Protection | Secure Treatment Orders — CYFSA Part VI

text:
Katerina signed a separation agreement one week before filing for divorce. She alleges that her husband Leon handed her the agreement at 11 pm, told her to sign it immediately or he would destroy her relationship with their children, and refused to allow her time to see a lawyer. She signed under those circumstances. She now seeks to have the agreement set aside.

Which ground under FLA s.56(4) is most directly applicable to Katerina's circumstances?
opts: Unconscionability — the terms of the agreement are grossly unfair.
Failure to disclose significant assets — Leon hid assets from Katerina before signing.

Duress or undue influence — Katerina's signing was coerced by Leon's threats regarding the children, made under time pressure and without access to independent legal advice; this engages both duress (coercion by threat) and potentially undue influence.
Fraud — Leon deceived Katerina about the legal effect of the contract.
expl: Family Law Act s.56(4) provides that a court may set aside a domestic contract or provision if a party failed to disclose significant assets/debts, did not understand the nature/consequences of the contract, or 'otherwise in accordance with the law of contract.' The grounds 'in accordance with the law of contract' import common law and equity grounds including duress and undue influence. Leon's threat to destroy Katerina's relationship with the children (a threat designed to coerce her into signing) accompanied by denial of time to obtain legal advice constitutes duress (or at minimum, undue influence). These are the most directly applicable grounds on these specific facts.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 56(4)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Domestic Contracts | Setting Aside Domestic Contracts — FLA s.56(4)

text:
Before signing a marriage contract, Gerald disclosed his home (value $500,000) but did not disclose his private investment portfolio worth $1.2 million or his RRSP holdings of $400,000. His spouse Linda did not know these assets existed. The marriage later broke down and Linda seeks to set aside the marriage contract.

What is the effect of Gerald's non-disclosure under FLA s.56(4)(a)?
opts: Non-disclosure of assets is not a ground for setting aside a domestic contract; only duress or fraud can have that effect.
Linda must prove that she would not have signed the contract had she known of the assets; without that proof, non-disclosure has no effect.

Under FLA s.56(4)(a), a court may set aside a domestic contract where a party failed to disclose significant assets or debts existing at the time of the contract; Gerald's non-disclosure of the $1.2 million investment portfolio and $400,000 RRSP is likely sufficient to justify setting aside the marriage contract.
Gerald's non-disclosure automatically voids the marriage contract without the need for a court application.
expl: Family Law Act s.56(4)(a) provides that a court may set aside a domestic contract or a provision where a party failed to disclose to the other significant assets or significant debts or other liabilities existing when the domestic contract was made. Non-disclosure of a $1.2 million investment portfolio and a $400,000 RRSP — while disclosing only a $500,000 home — constitutes a significant omission of material assets that existed at the time of signing. Linda has strong grounds to seek judicial rescission of the marriage contract. The court has discretion and will consider all circumstances including what the parties would likely have agreed had full disclosure been made.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 56(4)(a)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Domestic Contracts | Setting Aside — Non-Disclosure FLA s.56(4)(a)

text:
In their separation agreement, Ian and Donna include a clause specifying that no child support will ever be payable by either party, and that Ian waives his right to seek access to the children. Two years later, Ian seeks access to the children and Donna applies for child support. Ian argues the waiver in the separation agreement bars his access claim and Donna's support claim.

To what extent are clauses in a domestic contract governing child support and parenting (access/contact) binding on the parties and the court?
opts: Domestic contract clauses regarding child support and access are fully binding and can never be overridden by the court.

Under FLA s.56(1), a domestic contract provision respecting the education, moral training, or custody of or access to children may be disregarded by the court where the best interests of the child require it; similarly, child support provisions in domestic contracts may be overridden where the contracted amount is not in accordance with the applicable child support guidelines.
Domestic contracts cannot address child support or access at all; these issues must always be determined exclusively by a court.
A child support waiver in a separation agreement is valid and binding; the parties may contract out of their right to seek child support forever.
expl: Family Law Act s.56(1) provides that in the determination of a matter respecting the education, moral training, custody of or access to a child, the court is not bound by a provision of a domestic contract respecting such matters and shall disregard it if, in the opinion of the court, to do so is in the best interests of the child. As for child support, no domestic contract can permanently waive or extinguish child support; courts will override contractual provisions that are inconsistent with the applicable child support guidelines (Miglin v. Miglin, [2003] 1 SCR 303, applied to child support principle). Ian's waiver of access is not binding on him if he later seeks access, and Donna's contractual waiver of child support is not binding on the child's best interests.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 56(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Domestic Contracts | Limits on Domestic Contracts — Child Support and Access

text:
Rachel and her husband Tom negotiate the terms of a separation agreement during mediation. After reaching agreement, they shake hands and verbally confirm the deal but do not sign anything. Rachel later argues the verbal agreement is a binding separation agreement. Tom disputes this.

Under the Family Law Act, what are the formal requirements for a valid domestic contract, and does Rachel and Tom's verbal agreement satisfy them?
opts: A verbal agreement confirmed in good faith is a valid and enforceable domestic contract under the Family Law Act.

Under FLA s.55(1), a domestic contract is unenforceable unless it is made in writing, signed by both parties, and witnessed; a verbal agreement does not satisfy these requirements and is not a binding domestic contract.
A domestic contract is valid if two witnesses observe the verbal agreement, even without a written document.
A domestic contract requires notarization by a notary public to be valid; a signature alone is insufficient.
expl: Family Law Act s.55(1) provides that a domestic contract and an amendment to a domestic contract are unenforceable unless made in writing, signed by the parties, and witnessed. All three requirements — writing, signature, and witness — must be met for the contract to be valid and enforceable. Rachel and Tom's verbal handshake agreement does not satisfy any of these requirements. Without a written and witnessed signed document, the purported agreement is unenforceable as a domestic contract under the FLA.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 55(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Domestic Contracts | Formal Validity — FLA s.55

text:
Two individuals, Sam and Alex, are planning to marry. Before the wedding, Sam wants to enter into an agreement with Alex that specifies how their property will be divided in the event of marriage breakdown and limits any future spousal support claims. Their lawyer advises them on the appropriate type of domestic contract.

Under the Family Law Act, what is the appropriate domestic contract for parties who are about to marry and wish to govern their property and support rights in the event of marriage breakdown?
opts: Cohabitation agreement — because they have not yet married.
Separation agreement — because this is the agreement that governs marriage breakdown.

Marriage contract — under FLA s.52, two persons who are married or who intend to marry may enter into a marriage contract to govern ownership of or division of property, support obligations, and other matters in the event of separation, death, or other circumstances.
There is no recognized domestic contract for parties intending to marry; Sam and Alex must wait until after the wedding to negotiate their rights.
expl: Family Law Act s.52(1) defines a marriage contract as an agreement entered into by two persons who are married to each other or intend to marry, in which they agree on their respective rights and obligations under the marriage or on separation, annulment, dissolution of the marriage or death. A marriage contract (also called a prenuptial agreement) may address property division, spousal support, and other matters. It is the appropriate vehicle before marriage. A cohabitation agreement governs unmarried cohabiting couples; a separation agreement governs the terms of an existing separation.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 52(1) (marriage contract), s. 53 (cohabitation agreement), s. 54 (separation agreement)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Domestic Contracts | Types of Domestic Contracts — FLA ss.52-54

text:
Nathan has accumulated $18,000 in child support arrears. The FRO has been unable to collect payment. Nathan is employed and earns $5,000/month net salary. He also has a valid Ontario driver's licence. The FRO is considering what enforcement tools to use.

Which of the following FRO enforcement mechanisms are available against Nathan in these circumstances?
opts: The FRO may only apply to court for a contempt order; other enforcement tools require a court application.

The FRO may garnish Nathan's wages under FRSAEA s.14 and may suspend Nathan's driver's licence under FRSAEA s.34 without a court order; both mechanisms are available to the Director where there are arrears.
The FRO may garnish Nathan's wages but must obtain a court order specifically authorizing each and every garnishment.
The FRO cannot enforce child support arrears; the recipient must personally pursue enforcement through the courts.
expl: The FRSAEA gives the Director of the FRO extensive enforcement powers to collect support arrears without requiring individual court orders for each enforcement action. Under FRSAEA s.14, the Director may serve a notice of garnishment on Nathan's employer to divert a portion of his wages to satisfy arrears. Under FRSAEA s.34, where a payor is in default of a support order, the Director may serve notice on the Registrar of Motor Vehicles to suspend the payor's driver's licence. Both tools are available to the Director based on the existing arrears, without the need for further court proceedings.
caseRef: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31, s. 14 (garnishment), s. 34 (driver's licence suspension)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement | FRO Enforcement — Garnishment s.14 and Driver's Licence Suspension s.34

text:
Carlos has been in default of his support order for over a year and owes $25,000 in arrears. He has ignored FRO's garnishment efforts. He is self-employed and does not have a traditional employer. The Director brings a motion for a default hearing. Carlos asks what can happen to him at a default hearing.

What orders may a court make at a default hearing under FRSAEA s.37?
opts: A default hearing is a mediation session; the court cannot make any enforcement order at the hearing.
At a default hearing, the court may only order the payor to pay a lump sum equal to all arrears immediately; no other remedy is available.

Under FRSAEA s.37, at a default hearing the court may make a range of orders including ordering the payor to pay arrears by installments, imposing a recognizance, requiring security for payment, incarcerating the payor for up to 180 days, or making any other appropriate enforcement order.
A default hearing is available only where the payor has been served by personal service; substituted service is not permitted.
expl: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act s.37(11) provides that at a default hearing, the court has a wide range of remedies including: ordering payment of arrears by specified installments; requiring the payor to post security; requiring the payor to enter into a recognizance; and — as a last resort — incarcerating the payor for up to 180 days. Incarceration is not automatic; it is a remedy of last resort where the court finds the payor is able to pay but willfully refuses to do so (civil contempt in the support context). The default hearing is a court proceeding with real enforcement consequences, not a mediation.
caseRef: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31, s. 37(11)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement | Default Hearing — FRSAEA s.37

text:
After their divorce, Susan and Paul have an amicable relationship and prefer to handle child support payments directly between themselves without FRO involvement. They both want to opt out of the FRO. Paul assumes that because he is making payments voluntarily, there is no need to formally notify the FRO.

Under the FRSAEA, what is required for the parties to opt out of FRO enforcement?
opts: Either party may unilaterally opt out by filing a notice with the FRO; no consent from the other party is required.
Voluntary payment automatically removes the obligation to pay through FRO; no formal opt-out procedure is required.

Under FRSAEA s.7, a support order may be withdrawn from the FRO only upon the filing of a joint notice signed by both the payor and the recipient, indicating that the parties are aware of the consequences of withdrawal; the notice must be filed with the Director.
Opting out of FRO requires a court order; the parties cannot withdraw from the program by agreement alone.
expl: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act s.7(1) provides that a support order may be withdrawn from the Director's enforcement upon the filing of a joint notice with the Director, signed by both the payor and the recipient, indicating that the parties are aware of the consequences of withdrawing from the Director's enforcement. Both parties must consent and sign; neither can unilaterally withdraw from the FRO. Simply making voluntary payments does not exempt the parties from the FRO scheme — a formal joint withdrawal notice is required.
caseRef: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31, s. 7(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement | Opting Out of FRO — FRSAEA s.7

text:
A court makes a child support order requiring David to pay $1,200/month. Neither party asks about the Family Responsibility Office at the hearing. David asks his lawyer whether he must pay support through the FRO or whether he can pay his former spouse directly without involving the FRO.

Under the Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996 (FRSAEA), are support orders automatically filed with the Family Responsibility Office?
opts: Support orders are filed with the FRO only if the recipient requests enrollment; otherwise direct payment between the parties is the default.

Under FRSAEA s.3, every support order made by an Ontario court is automatically filed with the Director of the Family Responsibility Office; David must make payments through the FRO unless the parties have filed a joint notice to withdraw from the program.
Support orders are filed with the FRO only if the payor is in arrears at the time the order is made.
The FRO is optional; there is no automatic filing of any support order without the parties' express consent.
expl: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, s.3(1) provides that every support order made by a court in Ontario shall be filed with the Director of the Family Responsibility Office immediately upon being made. This filing is automatic and mandatory — it does not require either party's request or consent. Once filed, the Director takes over enforcement of the order, and payments must be made to the FRO (not directly to the recipient) unless the parties have filed a joint withdrawal under s.7.
caseRef: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31, s. 3(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement | Automatic Registration — FRSAEA s.3

text:
Jessica and Mark have cohabited for 9 years without marrying. They have one child together. They now separate. Jessica contributed significantly to the home (registered solely in Mark's name) by paying renovation costs and acting as the primary caregiver for their child. Jessica asks whether she is entitled to property equalization under the Family Law Act.

Is Jessica entitled to property equalization under Part I of the Family Law Act as a common-law spouse?
opts: Yes — after 3 years of cohabitation, unmarried spouses acquire the same property equalization rights as married spouses under the FLA.
Yes — any person who shares a child with another person is entitled to FLA property equalization regardless of whether they were married.

No — Part I of the Family Law Act (equalization of net family property) applies only to married spouses; Jessica and Mark are unmarried and Jessica has no right to equalization of net family property under the FLA.
Yes — after 5 years of cohabitation, a common-law spouse is automatically deemed a 'spouse' under all provisions of the FLA, including property equalization.
expl: Part I of the Family Law Act (equalization of net family property, including the matrimonial home regime) applies exclusively to married spouses. Section 1(1) of the FLA defines 'spouse' for the purposes of Part I to mean only persons who are or were married to each other. Common-law partners — regardless of the length of cohabitation or whether they have children together — have no right to equalization of net family property under the FLA. Jessica must rely on other legal theories (such as unjust enrichment or resulting trust) to assert a claim to an interest in the home.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 1(1) (definition of spouse — Part I), Part I
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights | No FLA Property Equalization for Unmarried Partners

text:
After 12 years of cohabitation, Sandra and Greg separate. During the relationship, Sandra worked part-time and managed the home and their two children, allowing Greg to advance his career. Greg accumulated significant savings and a business. The home is registered in Greg's name. Sandra now claims an interest in Greg's property.

What is the primary legal basis on which a common-law partner may claim an interest in property held in the other partner's name upon relationship breakdown in Ontario?
opts: The Family Law Act — Part I equalization applies equally to all couples who have cohabited for 12 or more years.
Adverse possession — Sandra may claim property through long-term occupation.

Unjust enrichment — where one partner has been enriched at the expense of the other, without juristic reason, in circumstances where a corresponding deprivation has occurred; the court may remedy the unjust enrichment by imposing a constructive trust or awarding a monetary remedy.
Statutory resulting trust — Ontario family law creates an automatic resulting trust in favour of any long-term cohabiting partner.
expl: In the absence of FLA equalization rights, a common-law partner who claims an interest in property held by the other partner must rely on the equitable doctrine of unjust enrichment as developed in Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834 and refined in Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269. Unjust enrichment requires proof of: (1) enrichment of the defendant; (2) a corresponding deprivation of the plaintiff; and (3) the absence of a juristic reason for the enrichment. If established, the court may impose a constructive trust over specific property or award a monetary remedy proportionate to the contribution.
caseRef: Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834; Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269; Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights | Unjust Enrichment — Basis for Common-Law Property Claim

text:
Carla and Howard cohabited for 2.5 years in a conjugal relationship and separated without having any children together. Carla now seeks spousal support from Howard under the Family Law Act. Howard argues that she is not his 'spouse' for support purposes because they never married and cohabited for less than three years.

Under the Family Law Act s.29, is Carla entitled to seek spousal support as a common-law partner?
opts: Yes — any person who has cohabited with another in a conjugal relationship for any period of time may seek spousal support under the FLA.

No — Howard is correct; under FLA s.29, a common-law partner must have cohabited continuously for at least three years or be the parent of a child with the other person; Carla has cohabited for only 2.5 years and they have no children together, so she does not qualify as a 'spouse' for support purposes.
Yes — because Carla is a common-law partner, she automatically qualifies as a 'spouse' under all provisions of the FLA including support.
No — common-law partners can never claim spousal support under the Family Law Act; they must proceed under the Divorce Act.
expl: Family Law Act s.29 defines 'spouse' for the purposes of Part III (support) to include a person who has cohabited with another person continuously for a period of not less than three years, OR a person who is in a relationship of some permanence if they are the natural or adoptive parents of a child together. Carla cohabited for only 2.5 years and has no children with Howard — neither threshold in s.29 is met. Howard's argument is correct: Carla does not qualify as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 and cannot claim spousal support under the FLA. (Note: The Divorce Act applies only to married persons and is unavailable to Carla.)
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29 (definition of spouse for support purposes)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights | Spousal Support for Common-Law Partners — FLA s.29

text:
For 10 years, Patricia worked full-time and contributed 40% of her salary to the household expenses, while her common-law partner Derek paid the mortgage on his home (registered in his name alone) and invested in his business. Patricia did not do more than her fair share of household work; she paid equally for shared expenses. She now claims unjust enrichment and seeks a constructive trust over Derek's home.

Is Patricia likely to establish unjust enrichment entitling her to a constructive trust over Derek's home?
opts: Yes — any contribution to household expenses by a cohabiting partner automatically establishes unjust enrichment and entitles the contributor to a constructive trust.
No — only contributions in the form of unpaid domestic labour (not monetary contributions to shared expenses) can ground an unjust enrichment claim.

Likely no — while Patricia made monetary contributions, a 50% equal sharing of household expenses does not give rise to unjust enrichment; Derek received no enrichment beyond what was bargained for in the mutual support of the household, and there is likely a juristic reason (equal contribution arrangement) negating the claim.
Yes — cohabitation for 10 years automatically establishes unjust enrichment entitling the claimant to a 50% interest in all jointly used property.
expl: Unjust enrichment requires: (1) enrichment of the defendant; (2) a corresponding deprivation of the plaintiff; and (3) no juristic reason for the enrichment (Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269). Where Patricia contributed equally to shared household expenses (a mutual arrangement), Derek received no unjust benefit — he paid his share and Patricia paid hers. The equal contribution arrangement constitutes a juristic reason (a mutual benefit-conferring arrangement) that negates the unjust enrichment claim. Patricia's failure to show that she contributed disproportionately — beyond what was expected in a mutual sharing arrangement — makes it unlikely that the third element (no juristic reason) is met.
caseRef: Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269; Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights | Unjust Enrichment — Three Elements

text:
Rogers Communications Inc. operates a federally regulated telecommunications undertaking in Quebec. The City of Chateauguay attempts to apply its municipal zoning by-law to prevent Rogers from installing telecommunications infrastructure on certain lands. Rogers argues the by-law cannot apply to it as a federal undertaking under the doctrine of interjurisdictional immunity.

Under the doctrine of interjurisdictional immunity as clarified in Canadian Western Bank and Rogers Communications, when does a provincial or municipal law become inapplicable to a federal undertaking?
opts: Whenever a provincial law regulates any activity that involves a federal undertaking, the law is automatically inapplicable to that undertaking.

A provincial law is inapplicable to a federal undertaking only where it impairs the vital or essential core of the federal head of power or undertaking; the doctrine of interjurisdictional immunity should be applied with restraint and only where the constitutional text or clear precedent requires it.
A provincial law always applies to federal undertakings unless Parliament has expressly enacted legislation displacing the provincial law.
Interjurisdictional immunity applies automatically to all federally regulated industries, including banks, railways, and telecommunications companies, regardless of the nature of the provincial law.
expl: The Supreme Court of Canada in Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22, and Rogers Communications Inc v Chateauguay (City), 2016 SCC 23, significantly narrowed the doctrine of interjurisdictional immunity. The doctrine applies only where a provincial law impairs the 'core' of a federal head of power or a vital and essential part of a federal undertaking. The Court expressed a preference for the paramountcy doctrine over interjurisdictional immunity and stated that IJI should be applied with restraint to avoid creating watertight compartments of exclusive federal authority. In Rogers, the Court found municipal zoning that prevented telecommunications installation impaired the core of federal telecommunications jurisdiction.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, s. 91, s. 92; Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22; Rogers Communications Inc v Chateauguay (City), 2016 SCC 23
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Interjurisdictional Immunity — Provincial Law Affecting Core of Federal Undertaking

text:
Parliament enacts the Canada Consumer Product Safety Act, which sets national safety standards for children's toys and expressly states that manufacturers may sell any toy that complies with the federal standards. Ontario subsequently enacts stricter safety standards for children's toys. A manufacturer complies with the federal standards but not the stricter Ontario standards. Ontario attempts to enforce its provincial standards against the manufacturer.

Under the federal paramountcy doctrine, which of the following tests determines whether the Ontario law is rendered inoperative by the federal legislation?
opts: The Ontario law is rendered inoperative only if it is impossible for a person to comply with both the federal and provincial laws at the same time.
The Ontario law is rendered inoperative if either there is an express conflict (impossibility of dual compliance) or if the provincial law frustrates the purpose of the federal legislation.
The Ontario law is rendered inoperative automatically whenever Parliament enacts legislation on the same subject matter, regardless of conflict.
The Ontario law is rendered inoperative only if the enabling statute expressly states that provincial legislation is displaced.
