expl: Under the federal paramountcy doctrine, a provincial law is rendered inoperative (not invalid) to the extent it conflicts with valid federal legislation. The Supreme Court of Canada has recognized two branches of the paramountcy test: (1) express or operational conflict — where compliance with both laws is impossible; and (2) frustration of federal purpose — where the provincial law, though technically capable of dual compliance, frustrates or contradicts the purpose of the federal scheme. Here, the federal scheme expressly permits manufacturers who meet federal standards to sell nationally; the stricter Ontario standards frustrate this federal purpose and are rendered inoperative.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, s. 91, s. 92; Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22; Rogers Communications Inc v Chateauguay (City), 2016 SCC 23
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Federal Paramountcy — Express Conflict and Frustration of Federal Purpose

text:
Parliament enacts legislation regulating the dumping of toxic waste into the ocean off Canada's coasts. The legislation is challenged by British Columbia, which argues that environmental regulation of coastal areas falls within provincial jurisdiction under s. 92 of the Constitution Act, 1867. Parliament relies on the Peace, Order and Good Government (POGG) power under s. 91 of the Constitution Act, 1867.

Under the national concern branch of the POGG power, what are the criteria that must be satisfied for Parliament to validly legislate on a matter not enumerated in s. 91?
opts: Parliament may invoke POGG only during a national emergency; absent a declared emergency, POGG cannot support legislation on matters not enumerated in s. 91.

Under the national concern branch established in R. v Crown Zellerbach, the matter must have a singleness, distinctiveness, and indivisibility that clearly distinguishes it from matters of provincial concern; and the scale of impact on provincial jurisdiction must be reconcilable with the division of powers.
POGG allows Parliament to legislate on any matter it considers to be in the national interest, subject only to a test of good faith.
The national concern branch of POGG applies only to matters that span multiple provinces; it does not apply to ocean dumping, which occurs in federal waters.
expl: The national concern branch of POGG was established in R. v Crown Zellerbach Canada Ltd., [1988] 1 SCR 401. The Supreme Court held that for a matter to fall under the national concern branch, it must have a singleness, distinctiveness, and indivisibility that clearly distinguishes it from matters of provincial concern, and that the failure of one province to legislate or effectively regulate the matter would have an adverse effect on other provinces or on the country as a whole (the 'provincial inability' test). The court upheld ocean dumping as a matter of national concern given the indivisible nature of marine environmental protection.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, s. 91 (preamble — POGG); R v Crown Zellerbach Canada Ltd., [1988] 1 SCR 401
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Peace, Order and Good Government — National Concern Branch

text:
A member of a provincial energy regulatory board is assigned to adjudicate a rate application filed by a natural gas company. Before the hearing, the company's lawyer discovers that the board member, prior to his appointment, publicly wrote and circulated an opinion paper strongly advocating for the very rate-setting methodology the company now opposes. The applicant submits a motion to disqualify the board member on grounds of bias.

What is the legal test for a reasonable apprehension of bias as established by the Supreme Court of Canada?
opts: Whether the decision-maker actually harbours subjective bias against the party seeking disqualification.

Whether a reasonable and informed person, having thought the matter through, would conclude that it is more likely than not that the decision-maker, whether consciously or unconsciously, would not decide fairly.
Whether the decision-maker has any prior connection whatsoever to the subject matter of the proceeding.
Whether there is direct evidence that the decision-maker communicated privately with one party before or during the hearing.
expl: The test for reasonable apprehension of bias was established in Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369, per de Grandpre J (dissent adopted as the correct test): the question is whether a reasonable and informed person, having thought the matter through, would apprehend that the decision-maker, consciously or unconsciously, would not decide fairly. This is an objective test measured from the perspective of a reasonable and fully informed observer — not whether actual bias exists.
caseRef: Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369; Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817; Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | Reasonable Apprehension of Bias

text:
A physician undergoing a fitness-to-practise inquiry before a disciplinary committee submits an affidavit from a witness who attended a committee pre-hearing meeting. The witness swears that she heard a committee member say: 'We all know this doctor is guilty; let's get the hearing over with quickly.' The physician applies to quash the proceeding, arguing both actual bias and reasonable apprehension of bias.

What is the legal distinction between actual bias and reasonable apprehension of bias, and what is the practical consequence for this disciplinary proceeding?
opts: Actual bias requires proof of a financial interest in the outcome; reasonable apprehension of bias requires proof of personal animosity. Both are difficult to establish and rarely succeed.

Actual bias means the decision-maker has already formed a definitive view on the merits, rendering the proceeding a sham; reasonable apprehension of bias is an objective test that may be established without proof of actual prejudgment. Both grounds, if established, disqualify the decision-maker and vitiate any resulting decision.
Actual bias is only relevant in criminal proceedings; in administrative law only reasonable apprehension of bias applies.
Reasonable apprehension of bias requires a higher standard of proof than actual bias; proof of actual bias is therefore easier to establish.
expl: Actual bias — where a decision-maker has pre-judged the outcome or cannot approach the matter with an open mind — is a distinct and more serious ground than reasonable apprehension of bias. Reasonable apprehension of bias, as established in Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369, requires only that a reasonable and informed observer would apprehend a lack of impartiality; proof of actual closed-mindedness is not required. Both grounds invalidate the proceeding and any decision rendered by the biased adjudicator. Here, the committee member's statement constitutes compelling evidence of actual bias (prejudgment) and would also satisfy the reasonable apprehension test.
caseRef: Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369; Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817; Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | Actual Bias vs Reasonable Apprehension of Bias

text:
An Ontario occupational health and safety tribunal has its members appointed to renewable one-year terms by Order in Council. Members' salaries are set by Cabinet order and are not protected against reduction. The tribunal's administrative support, including scheduling and document management, is provided by the ministry whose decisions the tribunal reviews. A regulated employer argues that the tribunal lacks the institutional independence required by procedural fairness.

Which of the Valente factors is most clearly violated by the circumstances described, and what is the legal consequence?
opts: Administrative independence — the fact that the ministry provides administrative support to the tribunal that reviews its decisions creates at minimum a reasonable apprehension that the tribunal lacks independence from the executive.
Security of tenure — because members are appointed for renewable one-year terms and serve at pleasure, they lack the security of tenure required for adjudicative independence.
Financial security — because salaries can be reduced by Cabinet order without legislative protection, members lack financial security, undermining their independence.
All three Valente factors are violated and the tribunal lacks any capacity to make lawful decisions.
expl: Valente v The Queen, [1985] 2 SCR 673, identified three essential conditions of judicial independence: (1) security of tenure, (2) financial security, and (3) administrative independence (institutional independence). Administrative independence requires that the tribunal control the administrative decisions that bear directly on adjudicative functions. Where the ministry whose decisions the tribunal reviews also controls its administrative support — scheduling, document management — a reasonable apprehension of institutional dependence on the executive arises. While security of tenure and financial security concerns are also present here, administrative independence is most directly implicated by the ministry's administrative control of the adjudicative process.
caseRef: Valente v The Queen, [1985] 2 SCR 673; Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817; Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | Institutional Independence — Valente Factors

text:
During a four-day regulatory hearing before an Ontario tribunal, a party's counsel becomes aware on day two that a tribunal member owns shares in one of the companies whose commercial interests would be directly affected by the regulatory outcome. Counsel does not raise the issue during the hearing and waits until after an adverse decision is issued to bring a motion seeking to quash the decision on grounds of reasonable apprehension of bias.

Is the party's bias objection likely to succeed, and what is the governing legal principle?
opts: Yes. A reasonable apprehension of bias arising from a financial interest can never be waived, even through conduct, and the objection is timely whenever raised.
Yes. The party is entitled to wait for the outcome of the proceeding before deciding whether to raise bias, and raising it post-decision is procedurally permissible.

No. A party who becomes aware of a potential bias ground during a proceeding and fails to raise it at the earliest reasonable opportunity is taken to have waived the objection; raising it only after an adverse decision is issued will generally be seen as an improper tactical manoeuvre.
No, but only because financial interest is not a recognized ground of reasonable apprehension of bias for tribunal members.
expl: A party who is aware of a potential bias ground must raise it at the earliest reasonable opportunity in the proceedings. Failure to do so constitutes a waiver of the objection. This principle, recognized in Canadian administrative law, prevents parties from 'keep in reserve' a bias objection as insurance against an unfavourable outcome. Waiting until after an adverse decision has been rendered — when the party was aware of the potential bias from day two of a four-day hearing — is a classic example of strategic waiver, and courts will generally refuse to entertain the belated objection.
caseRef: Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369; Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817; Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | Waiver of Bias Objection

text:
A regulated real estate agent receives an adverse decision from the Real Estate Council of Ontario (RECO). The agent wishes to challenge the decision by way of judicial review in Ontario. She is unsure whether to file in the Superior Court of Justice or the Divisional Court.

Which Ontario court has jurisdiction to hear judicial review applications of decisions made by Ontario provincial administrative tribunals?
opts: The Ontario Court of Appeal has exclusive jurisdiction to review decisions of Ontario tribunals.
The Superior Court of Justice has exclusive jurisdiction over all judicial review applications in Ontario.

The Divisional Court, as a branch of the Superior Court of Justice, has jurisdiction under s. 6(1) of the Courts of Justice Act to hear and determine applications for judicial review of Ontario provincial tribunals.
The Federal Court has jurisdiction over all administrative tribunal decisions in Canada, including provincial tribunals.
expl: Under the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s. 6(1), the Divisional Court has jurisdiction to hear and determine applications for judicial review. The Divisional Court is a branch of the Superior Court of Justice and is the primary forum for judicial review of decisions by Ontario provincial administrative tribunals. Leave to appeal from the Divisional Court goes to the Court of Appeal; the Federal Court has no jurisdiction over provincial tribunals.
caseRef: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s. 6(1); Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Divisional Court — Jurisdiction over Judicial Review of Ontario Tribunals

text:
A party receives an adverse decision from the Ontario Rental Housing Tribunal and wishes to seek judicial review. The Judicial Review Procedure Act governs the procedure. The party has been informed that in some cases leave of the Divisional Court is required before a judicial review application can proceed, while in others no leave is needed.

Under the Judicial Review Procedure Act, when is leave to seek judicial review required?
opts: Leave is always required before any judicial review application may proceed in Ontario.
Leave is never required; all judicial review applications may proceed as of right in the Divisional Court.

Leave is required where the enabling statute of the tribunal under review specifically requires leave as a condition of judicial review, or where the matter is within a category for which the JRPA or Courts of Justice Act prescribes a leave requirement.
Leave is required only where the applicant seeks certiorari; mandamus and prohibition applications may always proceed without leave.
expl: Under the Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, there is no blanket universal leave requirement for judicial review applications. However, the enabling statutes of specific Ontario tribunals may impose a leave requirement as a condition of seeking judicial review, and the Courts of Justice Act may impose leave requirements in specific contexts (for example, certain appeals). Where no leave requirement is prescribed, the application proceeds as of right. Practitioners must check the specific tribunal's enabling legislation for any leave conditions.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1; Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s. 6
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Divisional Court — Leave Requirements for Judicial Review

text:
An Ontario securities regulator is in the process of conducting a hearing but has not yet issued a decision. A respondent becomes aware during the hearing that the panel chair participated in an earlier staff investigation in a way that raises a serious reasonable apprehension of bias. The respondent seeks an immediate judicial review remedy to halt the ongoing hearing before a decision is made.

Which prerogative remedy is most appropriate to prevent the biased tribunal from continuing and issuing a decision it has no lawful jurisdiction to make?
opts: Certiorari, because certiorari is the remedy used to quash an administrative decision before it is made.
Mandamus, because the respondent is seeking to compel the tribunal to halt the proceeding.
Prohibition, because prohibition is the appropriate remedy to prevent an inferior tribunal from exercising jurisdiction it does not lawfully possess before a decision has been rendered.
Declaratory relief, because a declaration that the tribunal is biased is sufficient to halt the proceeding without the need for a prerogative writ.
expl: Prohibition is the prerogative remedy that prevents an inferior tribunal from exercising jurisdiction it does not possess — or from exceeding jurisdiction it does possess — before a decision has been issued. Under the Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2, all such prerogative remedies are available in a single application for judicial review. Where a bias ground disqualifies the panel from adjudicating, prohibition lies to prevent the unlawful proceeding from continuing. Once a decision has been made, certiorari would be the appropriate remedy to quash it.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2; Dunsmuir v New Brunswick, [2008] 1 SCR 190
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | JR Remedies — Certiorari, Mandamus, Prohibition, and Declaratory Relief

text:
A labour arbitrator issues a decision under an Ontario collective agreement. The Labour Relations Act contains the following clause: 'Every decision or order of an arbitrator under this Part is final and binding and is not subject to appeal to any court or to review or restraint by any court.' An employer seeks judicial review in the Divisional Court, arguing the arbitrator committed a jurisdictional error.

What effect does a full privative clause have on the availability of judicial review of an arbitrator's decision?
opts: A full privative clause completely bars judicial review; no court has any jurisdiction whatsoever to review the arbitrator's decision.
A full privative clause is constitutionally invalid because superior courts have inherent jurisdiction to review inferior tribunals that cannot be ousted by statute.

A full privative clause is a strong signal that the legislature intended deference to the decision-maker, informing the standard of review analysis; however, privative clauses do not entirely oust judicial review, which remains available to correct true jurisdictional errors, constitutional violations, and questions of natural justice.
A full privative clause shifts the burden of proof to the applicant to prove the decision was unreasonable beyond a reasonable doubt.
expl: Privative clauses reflect legislative intent that courts give deference to specialized decision-makers, and under the Vavilov framework they inform the standard of review analysis by supporting a reasonableness standard. However, s. 96 of the Constitution Act, 1867 protects the core jurisdiction of superior courts, and the Supreme Court of Canada has consistently held that privative clauses cannot entirely oust judicial review. Courts retain jurisdiction to review decisions for jurisdictional errors, constitutional violations, and breaches of natural justice notwithstanding a full privative clause.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65; Constitution Act, 1867, s. 96
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Privative Clauses — Effect on Judicial Review

text:
An Ontario statute governing a specialized environmental review board provides: 'The Board's decisions on matters within its jurisdiction are final.' A proponent seeks judicial review, arguing the Board exceeded its statutory jurisdiction by considering social impact evidence that the enabling statute does not mention. The Board argues its privative clause bars any review.

What is the legal distinction between a full privative clause and a partial privative clause, and how does each affect the availability of judicial review?
opts: A full privative clause bars review on all grounds; a partial privative clause bars review on questions of law but not questions of fact.
There is no meaningful distinction; both full and partial privative clauses are treated identically under the Vavilov framework as signals of deference.

A full privative clause purports to bar all judicial review; a partial privative clause (such as 'final on matters within its jurisdiction') bars review within the scope of the grant while implicitly preserving review for jurisdictional questions, excess of jurisdiction, or constitutional issues — thereby narrowing but not eliminating the scope of review.
A partial privative clause, unlike a full clause, is constitutionally invalid because it acknowledges the possibility of review without granting it.
expl: A full privative clause purports to exclude all judicial review entirely. A partial privative clause — such as one that protects decisions 'within jurisdiction' or 'on matters within the Board's authority' — by its own terms limits protection to decisions made within the scope of the grant, leaving open the possibility of review for decisions made in excess of jurisdiction. Courts have interpreted such language as preserving review for jurisdictional excess. Under Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, even a full privative clause cannot entirely oust review, but a partial clause is even more clearly limited in its protective scope.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65; Constitution Act, 1867, s. 96
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Privative Clauses — Full vs Partial; Jurisdictional Questions

text:
A regulated financial advisor is the subject of a hearing before the Financial Services Regulatory Authority of Ontario (FSRA) tribunal. On the second day of the scheduled three-day hearing, the advisor's counsel advises the tribunal that her key expert witness was involved in a traffic accident that morning and cannot attend. Counsel requests an adjournment of the remaining two days of the hearing. The tribunal chair denies the adjournment, stating the panel has another hearing scheduled immediately after and cannot accommodate a delay.

Under the SPPA and principles of procedural fairness, what legal standard governs the tribunal's exercise of discretion to grant or refuse an adjournment?
opts: The tribunal has absolute discretion to deny adjournments; once denied, the decision is not reviewable.
The tribunal must grant any adjournment requested by a party unless the requesting party has abused process.

Under SPPA s. 21, a tribunal has discretion to grant adjournments, but that discretion must be exercised consistent with the duty of fairness; refusing an adjournment solely for administrative convenience — where the refusal deprives a party of the ability to present their case — may constitute a breach of procedural fairness reviewable on judicial review.
The tribunal may refuse an adjournment only where the requesting party has previously been granted at least two adjournments in the same proceeding.
expl: Under the Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 21, a tribunal may, where it considers it appropriate, grant an adjournment of a hearing. While this discretion is broad, it must be exercised consistently with the duty of procedural fairness established in Baker v Canada, [1999] 2 SCR 817, and the common law. Denying an adjournment purely for administrative scheduling reasons, where the denial prevents a party from calling essential evidence, may render the hearing procedurally unfair and subject the decision to judicial review. The test is whether the refusal deprived the party of a fair hearing.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 21; Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Statutory Powers Procedure Act | Adjournments — SPPA Section 21

text:
At a hearing before an Ontario professional discipline tribunal governed by the SPPA, tribunal counsel seeks to introduce a written statement from a witness who is not present at the hearing. The witness's statement contains the witness's account of events, but the witness cannot be cross-examined. The respondent's counsel objects, arguing that hearsay evidence is inadmissible under the rules of evidence that apply in courts of law.

Under s. 15 of the SPPA, are Ontario tribunals bound by the strict rules of evidence applicable in courts, including the common law hearsay rule?
opts: Yes. Ontario tribunals must apply the same evidentiary rules as the Superior Court of Justice, including the common law rule against hearsay.

No. Under s. 15 of the SPPA, a tribunal may admit as evidence any oral testimony and any document or other thing relevant to the subject matter of the proceeding and may act on such evidence, regardless of whether it would be admissible as evidence in a court of law.
No. Ontario tribunals may admit any evidence they choose, including evidence that is irrelevant, privileged, or obtained through Charter violations.
Yes, but only where the enabling statute expressly requires application of the rules of evidence.
expl: Under the Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 15(1), a tribunal may admit as evidence at a hearing any oral testimony and any document or other thing relevant to the subject matter of the proceeding and may act on such evidence, whether or not the evidence would be admissible in a court of law. This means Ontario tribunals are not bound by the strict common law rules of evidence, including the hearsay rule. However, the evidence must be relevant, and the tribunal must still provide parties a fair opportunity to respond.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 15(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Statutory Powers Procedure Act | Evidence at SPPA Hearings — Section 15

text:
An Ontario tribunal governed by the SPPA sends a notice of hearing to a pharmacist at his clinic address by regular mail. The notice states the date and time of the hearing but does not identify the subject matter of the proceedings or the legal authority under which the tribunal is proceeding. The pharmacist attends the hearing without having sought an adjournment. At the hearing, the pharmacist's counsel argues the notice was defective.

Under s. 6 of the SPPA, what are the minimum requirements for a notice of hearing to be adequate?
opts: The notice need only state the date and time of the hearing; the subject matter may be disclosed at the hearing itself.

The notice must be in writing, state the date, time, and place of the hearing, and contain a statement of the subject matter of the proceeding with sufficient particularity to enable the party to understand the nature of the proceeding and prepare a response.
The notice must be personally served on the party; notice by mail is never sufficient under the SPPA.
The notice is adequate so long as it was sent to the party's last known address, regardless of its content.
expl: Under the Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 6, notice of a hearing must be given to all parties. The notice must be given reasonably in advance of the hearing and must contain a statement of the subject matter of the proceeding with sufficient particularity to enable the party to understand what is at issue and prepare their case. A notice that states only the date and time without identifying the subject matter of the proceeding is defective under s. 6 and constitutes a breach of procedural fairness.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 6
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Statutory Powers Procedure Act | SPPA Notice Requirements — Section 6

text:
An Ontario tribunal governed by the SPPA issues a final decision dismissing a complaint. Six months later, new evidence emerges that was not available at the time of the original hearing. The complainant applies to the tribunal to reconsider its decision on the basis of the new evidence. The tribunal's enabling statute does not address reconsideration. The respondent argues the tribunal has no power to reconsider a final decision.

Under s. 21.2 of the SPPA, does an Ontario tribunal have the power to reconsider its own final decision, and if so, under what conditions?
opts: No. A tribunal loses all jurisdiction over a matter once it issues a final decision; only a superior court on judicial review can revisit the decision.

Yes. Under s. 21.2 of the SPPA, a tribunal may, if it considers it advisable and if its rules permit, reconsider a decision made by it in a proceeding; however, the power to reconsider is not unlimited and is subject to the conditions in the tribunal's rules.
Yes. A tribunal may reconsider any final decision whenever new evidence is presented, without any conditions or procedural requirements.
No. Reconsideration is only available where the enabling statute of the tribunal expressly grants the power; the SPPA does not independently authorize reconsideration.
expl: Under the Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 21.2(1), a tribunal may, if it considers it advisable and if its rules permit, reconsider a decision made by it in a proceeding. Reconsideration is not automatic or unlimited; it is available only where the tribunal's own rules permit reconsideration and the tribunal considers it advisable. The existence of new evidence may be a ground for reconsideration if the tribunal's rules address that scenario. The SPPA thus provides a statutory basis for reconsideration even where the enabling statute is silent.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 21.2
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Statutory Powers Procedure Act | Reconsideration — SPPA Section 21.2

text:
A journalist submits an access to information request under FIPPA to the Ontario Ministry of Health for records relating to a ministerial briefing on a healthcare policy matter. The Ministry responds that the records are exempt and refuses access. The journalist challenges the refusal at the Information and Privacy Commissioner.

Under FIPPA, what is the general right of access and what is the key distinction between mandatory and discretionary exemptions?
opts: FIPPA does not grant any general right of access; access to government records is provided only at the discretion of the institution.

Under FIPPA s. 10, every person has a right of access to a record in the custody or under the control of an institution, subject to limited exemptions; mandatory exemptions (such as s. 21 personal privacy) must be applied by the institution, while discretionary exemptions (such as s. 19 solicitor-client privilege) may be applied if the institution decides in its discretion to claim them.
FIPPA grants a right of access only to Ontario residents; non-residents and corporations have no right of access to government records.
Under FIPPA all exemptions are mandatory; once an exemption applies, the institution must always withhold the record.
expl: Under the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 10, every person has a right of access to a record or part of a record in the custody or under the control of an institution unless the record or part falls within one of the enumerated exemptions. FIPPA distinguishes between mandatory exemptions (where the institution shall refuse access — e.g., s. 21 personal privacy) and discretionary exemptions (where the institution may refuse access — e.g., s. 14 law enforcement, s. 19 solicitor-client privilege). For discretionary exemptions, the institution must exercise its discretion to claim the exemption; if it fails to do so, access must be granted.
caseRef: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 10, s. 14, s. 19, s. 21
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information | FIPPA — Right of Access; Mandatory vs Discretionary Exemptions

text:
A requester submits a FIPPA access request to the Ontario Provincial Police for records relating to an ongoing criminal investigation. The OPP refuses access, citing s. 14 (law enforcement exemption). The requester then submits a separate request to the Ministry of the Attorney General for legal memoranda prepared by Ministry lawyers advising the government on the same investigation. The Ministry refuses access, citing s. 19 (solicitor-client privilege).

Which of the following correctly distinguishes the s. 14 law enforcement exemption from the s. 19 solicitor-client privilege exemption under FIPPA?
opts: Both s. 14 and s. 19 are mandatory exemptions; the institution must withhold records in both cases without any exercise of discretion.

Section 14 is a discretionary exemption that the institution may claim for records that could reasonably be expected to interfere with an ongoing law enforcement investigation; s. 19 is also a discretionary exemption protecting records that are subject to solicitor-client privilege, but the institution may choose to waive the privilege and disclose.
Section 14 is a mandatory exemption; s. 19 is a discretionary exemption that the institution may choose to claim or waive.
Section 19 is a mandatory exemption; s. 14 is a discretionary exemption requiring the institution to exercise its discretion to withhold.
expl: Under the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, both s. 14 (law enforcement) and s. 19 (solicitor-client privilege) are discretionary exemptions — the institution 'may' refuse access rather than 'shall' refuse access. For s. 14, the institution may refuse access to records where disclosure could reasonably be expected to interfere with law enforcement matters. For s. 19, the institution may refuse access to records subject to solicitor-client privilege. Because both are discretionary, the institution must exercise its discretion whether to claim the exemption; the institution may choose to disclose even if the exemption technically applies.
caseRef: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 14 (law enforcement), s. 19 (solicitor-client privilege)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information | FIPPA Exemptions — Section 14 Law Enforcement and Section 19 Solicitor-Client Privilege

text:
A requester submits a FIPPA access request to the Ontario Ministry of Finance. The Ministry refuses access under s. 19 (solicitor-client privilege). The requester disagrees and wishes to challenge the refusal. The requester's lawyer advises that the dispute should first go to the Information and Privacy Commissioner before any court proceedings.

Under FIPPA, what is the role of the Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario in reviewing a disputed access refusal, and what is the appeal route after an IPC order?
opts: The IPC acts as a mediator only; it has no power to issue binding orders and any order it makes may be ignored by the institution.

Under FIPPA, a requester who has been refused access may appeal the refusal to the IPC; the IPC investigates and may mediate the dispute. If mediation fails, the IPC may conduct an inquiry and issue a binding order requiring disclosure or confirming the exemption; the order may be appealed to the Divisional Court.
The IPC's role is limited to recommending changes to government privacy practices; it cannot intervene in individual access disputes.
A requester must bring a judicial review application directly to the Divisional Court without first going to the IPC.
expl: Under the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, a requester who is refused access may appeal the refusal to the Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario. The IPC has a multi-stage process: it may attempt to resolve the dispute through mediation, and if mediation fails, may conduct a formal inquiry. Following an inquiry, the IPC has the power to issue binding orders — including orders requiring the institution to disclose records — or confirming the institution's right to withhold. Orders of the IPC may be appealed to the Divisional Court under s. 54 of FIPPA.
caseRef: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 54 (appeal to Divisional Court)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information | IPC — Orders, Mediation, and Appeal Route

text:
A national retail chain operating across Canada collects customer purchase data and shares it with a third-party marketing company without the customers' knowledge or consent. Customers complain that their personal information has been shared without authorization. They are advised that Ontario's FIPPA does not apply because the retailer is a private sector company.

Which privacy statute governs the collection and disclosure of personal information by private sector organizations carrying on commercial activities in Canada, and what is the foundational consent principle?
opts: Ontario's Municipal Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act (MFIPPA) applies to all private sector organizations operating in Ontario.
There is no federal privacy legislation governing the private sector; privacy protection for private sector organizations is entirely a matter of provincial jurisdiction.

The Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA), S.C. 2000, c. 5, applies to organizations that collect, use, or disclose personal information in the course of commercial activities; PIPEDA's foundational principle requires organizations to obtain meaningful consent from individuals before collecting, using, or disclosing their personal information.
FIPPA applies to both public institutions and private companies operating in Ontario where the company performs government-like functions.
expl: The Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, S.C. 2000, c. 5 (PIPEDA), is the federal statute governing the collection, use, and disclosure of personal information by private sector organizations in the course of commercial activities. PIPEDA applies in provinces that have not enacted substantially similar privacy legislation (Alberta, British Columbia, and Quebec have their own legislation). PIPEDA's Schedule 1 sets out ten fair information principles based on the Canadian Standards Association Model Code, with consent as the foundational principle: organizations must obtain the knowledge and consent of the individual for any collection, use, or disclosure of personal information unless an exception applies.
caseRef: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, S.C. 2000, c. 5 (PIPEDA), Schedule 1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information | PIPEDA — Application, Consent Principles, and Federal Jurisdiction

text:
A property owner's land is accidentally flooded by a Ministry of Transportation work crew during a highway maintenance project. The owner wishes to sue the Ontario government for negligence and property damage. The owner's lawyer advises that at common law the Crown could not be sued in tort, but that the legal position has changed by statute.

Under the current Ontario statutory framework, can a private party sue the Ontario Crown in tort for property damage caused by government employees?
opts: No. The Crown retains absolute immunity from all tort claims under the common law doctrine that the King can do no wrong.

Yes. Under the Proceedings Against the Crown Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. P.27, the Crown in right of Ontario is subject to liability in tort in the same manner and to the same extent as a person of full age and capacity.
Yes, but only where the Crown employee who caused the damage was a senior officer; the Crown has no vicarious liability for the acts of junior employees.
No. A private party may only sue the individual Crown employee, not the Crown itself.
expl: At common law, the Crown was immune from tort suits under the prerogative principle that the King could do no wrong. This immunity has been abrogated in Ontario by the Proceedings Against the Crown Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. P.27, which provides that the Crown is subject to liability in tort in the same manner and to the same extent as if it were a person of full age and capacity. The Crown is also vicariously liable for the torts of its officers and employees acting in the course of their employment.
caseRef: Proceedings Against the Crown Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. P.27; Crown Liability and Proceedings Act, 2019, S.O. 2019, c. 7, Sched. 17
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Crown Liability | Crown Immunity — Historical Rule and Ontario's Proceedings Against the Crown Act

text:
A plaintiff was injured by a Ministry of Transportation vehicle on June 1, 2023. She did not discover the full extent of her injuries until September 1, 2024. She consults a lawyer on October 1, 2024, and asks whether she can still sue the Ontario government. Under the Limitations Act, 2002, the basic limitation period is two years from the date the claim is discovered. The lawyer notes that historically, shorter limitation periods applied to claims against the Crown.

Under Ontario law, what limitation period applies to a tort claim against the Ontario Crown in right of Ontario?
opts: Claims against the Ontario Crown are subject to a special 90-day notice requirement and a one-year limitation period under the Proceedings Against the Crown Act.
Claims against the Ontario Crown are subject to the same two-year basic limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002, as claims against private parties, with the discovery rule applying.
There is no limitation period for claims against the Crown; the Crown can never benefit from limitation periods.
Claims against the Ontario Crown must be commenced within six months of the date the cause of action arose, with no discovery rule extension.
expl: Under the Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 2(1), a claim must be commenced within two years after the day the claim was discovered. The Limitations Act, 2002, applies to claims against the Crown, and Ontario's Crown Liability and Proceedings Act, 2019, S.O. 2019, c. 7, Sched. 17 (which amended the Proceedings Against the Crown Act), did not create a separate shorter limitation period that displaces the Limitations Act, 2002 framework. The discovery rule under the Limitations Act, 2002, applies: time runs from when the plaintiff knew or ought to have known of the claim.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 2(1); Proceedings Against the Crown Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. P.27; Crown Liability and Proceedings Act, 2019, S.O. 2019, c. 7, Sched. 17
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Crown Liability | Limitation Periods Against the Crown

text:
The Ontario government decides, as a matter of government policy, to close three rural hospitals in order to reallocate healthcare resources to urban centres. A rural patient suffers a cardiac event and dies on the way to the nearest urban hospital. The patient's estate sues the provincial government in negligence, arguing the hospital closure was a negligent decision.

Under the policy/operational distinction in Crown liability law, is the government's decision to close the rural hospitals a matter for which the Crown may be held liable in negligence?
opts: Yes. The government owes a duty of care to all persons who may be affected by its decisions, and a negligent hospital closure is actionable regardless of whether it is a policy decision.

No. Core policy decisions — those involving the weighing of competing social, political, and economic considerations by elected officials or senior government officials — are immune from negligence liability; the decision to close hospitals and reallocate health resources is a classic core policy decision.
No. Government decisions about healthcare are absolutely immune from tort claims under the Proceedings Against the Crown Act.
Yes. The policy/operational distinction was abolished by the Supreme Court of Canada and all government decisions are now subject to negligence liability.
expl: The policy/operational distinction is a key principle in Crown liability law, recognized in Cooper v Hobart, 2001 SCC 79. Core policy decisions — those reflecting the exercise of discretionary authority at the level of government policy involving weighing of competing considerations including political, economic, and social factors — attract immunity from negligence liability. The decision to close hospitals and reallocate healthcare resources is a paradigmatic core policy decision made by elected and senior officials weighing competing public interests. Operational decisions (how the policy is implemented) may attract liability. The distinction survives in Canadian law.
caseRef: Cooper v Hobart, 2001 SCC 79; Proceedings Against the Crown Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. P.27; Anns v Merton London Borough Council, [1978] AC 728
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Crown Liability | Policy vs Operational Distinction — Core Policy Decisions Immune from Negligence

text:
An Ontario government safety inspector negligently fails to observe a known defect in the electrical system of a school during a required inspection. As a result, a fire breaks out three weeks later, injuring students. The government argues the inspection regime is a policy decision immune from liability. The plaintiffs argue the inspector's on-the-ground failure during the inspection is an operational matter.

Under the Anns/Cooper two-stage test for Crown negligence liability, how does the court determine whether proximity exists and whether the policy/operational distinction bars the claim?
opts: The government is never liable for negligent inspections because inspection decisions always involve policy considerations.

Under Anns/Cooper, the court first asks whether there is a sufficiently close and direct relationship between the parties to establish proximity (stage one). If proximity is established, the court asks at stage two whether there are policy reasons to negate the duty. A negligent operational act by an inspector during a mandatory inspection may establish proximity; the careless act of the inspector during the inspection is operational, not a core policy choice, and may attract liability.
The government automatically owes a duty of care to all persons harmed by any government activity; the Anns/Cooper test has no application to Crown negligence.
The distinction between policy and operational decisions is irrelevant once a government employee causes physical harm; the Crown is always vicariously liable for physical harm caused by employees.
expl: Under Cooper v Hobart, 2001 SCC 79, the Anns/Cooper test applies to establish a duty of care in government negligence cases. Stage one asks whether there is a sufficiently close and direct relationship (proximity) between the government and the plaintiff. Stage two asks whether there are residual policy reasons to negate the duty. Crucially, the court distinguishes between the policy decision (to create an inspection regime) and the operational execution of that policy (how the inspector carries out the inspection). The policy decision may be immune, but the operational negligence of an individual inspector carrying out a mandatory inspection function may give rise to liability where proximity is established.
caseRef: Cooper v Hobart, 2001 SCC 79; Anns v Merton London Borough Council, [1978] AC 728; Proceedings Against the Crown Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. P.27
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Crown Liability | Policy vs Operational Distinction — Anns/Cooper Two-Stage Test

text:
An employee with a back injury needs a modified work schedule and ergonomic equipment. The employer provides ergonomic equipment but refuses to modify the work schedule, arguing that any schedule modification would cause 'significant inconvenience' to customers and result in a small increase in labour costs. The employer argues that this constitutes undue hardship.

Under the Human Rights Code and the Meiorin test, does 'significant inconvenience' or minor increases in labour costs constitute undue hardship sufficient to excuse the employer from accommodating the employee?
opts: Yes. Any financial cost or operational inconvenience to the employer satisfies the undue hardship defence; accommodation is only required where it is cost-free.
Yes. Customer inconvenience is always sufficient to establish undue hardship in a service-based industry.

No. Under the Human Rights Code s. 17 and the Meiorin test, undue hardship is a high threshold; relevant factors include health and safety considerations, cost (assessed in the context of the employer's overall financial capacity), and external sources of funding. Significant inconvenience to customers and minor cost increases, standing alone, do not meet the undue hardship threshold.
No. The employer must provide every accommodation requested without limitation; the concept of undue hardship has no application to employment accommodation.
expl: The duty to accommodate to the point of undue hardship is established in British Columbia (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3, and codified in s. 17 of the Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19. Undue hardship is a high standard; the factors relevant to the assessment include cost (taking into account the employer's overall financial capacity), health and safety, and availability of outside funding. Inconvenience, minor disruption, and small cost increases do not reach the threshold of undue hardship. The employer must demonstrate hardship that is truly unreasonable in the circumstances, not merely inconvenient.
caseRef: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 17; British Columbia (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Human Rights | Duty to Accommodate — To the Point of Undue Hardship

text:
A fire department has a physical fitness standard requiring all firefighters to run 2.5 km in 11 minutes. A qualified female firefighter passes all other aspects of her annual fitness evaluation but consistently fails to meet this particular running standard despite her best efforts. Statistical evidence shows women on average require more time to complete this distance than men. The department threatens to terminate her employment.

Under the unified approach to the duty to accommodate established in Meiorin, what must the employer demonstrate to justify the standard as a bona fide occupational requirement (BFOR)?
opts: The employer must prove only that the standard was adopted for safety reasons; once safety is established, no further justification is needed.

Under the Meiorin unified three-part BFOR test, the employer must demonstrate: (1) the standard was adopted for a purpose rationally connected to the performance of the job; (2) the standard was adopted in an honest and good faith belief that it is necessary for the fulfilment of that legitimate work-related purpose; and (3) the standard is reasonably necessary to accomplish that purpose — meaning the employer cannot accommodate the employee without incurring undue hardship.
The employer must prove only that the standard applies equally to all employees; equal application alone satisfies the BFOR defence.
The Meiorin test does not apply to physical fitness standards; those standards are evaluated under a separate legislative regime for safety-critical occupations.
expl: In British Columbia (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3, the Supreme Court of Canada unified the previously separate direct and adverse effect discrimination tests and established a three-part BFOR test for discriminatory standards. The employer must prove: (1) the standard was adopted for a purpose rationally connected to job performance; (2) the standard was adopted in good faith belief it was necessary; and (3) the standard is reasonably necessary — meaning individual accommodation to the point of undue hardship is not possible. A running standard that has an adverse effect on women must be justified under all three parts of this test, including showing that no accommodation short of undue hardship is possible.
caseRef: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 17; British Columbia (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Human Rights | Duty to Accommodate — Meiorin Unified Test

text:
An employer refuses to hire a qualified applicant after learning during a pre-employment medical examination that the applicant has type 1 diabetes. The employer claims the applicant could not safely perform the essential duties of the position without accommodation. The applicant has not yet been given an opportunity to demonstrate her ability to do the job with accommodation.

Under the Ontario Human Rights Code, which prohibited ground of discrimination is most directly engaged by the employer's refusal to hire, and in which social area?
opts: Race discrimination under s. 5 of the Code in the area of services.
Disability discrimination under s. 5 of the Code in the area of employment.
Sex discrimination under s. 5 of the Code in the area of employment.
Creed discrimination under s. 5 of the Code in the area of vocational associations.
expl: Under the Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 5(1), every person has a right to equal treatment with respect to employment without discrimination because of, inter alia, disability. Type 1 diabetes is a recognized disability within the meaning of the Code. The employer's refusal to hire the applicant on the basis of her diabetes — without first allowing her to demonstrate her ability to perform the job with accommodation — constitutes prima facie discrimination on the ground of disability in the area of employment under s. 5.
caseRef: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 5(1) (employment), s. 10 (definitions including disability)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Human Rights | Human Rights Code Grounds — Prohibited Grounds and Social Areas

text:
An employer's workplace policy requires all employees to work Friday evenings and Saturdays. A Jewish employee whose religious observance requires him to abstain from work from sunset Friday to nightfall Saturday asks for an exemption. The employer refuses, stating the policy applies equally to all employees and was not designed to target any religion.

Under the Human Rights Code, does a facially neutral employment policy that has an adverse effect on persons sharing a protected characteristic constitute prohibited discrimination?
opts: No. A facially neutral policy that applies equally to all employees cannot constitute discrimination; there must be an intention to discriminate based on a prohibited ground.

Yes. Under s. 11 of the Code (constructive discrimination), a requirement that is not discriminatory on its face but results in adverse treatment of a group identified by a prohibited ground — here, creed — constitutes discrimination; the employer must accommodate the employee to the point of undue hardship.
No. Religious accommodation applies only where the employer specifically targeted the employee because of his religion; neutral policies with unintended effects are not within the scope of the Code.
Yes, but the employer has no obligation to accommodate unless the employee submits a written formal request for accommodation using the employer's specific HR procedures.
expl: Under the Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 11 (constructive discrimination), a requirement, qualification, or factor that is not explicitly discriminatory but has an adverse effect on a group of persons identified by a prohibited ground — such as creed — constitutes discrimination. The intent of the employer is irrelevant; adverse effect suffices. A rigid Saturday work requirement adversely impacts employees who observe the Jewish Sabbath, constituting constructive discrimination on the ground of creed. The employer is then required to accommodate to the point of undue hardship under s. 17.
caseRef: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 11 (constructive discrimination), s. 17 (undue hardship)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Human Rights | Prima Facie Discrimination — Constructive Discrimination

text:
A complainant files an application at the Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario (HRTO) alleging sexual harassment by her manager over a two-year period. The HRTO notifies the parties and offers mediation. The employer's counsel contacts the HRTO and argues the application should be dismissed summarily because the applicant's complaint is frivolous, having no reasonable prospect of success, and was filed purely to harm the reputation of the manager.

Under s. 45.1 of the Human Rights Code, on what grounds may the HRTO dismiss an application without a full hearing?
opts: The HRTO may dismiss any application where the respondent demonstrates the applicant has filed more than one previous complaint.

Under s. 45.1 of the Code, the HRTO may dismiss an application, in whole or in part, if the Tribunal is of the opinion that the application or part is frivolous, vexatious, or made in bad faith; or where the Code does not apply; or where the application has already been appropriately dealt with.
The HRTO may dismiss an application only after a full merits hearing; there is no summary dismissal power under the Code.
The HRTO may dismiss an application at any time simply if the respondent provides an affidavit denying the allegations.
expl: Under the Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 45.1, the HRTO may dismiss an application without a hearing on the merits if the Tribunal is of the opinion that the application is frivolous, vexatious, or made in bad faith; the Code does not apply to the claim; another proceeding has appropriately dealt with the substance of the application; or where the application was filed past applicable time limits. This summary dismissal power allows the HRTO to screen applications efficiently without requiring a full hearing in cases where the application clearly lacks merit.
caseRef: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 45.1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Human Rights | HRTO Process — Complaint, Mediation, Hearing, and s. 45.1 Dismissal

text:
The Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario finds that an employee was terminated from her employment because of her pregnancy, contrary to s. 5 of the Human Rights Code. The Tribunal awards monetary compensation. The employee argues that in addition to monetary compensation for lost wages, she should also receive compensation for injury to her dignity, feelings, and self-respect. The employer argues the HRTO can only award lost wages and cannot award non-economic damages.

Under s. 45.2 of the Human Rights Code, what remedies may the HRTO award following a finding of discrimination?
opts: The HRTO may award only monetary compensation for past and future lost wages; it has no power to award non-economic damages.

Under s. 45.2 of the Code, the HRTO may award monetary compensation for: (i) lost income and any other compensation monetarily quantifiable; and (ii) injury to dignity, feelings, and self-respect (non-economic general damages). The Tribunal may also make orders requiring the respondent to do or refrain from doing something.
The HRTO may award punitive damages as its primary remedy to deter future discrimination.
The HRTO has no remedial power; it can only issue declarations of discrimination, leaving monetary relief to the courts.
expl: Under the Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 45.2, the HRTO may, on finding a contravention, order the respondent to pay monetary compensation to the applicant for lost income and other quantifiable losses, and to compensate the applicant for injury to dignity, feelings, and self-respect. Section 45.2 also empowers the HRTO to make orders that the respondent do or refrain from doing anything to implement the Code's purposes. The HRTO does not award punitive damages in the traditional sense, though large awards for injury to dignity may serve a general deterrence function.
caseRef: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 45.2
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Human Rights | HRTO Remedies — Section 45.2

text:
Following mediation at the HRTO, a complainant and respondent reach a written settlement agreement. The respondent agrees to pay the complainant $25,000 and to implement a workplace anti-harassment training program within 90 days. The HRTO approves the settlement. Four months later, the respondent has paid the money but has not implemented the training program. The complainant asks the HRTO to enforce the settlement.

Under the Human Rights Code, how may a party enforce a settlement reached at the HRTO that the other party has partially breached?
opts: The complainant must commence a new application at the HRTO re-alleging the original discrimination; the settlement agreement cannot itself be enforced at the HRTO.
The complainant must bring an action in the Superior Court of Justice to enforce the settlement agreement as a contract.

Under s. 45 of the Code, a settlement agreement may be filed with the HRTO, which then becomes an order of the Tribunal; an order of the HRTO is enforceable in the same manner as an order of the Superior Court of Justice, allowing enforcement through the court's contempt or enforcement mechanisms.
A settlement reached at the HRTO is not legally binding; either party may unilaterally resile from the agreement at any time.
expl: Under the Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 45, the Tribunal may make an order giving effect to a settlement. Once filed and made into a Tribunal order, the order has the same force and effect as a Tribunal order made after a hearing and may be filed in the Superior Court of Justice for enforcement purposes. This mechanism allows the aggrieved party to enforce a breached settlement through the court's enforcement machinery (including writs of execution and contempt proceedings) without having to re-litigate the underlying human rights complaint.
caseRef: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 45 (settlement), s. 45.2 (remedies)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Human Rights | Code Section 46 — Settlement Agreement Enforcement

text:
The British Columbia government issues a permit to a logging company to harvest timber in an area where the Haida Nation has a pending but unproven claim to Aboriginal title. The Haida Nation has not yet established its title in court. The Haida seek a declaration that the Crown failed to consult them before issuing the logging permit.

Under Haida Nation v British Columbia, what triggers the Crown's duty to consult Indigenous peoples, and does the duty arise even before Aboriginal rights or title have been proven?
opts: The duty to consult arises only after Aboriginal title has been legally established by a court; until then, the Crown has no obligation to consult.

The duty to consult arises where: (1) the Crown has actual or constructive knowledge of a potential Aboriginal claim or right; and (2) the Crown contemplates conduct that might adversely affect the right or claim. The duty arises before rights are proven; the strength of the claim affects the depth of the duty.
The duty to consult is owed only to First Nations that have signed treaties; non-treaty groups like the Haida have no such right.
The duty to consult arises only when the Crown proposes legislation that would directly affect Aboriginal peoples, not when issuing administrative permits.
expl: In Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73, the Supreme Court of Canada established that the Crown's duty to consult and accommodate is triggered when two conditions are met: (1) the Crown has actual or constructive knowledge of the potential existence of an Aboriginal right or title claim; and (2) the Crown contemplates conduct — including administrative decisions like permit issuance — that might adversely affect that right or claim. Critically, the duty arises before rights are proven; the justification is the honour of the Crown and the need to protect Indigenous interests during the period of uncertainty. The scope of the duty (deep or shallow) is calibrated to the strength of the claim.
caseRef: Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73; Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Indigenous Law and Crown Obligations | Duty to Consult — Trigger and Spectrum

text:
An Ontario First Nation has a strong and well-documented claim to Aboriginal title over a large land area. The provincial government proposes to approve a major mineral development project in the heart of that claimed territory. The project would cause significant and potentially irreversible disruption to the land. The government consults with the First Nation but provides only two weeks to respond and does not modify the project to address concerns raised.

Under the Haida Nation spectrum, how deep is the duty to consult and accommodate in circumstances involving a strong claim and potentially significant adverse effects?
opts: The depth of the duty is always the same regardless of the strength of the claim or the severity of the potential impact; all consultations require a two-week response period.

Where the claim is strong and the potential adverse effects are significant, the duty to consult sits at the deeper end of the spectrum, requiring meaningful engagement — including a reasonable opportunity to understand and respond to the proposal — and may require accommodation such as altering or delaying the project to address concerns. A two-week consultation period for a major irreversible project may fall short.
Where Aboriginal title has not been established, the Crown has only a minimal duty to inform the First Nation of the proposed project; no accommodation is required.
The duty to accommodate means the Crown must obtain the First Nation's veto over any project that affects claimed territory.
expl: In Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73, the Supreme Court explained that the duty to consult and accommodate exists on a spectrum. At the lower end (weak claim, minor impact), the Crown may satisfy its duty by providing notice and basic information. At the deeper end (strong claim, significant impact), the duty requires meaningful engagement — including a genuine opportunity to understand and respond to the Crown's proposal — and accommodation: modifying the Crown's proposed conduct to address the Aboriginal group's concerns as far as possible without undue hardship. A major, irreversible project in the heart of a strongly supported claimed title area requires deep consultation and likely some form of accommodation.
caseRef: Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73; Taku River Tlingit First Nation v British Columbia, 2004 SCC 74; Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Indigenous Law and Crown Obligations | Depth of the Duty to Consult — Spectrum and Accommodation

text:
The Musqueam Nation has an established Aboriginal right to fish for food under s. 35 of the Constitution Act, 1982. The federal government enacts a regulation limiting the Musqueam's net length during certain spawning periods to protect the fish stock. The Musqueam challenge the regulation as an unjustified infringement of their s. 35 right.

Under R. v Sparrow, what two-part test must the Crown satisfy to justify a regulation that infringes a proven Aboriginal right under s. 35?
opts: The Crown must prove: (1) the regulation pursues a pressing and substantial objective; and (2) the regulation is proportionate — rationally connected, minimally impairing, and with proportionate effects — under s. 1 of the Charter.

The Crown must prove: (1) the infringement is justified by a compelling and substantial legislative objective (such as conservation); and (2) the infringement is consistent with the fiduciary duty the Crown owes to Aboriginal peoples, which includes giving Aboriginal rights priority after conservation needs are met.
Once the Aboriginal right is proven, the Crown cannot justify any infringement; all regulations affecting s. 35 rights are unconstitutional.
The Crown must only prove that the regulation applies equally to all Canadians; equal application is a complete justification for any infringement of s. 35 rights.
expl: In R. v Sparrow, [1990] 1 SCR 1075, the Supreme Court of Canada established the two-part justification framework for s. 35 infringements. First, the Crown must identify a compelling and substantial legislative objective — conservation and management of a natural resource is a valid objective. Second, the Crown's action must be consistent with its fiduciary duty to Aboriginal peoples, which requires, among other things, giving priority to Aboriginal rights after conservation needs are met, minimizing the infringement, and consulting the affected group. This Sparrow justification test is distinct from the Charter s. 1 analysis.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35; R v Sparrow, [1990] 1 SCR 1075
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Indigenous Law and Crown Obligations | Section 35 — Sparrow Justification Test for Infringement of Aboriginal Rights

text:
A law student argues to her colleague that the legal concept of the 'honour of the Crown' has no practical legal effect — it is merely a rhetorical flourish that courts use to signal good intentions toward Indigenous peoples without imposing any enforceable obligations.

Is the 'honour of the Crown' principle merely rhetorical, or does it impose legally enforceable obligations in the Crown-Indigenous relationship?
opts: The honour of the Crown is merely a political and moral concept; it creates no legally enforceable obligations and cannot ground court orders.

The honour of the Crown is a foundational constitutional principle that has been recognized by the Supreme Court of Canada as imposing enforceable obligations on the Crown; it underpins the duty to consult and accommodate, the fiduciary duty, and the requirement that Crown conduct must not be interpreted to allow the Crown to take unconscionable advantage of its Aboriginal partners.
The honour of the Crown is a concept that applies only after Aboriginal rights have been fully negotiated through treaty; it has no application during the pre-treaty or litigation phase.
The honour of the Crown was abolished by the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP), which replaced it with the concept of free, prior, and informed consent.
expl: The Supreme Court of Canada has consistently held that the honour of the Crown is a foundational constitutional principle with real legal effect. In Haida Nation v British Columbia, 2004 SCC 73, the Court held that the honour of the Crown underpins the duty to consult and accommodate. In R. v Sparrow, [1990] 1 SCR 1075, it informed the fiduciary duty and justification analysis. The principle requires the Crown to act with integrity in all dealings with Indigenous peoples and prohibits the Crown from acting in ways that would take unconscionable advantage of Aboriginal peoples. UNDRIP (adopted by Canada in 2016 and incorporated into Canadian law through legislation) does not replace the honour of the Crown but complements it by affirming the principle of free, prior, and informed consent as a further standard for Crown-Indigenous engagement.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35; Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73; R v Sparrow, [1990] 1 SCR 1075
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Indigenous Law and Crown Obligations | Reconciliation Principle — Honour of the Crown and UNDRIP
